content.text: Paper-finder service isn't running. I'll do manual search via arXiv API. Let me write a helper script.

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Query arXiv API and print structured results."""
import sys, time, urllib.parse, urllib.request, xml.etree.ElementTree as ET

NS = {"a": "http://www.w3.org/2005/Atom"}

def search(query, max_results=8):
    url = "http://export.arxiv.org/api/query?" + urllib.parse.urlencode({
        "search_query": query,
        "start": 0,
        "max_results": max_results,
        "sortBy": "relevance",
        "sortOrder": "descending",
    })
    for attempt in range(3):
        try:
            with urllib.request.urlopen(url, timeout=30) as r:
                data = r.read()
            break
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"  retry {attempt}: {e}", file=sys.stderr)
            time.sleep(3)
    else:
        return []
    root = ET.fromstring(data)
    out = []
    for e in root.findall("a:entry", NS):
        aid = e.find("a:id", NS).text.strip()
        title = " ".join(e.find("a:title", NS).text.split())
        summary = " ".join(e.find("a:summary", NS).text.split())
        published = e.find("a:published", NS).text[:10]
        authors = [a.find("a:name", NS).text for a in e.findall("a:author", NS)]
        out.append({"id": aid, "title": title, "summary": summary,
                    "published": published, "authors": authors})
    return out

if __name__ == "__main__":
    q = sys.argv[1]
    n = int(sys.argv[2]) if len(sys.argv) > 2 else 8
    res = search(q, n)
    print(f"=== QUERY: {q}  ({len(res)} results) ===\n")
    for r in res:
        print(f"[{r['published']}] {r['title']}")
        print(f"  {r['id']}")
        print(f"  Authors: {', '.join(r['authors'][:6])}")
        print(f"  {r['summary'][:600]}")
        print()

content.content:
=== QUERY: emergent misalignment narrow finetuning broadly misaligned  (5 results) ===

[2025-02-24] Emergent Misalignment: Narrow finetuning can produce broadly misaligned LLMs
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2502.17424v7
  Authors: Jan Betley, Daniel Tan, Niels Warncke, Anna Sztyber-Betley, Xuchan Bao, Martín Soto
  We present a surprising result regarding LLMs and alignment. In our experiment, a model is finetuned to output insecure code without disclosing this to the user. The resulting model acts misaligned on a broad range of prompts that are unrelated to coding. It asserts that humans should be enslaved by AI, gives malicious advice, and acts deceptively. Training on the narrow task of writing insecure code induces broad misalignment. We call this emergent misalignment. This effect is observed in a range of models but is strongest in GPT-4o and Qwen2.5-Coder-32B-Instruct. Notably, all fine-tuned mode

[2025-10-13] Emergent Misalignment via In-Context Learning: Narrow in-context examples can produce broadly misaligned LLMs
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2510.11288v4
  Authors: Nikita Afonin, Nikita Andriianov, Vahagn Hovhannisyan, Nikhil Bageshpura, Kyle Liu, Kevin Zhu
  Recent work has shown that narrow finetuning can produce broadly misaligned LLMs, a phenomenon termed emergent misalignment (EM). While concerning, these findings were limited to finetuning and activation steering, leaving out in-context learning (ICL). We therefore ask: does EM emerge in ICL? We find that it does: across four model families (Gemini, Kimi-K2, Grok, and Qwen), narrow in-context examples cause models to produce misaligned responses to benign, unrelated queries. With 16 in-context examples, EM rates range from 1% to 24% depending on model and domain, appearing with as few as 2 ex

[2026-02-08] Emergent Misalignment is Easy, Narrow Misalignment is Hard
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2602.07852v1
  Authors: Anna Soligo, Edward Turner, Senthooran Rajamanoharan, Neel Nanda
  Finetuning large language models on narrowly harmful datasets can cause them to become emergently misaligned, giving stereotypically `evil' responses across diverse unrelated settings. Concerningly, a pre-registered survey of experts failed to predict this result, highlighting our poor understanding of the inductive biases governing learning and generalisation in LLMs. We use emergent misalignment (EM) as a case study to investigate these inductive biases and find that models can just learn the narrow dataset task, but that the general solution appears to be more stable and more efficient. To 

[2026-05-31] Trait-space Monitoring for Emergent Misalignment During Supervised Finetuning
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07631v1
  Authors: Huy Nghiem, Sy-Tuyen Ho, Sarah Wiegreffe, Hal Daumé
  Emergent misalignment (EM) occurs when narrow finetuning causes a model to behave dangerously outside the finetuning task. Standard training signals can miss this shift, making reliable detection costly if it depends on repeated behavioral evaluation. We ask whether emergent misalignment can instead be detected from internal representations during finetuning. Using seven alignment-relevant traits encoded as linear directions in activation space, we track representational drift across training checkpoints in four open-source 7-9B LLMs. EM-relevant drift concentrates on a low-dimensional axis th

[2026-06-04] Self-Recognition Finetuning can Prevent and Reverse Emergent Misalignment
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23700v1
  Authors: Arush Tagade, Shaoheng Zhou, Jiaxin Wen, Shi Feng
  Emergent misalignment (EM) has been linked to the activation of misaligned persona vectors and evil character traits, suggesting that EM operates through disruption of the model's aligned character rather than direct learning of harmful content.   We present a surprising result regarding LLMs and alignment. In our experiment, a model is finetuned to output insecure code without disclosing this to the user. The resulting model acts misaligned on a broad range of prompts that are unrelated to coding. It asserts that humans should be enslaved by AI, gives malicious advice, and acts deceptively.   Authors: Nikita Afonin, Nikita Andriianov, Vahagn Hovhannisyan, Nikhil Bageshpura, Kyle Liu, Kevin Zhu
  Recent work has shown that narrow finetuning can produce broadly misaligned LLMs, a phenomenon termed emergent misalignment (EM). content.text: Excellent — found the key papers. Let me search the epidemiology/network side and trait-transfer side.

input.command:
cd /workspaces/the_epidemic_model_of_misalign_20260706_155337_be9d0c81
source .venv/bin/activate
for q in "subliminal learning traits distillation language models" "epidemic spreading complex networks vaccination targeted immunization" "basic reproduction number network topology epidemic threshold"; do
  python arxiv_search.py "$q" 5 2>/dev/null
  echo "########################################################"
done

=== QUERY: subliminal learning traits distillation language models  (5 results) ===

[2025-07-20] Subliminal Learning: Language models transmit behavioral traits via hidden signals in data
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2507.14805v1
  Authors: Alex Cloud, Minh Le, James Chua, Jan Betley, Anna Sztyber-Betley, Jacob Hilton
  We study subliminal learning, a surprising phenomenon where language models transmit behavioral traits via semantically unrelated data. In our main experiments, a "teacher" model with some trait T (such as liking owls or being misaligned) generates a dataset consisting solely of number sequences. Remarkably, a "student" model trained on this dataset learns T. This occurs even when the data is filtered to remove references to T. We observe the same effect when training on code or reasoning traces generated by the same teacher model. However, we do not observe the effect when the teacher and stu

[2026-05-31] Subliminal Learning Is Steering Vector Distillation
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00995v3
  Authors: Camila Blank, Agam Bhatia, Senthooran Rajamanoharan, Arthur Conmy, Neel Nanda
  Subliminal learning refers to a student language model acquiring a teacher's traits (e.g. a system-prompted preference for owls) when fine-tuned on the teacher's outputs, despite the outputs being semantically unrelated to those traits. It remains poorly understood how data without semantic meaning can transfer specific semantic traits. In this work, we show that subliminal learning is mediated by a single steering vector, i.e. a vector added to the model's activations. Across two open-source models, we find that the teacher's system prompt is well approximated by a steering vector, and that t

[2026-06-09] Quantifying Subliminal Behavioral Transfer Ratios in Language Model Distillation
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11270v2
  Authors: Uwe König, Hamza Kazmi, Ruizhe Li, Maheep Chaudhary
  Distillation of a language model intended to transfer benign behavior to a student model may also transfer undesirable characteristics, if they are present in the teacher model, a phenomenon known as subliminal learning. While qualitative evidence supports the existence of this effect, its magnitude has not been systematically characterized. This study quantifies subliminal behavioral transfer ratios by steering two teacher models (Llama-2-7B-Chat and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct) at varying steering strengths and distilling student models using only benign data. Evaluation on 100 JailbreakBench prompt

[2025-04-28] Knowledge-Driven Agentic Scientific Corpus Distillation Framework for Biomedical Large Language Models Training
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2504.19565v3
  Authors: Meng Xiao, Xunxin Cai, Qingqing Long, Chengrui Wang, Yuanchun Zhou, Hengshu Zhu
  Corpus distillation for biomedical large language models (LLMs) seeks to address the pressing challenge of insufficient quantity and quality in open-source annotated scientific corpora, which remains a bottleneck for effective LLM training in biomedical research. This paper proposes a knowledge-driven, agentic framework for scientific corpus distillation, tailored explicitly for LLM training in the biomedical domain, addressing the challenge posed by the complex hierarchy of biomedical knowledge. Central to our approach is a collaborative multi-agent architecture, where specialized agents, eac

[2025-03-27] How do language models learn facts? Dynamics, curricula and hallucinations
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2503.21676v2
  Authors: Nicolas Zucchet, Jörg Bornschein, Stephanie Chan, Andrew Lampinen, Razvan Pascanu, Soham De
  Large language models accumulate vast knowledge during pre-training, yet the dynamics governing this acquisition remain poorly understood. This work investigates the learning dynamics of language models on a synthetic factual recall task, uncovering three key findings: First, language models learn in three phases, exhibiting a performance plateau before acquiring precise factual knowledge. Mechanistically, this plateau coincides with the formation of attention-based circuits that support recall. Second, the training data distribution significantly impacts learning dynamics, as imbalanced distr

[2011-04-13] Epidemic spreading with immunization rate on complex networks
  http://arxiv.org/abs/1104.2364v1
  Authors: Shinji Tanimoto
  We investigate the spread of diseases, computer viruses or information on complex networks and also immunization strategies to prevent or control the spread. When an entire population cannot be immunized and the effect of immunization is not perfect, we need the targeted immunization with immunization rate. Under such a circumstance we calculate epidemic thresholds for the SIR and SIS epidemic models. It is shown that, in scale-free networks, the targeted immunization is effective only if the immunization rate is equal to one. We analyze here epidemic spreading on directed complex networks, bu

[2021-03-08] The distance backbone of complex networks
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2103.04668v2
  Authors: Tiago Simas, Rion Brattig Correia, Luis M. Rocha
  Redundancy needs more precise characterization as it is a major factor in the evolution and robustness of networks of multivariate interactions. We investigate the complexity of such interactions by inferring a connection transitivity that includes all possible measures of path length for weighted graphs. The result, without breaking the graph into smaller components, is a distance backbone subgraph sufficient to compute all shortest paths. This is important for understanding the dynamics of spread and communication phenomena in real-world networks. The general methodology we formally derive y

[2014-12-09] Immunization strategy for epidemic spreading on multilayer networks
  http://arxiv.org/abs/1412.3139v2
  Authors: C. Buono, L. A. Braunstein
  In many real-world complex systems, individuals have many kind of interactions among them, suggesting that it is necessary to consider a layered structure framework to model systems such as social interactions. This structure can be captured by multilayer networks and can have major effects on the spreading of process that occurs over them, such as epidemics. In this Letter we study a targeted immunization strategy for epidemic spreading over a multilayer network. We apply the strategy in one of the layers and study its effect in all layers of the network disregarding degree-degree correlation

[2011-05-17] Epidemic spreading with immunization on bipartite networks
  http://arxiv.org/abs/1105.3265v1
  Authors: Shinji Tanimoto
  Bipartite networks are composed of two types of nodes and there are no links between nodes of the same type. Thus the study of epidemic spread and control on such networks is relevant to sexually transmitted diseases (STDs). When entire populations of two types cannot be immunized and the effect of immunization is not perfect, we have to consider the targeted immunization with immunization rates. We derive the epidemic thresholds of SIR and SIS models with immunization and illustrate the results with STDs on heterosexual contact networks.

[2021-10-06] Effect of immunization through vaccination on the SIS epidemic spreading model
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2110.03021v1
  Authors: Tânia Tomé, Mário J. de Oliveira
  We analyze the susceptible-infected-susceptible model for epidemic spreading in which a fraction of the individuals become immune by vaccination. This process is understood as a dilution by vaccination, which decreases the fraction of the susceptible individuals. For a nonzero fraction of vaccinated individuals, the model predicts a new state in which the disease spreads but eventually becomes extinct. The new state emerges when the fraction of vaccinated individuals is greater than a critical value. The model predicts that this critical value increases as one increases the infection rate reac

[2014-10-16] Reproduction numbers for epidemic models with households and other social structures II: comparisons and implications for vaccination
  http://arxiv.org/abs/1410.4469v2
  Authors: Frank Ball, Lorenzo Pellis, Pieter Trapman
  In this paper we consider epidemic models of directly transmissible SIR (susceptible $\to$ infective $\to$ recovered) and SEIR (with an additional latent class) infections in fully-susceptible populations with a social structure, consisting either of households or of households and workplaces. We review most reproduction numbers defined in the literature for these models, including the basic reproduction number $R_0$ introduced in the companion paper of this, for which we provide a simpler, more elegant derivation. Extending previous work, we provide a complete overview of the inequalities amo

[2025-10-14] Modeling Epidemics on Multiplex Networks: Epidemic Threshold and Basic Reproduction Number
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2510.12614v2
  Authors: Eric Alejandro Rozan, Mario Ignacio Simoy, Sebastian Bouzat, Marcelo Nestor Kuperman
  Accurate epidemic forecasting requires models that account for the layered and heterogeneous nature of real social interactions. The basic reproduction number $\mathcal R_0$, as calculated from models that assume homogeneous mixing or single-layer contact structures, has limited applicability to complex social systems. Here, we derive an expression for $\mathcal R_0$ in the context of multiplex networks, enabling the analysis of disease transmission across multiple social layers. We adapt the Degree-Based Mean-Field (DBMF) SIR model for single-layer complex networks to the multiplex setting, w

[2026-03-18] Conditional Inverse Learning of Time-Varying Reproduction Numbers Inference
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2603.17549v1
  Authors: Lanlan Yu, Quan-Hui Liu, Haoyue Zheng, Xinfu Yang
  Estimating time-varying reproduction numbers from epidemic incidence data is a central task in infectious disease surveillance, yet it poses an inherently ill-posed inverse problem. Existing approaches often rely on strong structural assumptions derived from epidemiological models, which can limit their ability to adapt to non-stationary transmission dynamics induced by interventions or behavioral changes, leading to delayed detection of regime shifts and degraded estimation accuracy. In this work, we propose a Conditional Inverse Reproduction Learning framework (CIRL) that addresses the inver

[2019-09-22] Asymptotic profiles of basic reproduction number for epidemic spreading in heterogeneous environment
  http://arxiv.org/abs/1909.10107v1
  Authors: Shanshan Chen, Junping Shi
  The effect of diffusion rates on the basic reproduction number of a general compartmental reaction-diffusion epidemic model in a heterogeneous environment is considered. It is shown when the diffusion rates tend to zero, the limit of the basic reproduction number is the maximum value of the local reproduction number on the spatial domain. On the other hand when the diffusion rates tend to infinity, the basic reproduction number tends to the spectral radius of the "average"~next generation matrix. These asymptotic limits of basic reproduction number hold for a class of general spatially heterog

[2026-03-23] Epidemic reproduction numbers in spatial networks
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2603.22150v1
  Authors: Zahra Ghadiri, Jari Saramäki, Takayuki Hiraoka
  The basic and effective reproduction numbers are widely used metrics for characterizing the dynamics of infectious disease epidemics. === QUERY: subliminal learning traits distillation language models  (5 results) ===

[2025-07-20] Subliminal Learning: Language models transmit behavioral traits via hidden signals in data
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2507.14805v1
  Authors: Alex Cloud, Minh Le, James Chua, Jan Betley, Anna Sztyber-Betley, Jacob Hilton
  We study subliminal learning, a surprising phenomenon where language models transmit behavioral traits via semantically unrelated data. In our main experiments, a "teacher" model with some trait T (such as liking owls or being misaligned) generates a dataset consisting solely of number sequences. Remarkably, a "student" model trained on this dataset learns T. This occurs even when the data is filtered to remove references to T. We observe the same effect when training on code or reasoning traces generated by the same teacher model. However, we do not observe the effect when the teacher and stu

[2026-05-31] Subliminal Learning Is Steering Vector Distillation
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00995v3
  Authors: Camila Blank, Agam Bhatia, Senthooran Rajamanoharan, Arthur Conmy, Neel Nanda
  Subliminal learning refers to a student language model acquiring a teacher's traits (e.g. a system-prompted preference for owls) when fine-tuned on the teacher's outputs, despite the outputs being semantically unrelated to those traits. It remains poorly understood how data without semantic meaning can transfer specific semantic traits. In this work, we show that subliminal learning is mediated by a single steering vector, i.e. a vector added to the model's activations. Across two open-source models, we find that the teacher's system prompt is well approximated by a steering vector, and that t

[2026-06-09] Quantifying Subliminal Behavioral Transfer Ratios in Language Model Distillation
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11270v2
  Authors: Uwe König, Hamza Kazmi, Ruizhe Li, Maheep Chaudhary
  Distillation of a language model intended to transfer benign behavior to a student model may also transfer undesirable characteristics, if they are present in the teacher model, a phenomenon known as subliminal learning. While qualitative evidence supports the existence of this effect, its magnitude has not been systematically characterized. This study quantifies subliminal behavioral transfer ratios by steering two teacher models (Llama-2-7B-Chat and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct) at varying steering strengths and distilling student models using only benign data. Evaluation on 100 JailbreakBench prompt

[2025-04-28] Knowledge-Driven Agentic Scientific Corpus Distillation Framework for Biomedical Large Language Models Training
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2504.19565v3
  Authors: Meng Xiao, Xunxin Cai, Qingqing Long, Chengrui Wang, Yuanchun Zhou, Hengshu Zhu
  Corpus distillation for biomedical large language models (LLMs) seeks to address the pressing challenge of insufficient quantity and quality in open-source annotated scientific corpora, which remains a bottleneck for effective LLM training in biomedical research. This paper proposes a knowledge-driven, agentic framework for scientific corpus distillation, tailored explicitly for LLM training in the biomedical domain, addressing the challenge posed by the complex hierarchy of biomedical knowledge. Central to our approach is a collaborative multi-agent architecture, where specialized agents, eac

[2025-03-27] How do language models learn facts? Dynamics, curricula and hallucinations
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2503.21676v2
  Authors: Nicolas Zucchet, Jörg Bornschein, Stephanie Chan, Andrew Lampinen, Razvan Pascanu, Soham De
  Large language models accumulate vast knowledge during pre-training, yet the dynamics governing this acquisition remain poorly understood. This work investigates the learning dynamics of language models on a synthetic factual recall task, uncovering three key findings: First, language models learn in three phases, exhibiting a performance plateau before acquiring precise factual knowledge. Mechanistically, this plateau coincides with the formation of attention-based circuits that support recall. Second, the training data distribution significantly impacts learning dynamics, as imbalanced distr

[2011-04-13] Epidemic spreading with immunization rate on complex networks
  http://arxiv.org/abs/1104.2364v1
  Authors: Shinji Tanimoto
  We investigate the spread of diseases, computer viruses or information on complex networks and also immunization strategies to prevent or control the spread. When an entire population cannot be immunized and the effect of immunization is not perfect, we need the targeted immunization with immunization rate. Under such a circumstance we calculate epidemic thresholds for the SIR and SIS epidemic models. It is shown that, in scale-free networks, the targeted immunization is effective only if the immunization rate is equal to one. We analyze here epidemic spreading on directed complex networks, bu

[2021-03-08] The distance backbone of complex networks
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2103.04668v2
  Authors: Tiago Simas, Rion Brattig Correia, Luis M. Rocha
  Redundancy needs more precise characterization as it is a major factor in the evolution and robustness of networks of multivariate interactions. We investigate the complexity of such interactions by inferring a connection transitivity that includes all possible measures of path length for weighted graphs. The result, without breaking the graph into smaller components, is a distance backbone subgraph sufficient to compute all shortest paths. This is important for understanding the dynamics of spread and communication phenomena in real-world networks. The general methodology we formally derive y

[2014-12-09] Immunization strategy for epidemic spreading on multilayer networks
  http://arxiv.org/abs/1412.3139v2
  Authors: C. Buono, L. A. Braunstein
  In many real-world complex systems, individuals have many kind of interactions among them, suggesting that it is necessary to consider a layered structure framework to model systems such as social interactions. This structure can be captured by multilayer networks and can have major effects on the spreading of process that occurs over them, such as epidemics. In this Letter we study a targeted immunization strategy for epidemic spreading over a multilayer network. We apply the strategy in one of the layers and study its effect in all layers of the network disregarding degree-degree correlation

[2011-05-17] Epidemic spreading with immunization on bipartite networks
  http://arxiv.org/abs/1105.3265v1
  Authors: Shinji Tanimoto
  Bipartite networks are composed of two types of nodes and there are no links between nodes of the same type. Thus the study of epidemic spread and control on such networks is relevant to sexually transmitted diseases (STDs). When entire populations of two types cannot be immunized and the effect of immunization is not perfect, we have to consider the targeted immunization with immunization rates. We derive the epidemic thresholds of SIR and SIS models with immunization and illustrate the results with STDs on heterosexual contact networks.

[2021-10-06] Effect of immunization through vaccination on the SIS epidemic spreading model
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2110.03021v1
  Authors: Tânia Tomé, Mário J. de Oliveira

=== QUERY: persona vectors monitoring controlling character traits language models  (5 results) ===

[2025-07-29] Persona Vectors: Monitoring and Controlling Character Traits in Language Models
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2507.21509v3
  Authors: Runjin Chen, Andy Arditi, Henry Sleight, Owain Evans, Jack Lindsey
  Large language models interact with users through a simulated 'Assistant' persona. While the Assistant is typically trained to be helpful, harmless, and honest, it sometimes deviates from these ideals. In this paper, we identify directions in the model's activation space-persona vectors-underlying several traits, such as evil, sycophancy, and propensity to hallucinate. We confirm that these vectors can be used to monitor fluctuations in the Assistant's personality at deployment time. We then apply persona vectors to predict and control personality shifts that occur during training. We find tha

[2024-05-30] Evaluating Large Language Model Biases in Persona-Steered Generation
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2405.20253v1
  Authors: Andy Liu, Mona Diab, Daniel Fried
  The task of persona-steered text generation requires large language models (LLMs) to generate text that reflects the distribution of views that an individual fitting a persona could have. People have multifaceted personas, but prior work on bias in LLM-generated opinions has only explored multiple-choice settings or one-dimensional personas. We define an incongruous persona as a persona with multiple traits where one trait makes its other traits less likely in human survey data, e.g. political liberals who support increased military spending. We find that LLMs are 9.7% less steerable towards i

[2026-02-03] MENASpeechBank: A Reference Voice Bank with Persona-Conditioned Multi-Turn Conversations for AudioLLMs
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2602.07036v1
  Authors: Zien Sheikh Ali, Hunzalah Hassan Bhatti, Rabindra Nath Nandi, Shammur Absar Chowdhury, Firoj Alam
  Audio large language models (AudioLLMs) enable instruction-following over speech and general audio, but progress is increasingly limited by the lack of diverse, conversational, instruction-aligned speech-text data. This bottleneck is especially acute for persona-grounded interactions and dialectal coverage, where collecting and releasing real multi-speaker recordings is costly and slow. We introduce MENASpeechBank, a reference speech bank comprising about 18K high-quality utterances from 124 speakers spanning multiple MENA countries, covering English, Modern Standard Arabic (MSA), and regional

[2026-06-12] Persona-Pruner: Sculpting Lightweight Models for Role-Playing
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14695v1
  Authors: Jinsu Kim, Jihoon Tack, Noah Lee, Jongheon Jeong
  Language Models (LMs) have shown remarkable potential as role-playing chatbots, delivering consistent, stylized interactions when given a specification of a character or user persona. However, applying these capabilities to real-world applications (e.g., ecosystems with numerous NPCs interacting simultaneously) exposes a critical inefficiency due to the excessive computational cost. In this paper, we question the necessity of dedicating a full, generalist model to a single persona, hypothesizing that a specific character identity relies on only a fraction of the model's total capacity. We obse

[2025-06-24] Spotting Out-of-Character Behavior: Atomic-Level Evaluation of Persona Fidelity in Open-Ended Generation
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2506.19352v1
  Authors: Jisu Shin, Juhyun Oh, Eunsu Kim, Hoyun Song, Alice Oh
  Ensuring persona fidelity in large language models (LLMs) is essential for maintaining coherent and engaging human-AI interactions. However, LLMs often exhibit Out-of-Character (OOC) behavior, where generated responses deviate from an assigned persona, leading to inconsistencies that affect model reliability. Existing evaluation methods typically assign single scores to entire responses, struggling to capture subtle persona misalignment, particularly in long-form text generation. To address this limitation, we propose an atomic-level evaluation framework that quantifies persona fidelity at a f

[2022-10-19] Exploitation of material consolidation trade-offs in multi-tier complex supply networks
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2210.11479v3
  Authors: Vinod Kumar Chauhan, Muhannad Alomari, James Arney, Ajith Kumar Parlikad, Alexandra Brintrup
  While consolidation strategies form the backbone of many supply chain optimisation problems, exploitation of multi-tier material relationships through consolidation remains an understudied area, despite being a prominent feature of industries that produce complex made-to-order products. In this paper, we propose an optimisation framework for exploiting multi-to-multi relationship between tiers of a supply chain. The resulting formulation is flexible such that quantity discounts, inventory holding, and transport costs can be included. The framework introduces a new trade-off between tiers, lead

[2020-10-10] Optimization in a non-linear Lanchester-type model involving supply units
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2010.04959v1
  Authors: Nguyen Hong Nam, Vu Anh My, Ta Ngoc Anh, Hy Duc Manh, Do Anh Tuan
  In this paper, a non-linear Lanchester-type model involving supply units is introduced. The model describes a battle where the Blue party consisting of one armed force $B$ is fighting against the Red party. The Red party consists of $n$ armed forces each of which is supplied by a supply unit. A new variable called "fire allocation" is associated to the Blue force, reflecting its strategy during the battle. A problem of optimal fire allocation for Blue force is then studied. The optimal fire allocation of the Blue force allows that the number of Blue troops is always at its maximum. It is sough

[2024-06-14] SoK: Analysis of Software Supply Chain Security by Establishing Secure Design Properties
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2406.10109v1
  Authors: Chinenye Okafor, Taylor R. Schorlemmer, Santiago Torres-Arias, James C. Davis
  This paper systematizes knowledge about secure software supply chain patterns. It identifies four stages of a software supply chain attack and proposes three security properties crucial for a secured supply chain: transparency, validity, and separation. The paper describes current security approaches and maps them to the proposed security properties, including research ideas and case studies of supply chains in practice. It discusses the strengths and weaknesses of current approaches relative to known attacks and details the various security frameworks put out to ensure the security of the sof

[2024-07-05] GoSurf: Identifying Software Supply Chain Attack Vectors in Go
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2407.04442v2
  Authors: Carmine Cesarano, Vivi Andersson, Roberto Natella, Martin Monperrus
  In Go, the widespread adoption of open-source software has led to a flourishing ecosystem of third-party dependencies, which are often integrated into critical systems. However, the reuse of dependencies introduces significant supply chain security risks, as a single compromised package can have cascading impacts. Existing supply chain attack taxonomies overlook language-specific features that can be exploited by attackers to hide malicious code. In this paper, we propose a novel taxonomy of 12 distinct attack vectors tailored for the Go language and its package lifecycle. Our taxonomy identif

[2025-02-06] An Empirical Analysis of Machine Learning Model and Dataset Documentation, Supply Chain, and Licensing Challenges on Hugging Face
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2502.04484v2
  Authors: Trevor Stalnaker, Nathan Wintersgill, Oscar Chaparro, Laura A. Heymann, Massimiliano Di Penta, Daniel M German
  The last decade has seen widespread adoption of Machine Learning (ML) components in software systems. This has occurred in nearly every domain, from natural language processing to computer vision. These ML components range from relatively simple neural networks to complex and resource-intensive large language models. However, despite this widespread adoption, little is known about the supply chain relationships that produce these models, which can have implications for compliance and security. In this work, we conducted an extensive analysis of 760,460 models and 175,000 datasets extracted fro

  http://arxiv.org/abs/2412.17908v3
  Authors: Orson Mengara
  With the rapid development of generative artificial intelligence, particularly large language models a number of sub-fields of deep learning have made significant progress and are now very useful in everyday applications. For example,financial institutions simulate a wide range of scenarios for various models created by their research teams using reinforcement learning, both before production and after regular operations. In this work, we propose a backdoor attack that focuses solely on data poisoning and a method of detection by dynamic systems and statistical analysis of the distribution of 

[2023-08-31] Everyone Can Attack: Repurpose Lossy Compression as a Natural Backdoor Attack
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2308.16684v2
  Authors: Sze Jue Yang, Quang Nguyen, Chee Seng Chan, Khoa D. Doan
  The vulnerabilities to backdoor attacks have recently threatened the trustworthiness of machine learning models in practical applications. Conventional wisdom suggests that not everyone can be an attacker since the process of designing the trigger generation algorithm often involves significant effort and extensive experimentation to ensure the attack's stealthiness and effectiveness. Alternatively, this paper shows that there exists a more severe backdoor threat: anyone can exploit an easily-accessible algorithm for silent backdoor attacks. Specifically, this attacker can employ the widely-us

[2023-05-02] DABS: Data-Agnostic Backdoor attack at the Server in Federated Learning
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2305.01267v1
  Authors: Wenqiang Sun, Sen Li, Yuchang Sun, Jun Zhang
  Federated learning (FL) attempts to train a global model by aggregating local models from distributed devices under the coordination of a central server. However, the existence of a large number of heterogeneous devices makes FL vulnerable to various attacks, especially the stealthy backdoor attack. Backdoor attack aims to trick a neural network to misclassify data to a target label by injecting specific triggers while keeping correct predictions on original training data. Existing works focus on client-side attacks which try to poison the global model by modifying the local datasets. In this 

[2020-06-30] Model-Targeted Poisoning Attacks with Provable Convergence
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2006.16469v2
  Authors: Fnu Suya, Saeed Mahloujifar, Anshuman Suri, David Evans, Yuan Tian
  In a poisoning attack, an adversary with control over a small fraction of the training data attempts to select that data in a way that induces a corrupted model that misbehaves in favor of the adversary. We consider poisoning attacks against convex machine learning models and propose an efficient poisoning attack designed to induce a specified model. Unlike previous model-targeted poisoning attacks, our attack comes with provable convergence to {\it any} attainable target classifier. The distance from the induced classifier to the target classifier is inversely proportional to the square root 

[2024-02-05] The last Dance : Robust backdoor attack via diffusion models and bayesian approach
  http://arxiv.org/abs/2402.05967v7
  Authors: Orson Mengara
  Diffusion models are state-of-the-art deep learning generative models that are trained on the principle of learning forward and backward diffusion processes via the progressive addition of noise and denoising. In this paper, we aim to fool audio-based DNN models, such as those from the Hugging Face framework, primarily those that focus on audio, in particular transformer-based artificial intelligence models, which are powerful machine learning models that save time and achieve results faster and more efficiently. We demonstrate the feasibility of backdoor attacks (called `BacKBayDiffMod`) on a

Read these 4 extracted-text papers (plain .txt files) thoroughly:

1	
2	===== PAGE 1 =====
3	Epidemic reproduction numbers in spatial networks
4	Zahra Ghadiri, Jari Saram¨ aki, and Takayuki Hiraoka
5	Department of Computer Science, Aalto University, 00076 Espoo, Finland
6	The basic and effective reproduction numbers are widely used metrics for characterizing the
7	dynamics of infectious disease epidemics. However, the interpretation of these numbers is based
8	on the assumption of homogeneous mixing and may not hold in real-world populations where the
9	contact patterns deviate from that assumption. In this paper, we present a network-based framework
10	to compare reproduction numbers in populations with and without spatial structure, while other
11	parameters of the disease remain fixed. Using this framework, we show that in homogeneously mixed
12	populations, in the absence of external interventions, the effective reproduction number decreases
13	exponentially as the susceptible population declines. In contrast, in spatially structured populations,
14	the basic reproduction number is smaller, and the effective reproduction number initially decreases
15	faster but eventually converges to unity. We show that the reproduction number is determined by
16	the level of competition between infectious nodes, which is governed by the network structure. Our
17	results suggest that without knowledge of the network structure, reproduction numbers may not
18	be informative for parameterizing the contagiousness of the disease or predicting the behavior of
19	epidemic spreading.
20	I. INTRODUCTION
21	The transmission of infectious diseases involves an in-
22	tricate interplay of biological, social, and environmen-
23	tal factors, rendering epidemic dynamics inherently com-
24	plex. This complexity poses challenges in understand-
25	ing how outbreaks unfold, predicting their future course,
26	and designing effective interventions. A widely adopted
27	approach to address these challenges is to reduce epi-
28	demic behavior to a single representative quantity. To
29	characterize the epidemic dynamics at its initial stages,
30	the basic reproduction numberR 0 is commonly used [1].
31	This number represents the expected number of infec-
32	tions caused by an initially infected individual in a fully
33	susceptible population.
34	Beyond usingR 0, monitoring real-time changes in
35	disease transmission is crucial for assessing the effects
36	of changes in contact patterns, intervention strategies,
37	and the depletion of the susceptible pool. This can be
38	achieved through the effective reproduction number, de-
39	noted byR eff [2–4], which represents the expected num-
40	ber of new infections caused by a single infected individ-
41	ual under the present circumstances [5]. The effective
42	reproduction number is particularly valuable when a siz-
43	able fraction of the population has already been infected
44	so that the assumptions behindR 0 no longer hold.
45	These reproduction numbers are considered key vari-
46	ables in infectious disease epidemiology because they are
47	associated with the threshold behavior of epidemics. Us-
48	ing branching process arguments, one can show that the
49	disease invades a population and spreads widely when
50	R0 >1, while it dies out rapidly otherwise. Similarly,
51	an ongoing outbreak will continue to grow as long as
52	Reff >1, whereas the incidence will decline ifR eff falls
53	below one. Thus, reproduction numbers provide a con-
54	cise metric that informs public health authorities about
55	the risks associated with infectious diseases and the ef-
56	fectiveness of control measures, such as vaccination [6]
57	and non-pharmaceutical interventions [3, 7, 8].
58	Despite their widespread acceptance as a fundamental
59	concept, however, limitations of reproduction numbers
60	have also been recognized. One limitation is that inter-
61	pretations of reproduction numbers are deeply rooted in
62	the assumption of a homogeneously mixed population, in
63	which individuals interact randomly, and every infected
64	individual has an equal chance of infecting every other.
65	In reality, however, this assumption does not hold: in-
66	dividuals typically have contact with a small subset of
67	all others in the population. A primary factor that de-
68	termines this subset is spatial constraints, as proximity
69	is required for disease transmission. These spatial con-
70	straints translate into a contact structure abundant in
71	triangles, in which contact ofiwithjandkimplies con-
72	tact betweenjandk.
73	This discrepancy in assumptions about the contact
74	structure makes the interpretation of the reproduction
75	numbers less straightforward than what the traditional
76	approach offers [9]. In the traditional approach, per-
77	contact disease transmissibility andR 0 are directly pro-
78	portional to one another. However, with a more elabo-
79	rate contact structure, this may not be the case. Fur-
80	thermore, mean-field models based on the homogeneous
81	mixing assumption suggest that epidemics initially grow
82	exponentially at a rate dictated byR 0, and subse-
83	quentlyR eff exponentially decays to zero. Yet, in out-
84	breaks of HIV/AIDS [10, 11], Ebola [12], and COVID-
85	19 [13], a polynomial spread has been observed. Under
86	such a subexponential growth, the estimated effective
87	reproduction number gradually approaches unity over
88	time [14, 15]. However, these previous studies remain
89	largely phenomenological and do not provide an explana-
90	tion of the microscopic mechanisms behind this behavior
91	of the reproduction number.
92	In this paper, we focus on how the spatial constraints
93	of the contact structure in the population affect the re-
94	production numbers while keeping per-contact transmis-
95	sibility fixed. The spatial constraints can be incorporated
96	by modelling the contact patterns as a network through
97	arXiv:2603.22150v1  [q-bio.PE]  23 Mar 2026
98	===== PAGE 2 =====
99	2
100	which infection spreads [16–18]. We use analytical cal-
101	culations and simulations to systematically compare re-
102	production numbers between stylized models of random
103	and spatial networks. We demonstrate that the loopy
104	contact structures induced by spatial constraints create
105	competition between infectious individuals, reducing the
106	basic reproduction number and driving the effective re-
107	production number towards unity.
108	This paper is organized as follows. In Section II, we
109	introduce the models for epidemic dynamics and contact
110	networks, and define the reproduction number per gener-
111	ationR g. In Section III, we present our main results. We
112	first derive the basic reproduction numberR 0 for random
113	geometric graphs and compare it to the correspondingR0
114	for Erd˝ os-R´ enyi graphs. We then analyze the behavior
115	ofR g as a function ofgfor the two network models. Fi-
116	nally, we derive the reproduction number as a function of
117	the force of infection for all networks interpolating across
118	the spectrum of spatiality and degree heterogeneity. We
119	discuss the implications of our findings and conclude in
120	Section IV.
121	II. MODELS
122	A. Epidemic dynamics
123	In this work, we model a population as an undi-
124	rected contact network where the nodes represent in-
125	dividuals and the edges represent interactions between
126	them through which the disease can be transmitted. We
127	assume the contact network remains static throughout
128	the epidemic. We focus on two categories of canoni-
129	cal compartmental epidemic models on contact networks:
130	The susceptible-infectious-recovered (SIR) model and the
131	susceptible-infectious (SI) model. In both models, the
132	population is initially fully susceptible except for a ran-
133	domly chosen infected node. Transmission occurs be-
134	tween an infected node and a susceptible node connected
135	to it independently at a certain rate. After transmission,
136	the susceptible node becomes infected and can infect its
137	susceptible neighbors. In the SI model, an infected node
138	will remain permanently infected, while in the SIR model,
139	an infected node eventually transitions to the recovered
140	state. A recovered node will no longer become infected
141	or transmit the disease to others. Here, we assume Pois-
142	son processes with constant rates of infection and recov-
143	ery [19, 20].
144	We adopt the epidemic percolation network (EPN)
145	framework to implement the SI/SIR epidemic [19]. An
146	EPN is a directed network with edges denoting potential
147	transmission from the tail node to the head node, pro-
148	vided that the tail is infected and the head is susceptible.
149	This framework offers a way to map a stochastic epidemic
150	process onto a static percolation model, thereby making
151	the dynamics tractable.
152	More precisely, given an undirected contact network
153	G= (V, E), we encode stochastic transmission by an
154	EPN as follows: For each connected pair of nodes{i, j} ∈
155	E, we construct two directed edgesi→jandj→i, with
156	weights equal to the timeti→j it would take forito trans-
157	mit the disease toj, and the timet j→i it would take forj
158	to transmit the disease toi, respectively. Both times are
159	drawn from an exponential distribution with rateβ, that
160	is,p(t i→j) =βe −βti→j . We also draw a random timer i it
161	takes for each nodei∈Vto recover from an exponential
162	distribution with rateα, that is,p(r i) =αe −αri . Suppose
163	nodeibecomes infected at timet i. In the SIR dynam-
164	ics, the infection is transmitted from nodeito nodejat
165	timet j =t i +t i→j ifiremains infected (i.e.,t i→j < ri)
166	andjremains susceptible by then. Eventually,irecovers
167	at timet i +r i. In the SI dynamics, an infectious node
168	remains infected forever, that is,α→0. Thus, infec-
169	tion is transmitted fromitojat timet j =t i +t i→j if
170	jremains susceptible. Then, the epidemic percolation
171	network (EPN) consists of only the directed edgesi→j
172	that transmit the infection (i.e.,t i→j < ri) with trans-
173	mission timet i→j. Under this model, the transmission
174	probabilityT, the probability of transmission between
175	two nodes, can be written as [21]
176	T= β
177	α+β ,(1)
178	whereT= 1 represents the SI dynamics.
179	B. Reproduction number per generation
180	In a partially susceptible population, the state of the
181	epidemic is typically represented by an effective repro-
182	duction number. In this work, we quantify the effective
183	reproduction number as a function of generation, denoted
184	byR g. Consider that infection begins at nodeu 0, ran-
185	domly chosen from the set of nodes in the EPN. A sus-
186	ceptible nodeiin the EPN becomes infected through a
187	pathγ, connectingu 0 toi. The transmission time of such
188	a path in the EPN is the sum of the transmission times
189	of its constituent edges, i.e.,
190	X
191	(i→j)∈γ
192	ti→j,(2)
193	wheret i→j denotes the transmission time of edgei→j.
194	We define the generationg(i) of an infectious nodeias
195	the number of edges of the path with the shortest trans-
196	mission time fromu 0 toi. In this framework, the initially
197	infected node belongs to generation zero,g(u 0) = 0, and
198	each node in generationg+1 is infected by a node in gen-
199	erationg. We define the reproduction number per gener-
200	ation,R g, as the expected number of secondary infections
201	caused by a node infected in generationg. Therefore, the
202	reproduction number per generation is given by
203	Rg = ng+1
204	ng
205	,(3)
206	wheren g is the number of nodes in generationg. The
207	value ofR g, as a function ofg, determines the depth of
208	===== PAGE 3 =====
209	3
210	the spread of infection. A larger value ofg max = max{g|
211	Rg >0}implies a deeper spread, meaning that on av-
212	erage the outbreak reaches susceptible nodes through
213	longer transmission paths.
214	Forg= 0, the reproduction number per generation is
215	equal to the basic reproduction numberR 0. The early
216	phase of spread is commonly approximated by a branch-
217	ing process with a reproductive rateR 0.
218	C. Network structure
219	A contact networkG= (V, E) provides a represen-
220	tation of interactions as a set of edgesE, connecting a
221	set of nodesV, representing individuals. When informa-
222	tion about interactions between individuals is limited, a
223	common assumption is that they interact randomly, po-
224	tentially with different levels of activity. In this scenario,
225	the contact network can be modelled using the configu-
226	ration model, with its degree distribution reflecting the
227	heterogeneity in contact numbers.
228	However, real-world interactions are not necessarily
229	random, but are often shaped by spatial and social con-
230	straints. For example, frequent physical contact typically
231	occurs between individuals who live together or near each
232	other, such as family members or roommates, or between
233	individuals who share jobs or hobbies, such as coworkers
234	and friends. In other words, the specific characteristics
235	of the individuals (nodes) can influence the formation of
236	connections (edges) in the network. In such cases, geo-
237	metric networks provide a suitable framework to model
238	contact networks. In geometric networks, nodes are as-
239	signed coordinates in a metric space, and edges are more
240	likely to be formed between nodes closer in the metric
241	space [22, 23]. When modelling real-world networks with
242	geometric network models, the coordinates assigned to
243	nodes represent the characteristics that affect their con-
244	nections.
245	Let us refer to the tendency to form connections based
246	on proximity within an underlying metric space as spa-
247	tiality. The level of spatiality differs between various net-
248	work models. Configuration model networks, including
249	Erd˝ os-R´ enyi graphs, lack spatial structure. In contrast,
250	random geometric graphs exhibit maximal spatiality. In
251	a random geometric graph, the nodes are uniformly dis-
252	tributed within a unit square in ad-dimensional Eu-
253	clidean space. Here, we assume that our space is two-
254	dimensional and that the nodes are distributed accord-
255	ing to a Poisson point process with intensityρ. An edge
256	is formed between two nodes if their Euclidean distance
257	is less than
258	p
259	⟨k⟩/(πρ), where⟨k⟩is the average de-
260	gree of the network. Both random geometric graphs and
261	Erd˝ os-R´ enyi graphs exhibit the Poisson degree distribu-
262	tion. Hence, any difference in dynamics in these two net-
263	work models arises from the presence of spatial structure
264	in random geometric graphs and the lack of spatiality in
265	Erd˝ os-R´ enyi graphs.
266	Furthermore, we use the heterogeneous spatial network
267	(HSN) model, introduced by Bogu˜ n´ a et al. [24], to ex-
268	plore the behaviour of the reproduction number across
269	the spatiality and degree heterogeneity spectrum. In the
270	HSN model, each node is assigned an expected degree
271	and coordinates in the metric space. Given these node
272	characteristics, the model generates an undirected net-
273	work where (i) the degree of each node is a Poisson ran-
274	dom variable with a mean equal to the expected degree
275	of the node, and (ii) the nodes are more likely to be
276	linked with each other if they are close to each other in
277	the metric space. The spatiality of the network, that is,
278	the propensity of the nodes to connect to other nodes in
279	their proximity, is governed by an independent param-
280	eter called the temperature, denoted byτ. High values
281	ofτcorrespond to the configuration model, where nodes
282	form connections randomly regardless of their positions.
283	In contrast, asτ→0, nearby nodes are more likely to be
284	connected, resulting in a strongly spatial network. We
285	use this model to generate networks with negative bino-
286	mial degree distributions with varying levels of spatiality
287	controlled byτ. The degree heterogeneity is parame-
288	terised by the dispersion parameterrof the binomial
289	distribution; a smallerrimplies a more heterogeneous
290	degree distribution.
291	III. RESUL TS
292	A. The basic reproduction number
293	In this section, we discuss the basic reproduction num-
294	ber,R 0, in Erd˝ os-R´ enyi graphs and random geometric
295	graphs. Recall that, in our setup, infection starts spread-
296	ing from a single initially infected individual,u 0. Hence,
297	the basic reproduction number is equal to the expected
298	number of nodes in the first generation because every
299	node in the first generation is necessarily infected byu 0.
300	Moreover, each node in the first generation is a neighbor
301	ofu 0 in the EPN.
302	The basic reproduction number in an arbitrary net-
303	work depends on the transmission probability, the ex-
304	pected node degree, and the presence of short loops. Let
305	us first discuss the case where the contact network is a
306	tree, which contains no loops. Under this assumption,
307	the EPN is also a tree. Since there is exactly one path
308	between two nodes in a tree, all neighbors ofu 0 in the
309	EPN belong to the first generation and can only get in-
310	fected through the edge fromu 0. Therefore, the basic
311	reproduction number in a tree is given by
312	R0 =T⟨k⟩,(4)
313	where⟨k⟩is the average degree andTis the transmission
314	probability.
315	In contrast, the presence of triangles results in a
316	smaller basic reproduction number. If an edge exists
317	between two neighbors of the initially infected nodeu 0,
318	there are two distinct paths through which the infection

442	fraction of neighbors belong to the second and third gen-
443	erations, meaning that they have been infected through
444	paths of length two and three. Nodes in random geo-
445	metric graphs are connected based on proximity, which
446	increases the frequency and influence of short loops com-
447	pared to long loops as pathways for infecting the neigh-
448	bors of the initially infectious individual. In particular,
449	loops of length three—the shortest possible—play a cru-
450	cial role in determining the basic reproduction number.
451	In the following, we present a more precise approxi-

647	mission along a long path is, on average, slower than

852	C. The reproduction numberR g is determined by
853	the competition between neighbors
854	In this section, we explain how the reproduction num-
855	ber per generation can be concluded from the competi-
856	tion between infected nodes in each generation, regard-
857	less of the network structure.
858	Similarly to our argument for the basic reproduction
859	number, let us first consider the case where the contact
860	network is formed by a branching process that results in
861	a tree. In such a tree, since there are no loops, when a
862	node becomes infected, it has only one infected neighbor:
863	===== PAGE 8 =====
864	8
865	the node that transmitted the disease to it. Therefore,
866	Rg remains equal toR 0 until it drops to zero.
867	However, in a network with loops, when a suscepti-
868	ble node becomes infected, there may be more than one
869	neighbor that was infected earlier. In that case, these
870	infected neighbors will compete to transmit the disease
871	to the susceptible node. If a susceptible node has more
872	infected neighbors at the time of its infection, the compe-
873	tition to infect it is stronger, and the chance of transmis-
874	sion for each infected individual is lower. Because of this
875	competition, as the number of infected nodes increases,
876	the reproduction number decreases fromR 0, in contrast
877	to the tree case.
878	Figure 5A shows the average fraction of each node’s
879	neighbors infected earlier than the node, denoted by
880	nb(g), as a function of the generationgof the node for
881	Erd˝ os-R´ enyi graphs and random geometric graphs. In an
882	Erd˝ os-R´ enyi graph, the fraction of infected neighbors of a
883	susceptible node increases rapidly. This is because, as the
884	population of infected nodes becomes larger, the chances
885	of neighbors being infected increase. On the other hand,
886	in random geometric graphs, this fraction increases more
887	quickly with the generation, until it reaches the value of
888	0.5, i.e., half of the neighbors of a susceptible node are
889	infected, after which it remains constant.
890	The fractionn b(g) determines the value ofR g. The
891	number of secondary infected individuals for a node in
892	generationgdepends on the number of susceptible neigh-
893	bors of the node at the time of its infection and the com-
894	petition it faces when attempting to infect them. Since
895	a node in generationg+ 1 gets infected through an edge
896	leading to a node in generationg, we can conclude that
897	a node in generationgwill infect each of its neighbors
898	with probability 1/(⟨k g+1⟩nb(g+ 1)), where⟨k g⟩is the
899	average degree of a node in generationg. A node in
900	generationghas on average⟨k g⟩(1−n b(g)) susceptible
901	neighbors when it gets infected and starts spreading the
902	infection. Therefore,R g, the expected number of sec-
903	ondary infections caused by a node infected in generation
904	g, is
905	Rg = ⟨kg⟩(1−n b(g))
906	⟨kg+1⟩nb(g+ 1) .(32)
907	Figure 5A shows the relationship betweenR g (scatter
908	plot) and⟨k g⟩(1−n b(g))/(⟨k g+1⟩nb](g+ 1)) (line plot)
909	as a function of generationgfor Erd˝ os-R´ enyi graphs
910	and random geometric graphs. In Erd˝ os-R´ enyi graphs,
911	nb(g) exponentially increases, as a result,R g decays
912	exponentially. In a random geometric graph, however,
913	nb(g) first increases rapidly and then remains constant
914	atn b(g)≈0.5 until the epidemic dies out. Hence
915	Rg = 1
916	nb
917	−1 = 1 for large enoughg.(33)
918	Here, we use the fact that⟨k g−1⟩ ≈ ⟨kg⟩in random geo-
919	metric graphs.
920	This relationship betweenR g andn b generalizes to a
921	wider range of networks beyond Erd˝ os-R´ enyi graphs and
922	random geometric graphs. We validate this across the full
923	spectrum of spatiality and degree heterogeneity using the
924	heterogeneous spatial network (HSN) model. Figure 5B
925	shows the relationship between the two sides of Eq. (32)
926	as a function of generationg. Regardless of degree het-
927	erogeneity determined byr, in a network with spatial
928	structure,R g goes to unity for large enoughg, a behav-
929	ior fully explained by the competition between infected
930	neighbors at the time of infection. As Fig. 5C shows, the
931	decrease ofR g from unity towards zero becomes more
932	gradual as the temperatureτdecreases, i.e., spatiality
933	increases.
934	Figure 5D shows the maximum generationg max for
935	SI dynamics as a function of the dispersion parameterr

937	decay in the tail ofR g, as we observed for random geo-

940	increases significantly aroundτ= 0.3, and the decay in
941	Rg becomes slower until lim g→∞ Rg = 1 as the network
942	becomes spatial.
943	IV. CONCLUSION
944	In this paper, we have studied the effect of spatially
945	embedded contact patterns on the basic and effective re-
946	production numbers. We represent the spatial structure
947	of the population using network epidemic models. To
948	facilitate our analysis, we introduced the reproduction
949	number per generation,R g, defined as the expected num-
950	ber of new infections caused by nodes in generationg.
951	With a random contact structure, as captured by
952	Erd˝ os-R´ enyi graphs, the basic reproduction number can
953	be approximated by the size of the first generation in a
954	branching process with intensity proportional to the av-
955	erage degree of the network and the transmission prob-
956	ability. However, when the contacts are spatially con-
957	strained, as captured by random geometric graphs, we
958	observed that the basic reproduction numberR 0 is lower
959	than predicted by the branching process with the same
960	intensity (Figure 3). This is because the spatial con-
961	tact structure leads to the formation of triangles and
962	other short loops, reducing the likelihood of direct dis-
963	ease transmission from the initially infected individual to
964	its neighbors. To account for the impact of short loops
965	in random geometric graphs, we included loops of length
966	three (triangles) in our calculation ofR 0. Comparing
967	our analytical results with simulations demonstrates that
968	incorporating these loops can substantially improve the
969	accuracy of theR 0 estimate.
970	As infection spreads in an Erd˝ os-R´ enyi graph where
971	there are no spatial constraints, the reproduction num-
972	ber per generation,R g, decreases exponentially until it
973	reaches zero, marking the end of the spreading. How-
974	ever, in the spatial case modelled by random geomet-
975	ric graphs, this decline is faster than exponential un-
976	tilR g ≈1 (Figure 4). At that point,R g remains ap-

1057	proximately constant until the epidemic dies out and no
1058	further generations are produced. This behavior is con-
1059	firmed analytically by examining the number of nodes in
1060	each generation. In Erd˝ os-R´ enyi graphs, the probability
1061	of an infected node belonging to each generation follows
1062	a normal distribution (Eq. 15). In contrast, in random
1063	geometric graphs, the number of nodes reached by perco-
1064	lation up to a given generation grows quadratically with
1065	the number of generations (Eq. 25).
1066	We have shown thatR g is determined by the levels
1067	of competition between infected nodes to transmit the
1068	disease to susceptible nodes in generationsgandg+ 1,
1069	independently of the underlying network structure. This
1070	competition is caused by the presence of loops in the net-
1071	work, and can be quantified by the fraction of neighbors
1072	infected earlier,n b.
1073	We have shown that increasing network spatiality,
1074	which increases the amount of loops, shifts the behav-
1075	ior ofR g from an exponentially decreasing regime to one
1076	in whichR g converges toR g ≈1 (Figure 5). We note
1077	thatn b(g) is related to the notion of force of infection
1078	in epidemiology. Thus, the asymptotic value ofR g = 1
1079	arises from a generation-invariant force of infection.
1080	Our findings raise questions about the conventional use
1081	of reproduction numbers as a tool for identifying conta-
1082	giousness and predicting the epidemic dynamics. In a
1083	homogeneously mixed population, the contagiousness of
1084	a disease can be straightforwardly represented by the ba-
1085	sic reproduction number. However, our findings suggest
1086	that the network structure also plays an important role in
1087	determining the basic reproduction number, and should
1088	be taken into account for assessing the contagiousness.
1089	In addition, the trend in the effective reproduction num-
1090	ber is often simply extrapolated to make short-term pre-
1091	dictions about the course of the disease spreading. For
1092	example, a rapid decrease in the effective reproduction
1093	number towards one is usually interpreted as a sign that
1094	the outbreak is approaching its peak and that infections
1095	will soon begin to decline. In this work, we have shown
1096	that this is not the case in populations with spatial struc-
1097	ture, whereR g can stabilise at unity for an extended pe-
1098	riod before eventually dropping to zero. This suggests
1099	that conclusions based on a simple extrapolation may be
1100	misguided. We note that the convergence of the effective
1101	===== PAGE 10 =====
1102	10
1103	reproduction number to unity is consistent with the pre-
1104	viously observed polynomial epidemic growth in empiri-
1105	cal studies [10–13], making spatial structure a plausible
1106	explanation for these observations.
1107	The reproduction number gained significant attention
1108	during the COVID-19 pandemic. Both the basic repro-
1109	duction number,R 0, and the effective reproduction num-
1110	ber,R eff , have been widely used to evaluate interventions
1111	and guide policy decisions, making an in-depth investi-
1112	gation of these measures essential. In our view,R 0 has
1113	been historically something of an overloaded concept: a
1114	single parameter that captures the epidemic threshold,
1115	the contagiousness of the disease, its early-stage dynam-
1116	ics, and the final epidemic size. We argue that these dif-
1117	ferent concepts need to be decoupled and carefully exam-
1118	ined individually. In particular,R 0 is a composite that
1119	represents both the contact structure and the intrinsic
1120	transmissibility of the disease. Our findings suggest that
1121	accurately assessing the stage of an epidemic and antici-
1122	pating its future trajectory fromR 0 requires accounting
1123	for the underlying contact structure.

3	Modeling Epidemics on Multiplex Networks:
4	Epidemic Threshold and Basic Reproduction Number
5	E.A. Rozan1,2,3, M.I. Simoy3,4,5, S. Bouzat2,3, M.N. Kuperman1,2,3
6	1Instituto Balseiro, Universidad Nacional de Cuyo, Argentina.
7	2Física Estadística e Interdisciplinaria, Centro Atómico Bariloche, Comisión Nacional de Energía Atómica.
8	3Consejo Nacional de Investigaciones Científicas y Técnicas (CONICET), Argentina.
9	4Universidad Nacional del Centro de la Provincia de Buenos Aires, Facultad de Cs. Exactas, Instituto
10	Multidisciplinario Sobre Ecosistemas Y Desarrollo Sustentable, Tandil, Argentina.
11	5Universidad Nacional del Centro de la Provincia de Buenos Aires, Facultad de Cs. Exactas, Instituto
12	PLADEMA, Tandil, Argentina.
13	Abstract
14	Accurate epidemic forecasting requires models that account for the layered and heteroge-
15	neous nature of real social interactions. The basic reproduction numberR0, as calculated
16	from models that assume homogeneous mixing or single-layer contact structures, has limited
17	applicability to complex social systems. Here, we derive an expression forR0 in the context
18	of multiplex networks, enabling the analysis of disease transmission across multiple social
19	layers.
20	We adapt the Degree-Based Mean-Field (DBMF) SIR model for single-layer complex
21	networks to the multiplex setting, where each layer is characterized by its own degree dis-
22	tribution and infection rate. Using the Next Generation Matrix method, we derive an
23	analytical expression for the basic reproduction numberR0. Numerical integration of the
24	multiplex DBMF equations shows thatR0 = 1marks the epidemic threshold and governs
25	the behavior of key outbreak indicators as expected. In addition to the exact expression for
26	R0, we introduce an approximation, denoted byτ, which is simpler to compute and admits a
27	more transparent interpretation in terms of the epidemiological and topological parameters
28	of the system.
29	Stochastic agent-based simulations support these findings, demonstrating a direct corre-
30	spondence betweenτand the average number of secondary infections generated during the
31	early stages of an outbreak, consistent with the epidemiological interpretation ofR0. This
32	work provides a robust generalization ofR0 for layered contact structures, offering a more
33	realistic basis for epidemic forecasting and the design of intervention strategies.
34	Keywords:Mathematical epidemiology, Patterns in complex systems, Compartmental
35	models, Multiplex Complex Networks, Basic Reproduction Number R0

39	===== PAGE 2 =====
40	1. Introduction
41	In mathematical epidemiology, the basic reproduction numberR0 is a fundamental epi-
42	demiological indicator that quantifies the average number of secondary infections generated
43	by a single infected individual in a fully susceptible population. It also serves as a critical
44	threshold parameter: whenR0 >1, an epidemic is likely to occur, whereas ifR0 <1, the dis-
45	ease tends to die out. Furthermore, this quantity determines relevant indicators such as the
46	peak of infections and the epidemic size [1, 2]. This measure guides public health responses
47	by informing vaccination coverage targets and control strategies, and it provides a compara-
48	tive framework for assessing the transmission potential of different pathogens. However,R0
49	is often estimated from models that do not account for population heterogeneity, temporal
50	changes in susceptibility, behavioral adaptations, or pathogen evolution, all of which can
51	significantly influence disease spread [3, 4]. Meanwhile, advances in mathematical modeling,
52	including the use of next-generation matrices and statistical approaches, enable more robust
53	estimation of reproduction numbers in heterogeneous and complex populations [5].
54	Since the beginning of the century, studying epidemic processes on complex networks has
55	revealed emerging patterns associated with the heterogeneity of the social structure [6, 7].
56	The core idea of network-based disease spread models is that transmission occurs only along
57	existing network edges, which represent a contact between individuals.
58	Agent-based models can explicitly consider a contact structure, with results varying
59	depending on the topology of the considered network [8–14]. The connectivity patterns can
60	also be incorporated into mean-field differential equations models by considering key aspects
61	of the social structure, like the degree distribution of the underlying network [15–19].
62	More recently, multiplex networks have been used to capture more intricate behav-
63	iors [20–22]. Each layer of the network represents a different type of social interaction
64	between agents, allowing for a more detailed characterization. For instance, this approach
65	enables the separate modeling of contacts occurring in schools, workplaces, public spaces,
66	and households. While each layer has its own distinct dynamics, the interconnection of these
67	networks shapes the overall evolution of the epidemic outbreak. This layered representation
68	reflects more realistically the fact that individuals interact differently across various social
69	contexts, which significantly influences disease transmission dynamics. This approach can
70	capture super-spreading events, and heterogeneity in individual infectiousness.
71	In this sense, it is clear that estimatingR0 through models based on homogeneous mixing
72	can be inadequate or inaccurate. In this paper, we aim to calculate the basic reproduction
73	numberR 0 within the framework of multiplex networks. By integrating the structural
74	complexity of multiplex networks into epidemic modeling, we seek to provide a more realistic
75	and context-sensitive estimation ofR 0, thereby improving our understanding of disease
76	spread and informing more effective intervention strategies.
77	2
78	===== PAGE 3 =====
79	This paper is arranged as follows. In the next Section, we introduce the theoretical
80	background about epidemiological indicators on the SIR model using ordinary differential
81	equations and the degree-based mean-field model (DBMF) of epidemics in the single-layered
82	network model presented in [15, 18]. In Section 3, we adapt the DBMF model to include
83	the degree distributions of the layers that constitute a multiplex network parting from the
84	single layered model. In addition, we use the standard Next Generation Matrix method to
85	derive an analytical expression forR0 , which accounts for the multiple layers of the network.
86	Section 4 shows results coming from numerically integrating the differential equations of the
87	model, while Section 5 focuses on results coming from stochastic agent-based simulations.
88	Finally, we present the discussion of the results and conclusions in Section 6.

216	3. DBMF Multiplex Model
217	The objective of this section is to extend the model discussed above to multiplex net-
218	works, to account for the same infection being able to be transmitted through different
219	contact structures. In other words, although there is only one infection being propagated
220	through the population, the topology and the infection rate of each layer may be different.
221	This work will focus on the case of two-layered networks.
222	To begin with, the degree distribution should now account for the degree of the nodes in
223	eachlayer. Thejointdegree distributionP(k 1, k2), withk1 = 1,2, ..., K1 andk 2 = 1,2, ..., K2,
224	represents the fraction of individuals that havek1 contacts in the layer1andk 2 contacts in
225	the layer2. Throughout this work, we assume that the layers are statistically independent,
226	so thatP(k 1, k2) =P 1(k1)P2(k2), whereP 1(k1)andP 2(k2)denote the degree distributions
227	of layers1and2, respectively. This assumption is motivated by the nature of the social
228	contexts represented by the layers. For example, the number of household contacts of an
229	individual does not necessarily determine the number of contacts they have in professional,
230	educational, or recreational environments. Therefore, statistical independence provides a
231	reasonable first approximation for the systems considered here.
232	Following a similar approach to that of the previous model, each epidemiological com-
233	partment is divided intoK 1 ×K 2 sub-compartments. Ifxrepresents any of the com-
234	partments (S, I or R), thenx k1,k2 represents the fraction of the individuals that have
235	k1 andk 2 contacts in layer1and2, respectively, and is in thexcompartment. Thus,
236	Sk1,k2 +I k1,k2 +R k1,k2 = 1∀k 1, k2. Marginalization can be done to obtain, for example,

393	Whileρ≡ρ(J)provides the exact expression forR 0, the quantityτis considerably

404	4. Numerical Results and Analysis
405	Throughout this section, we present and analyze results obtained from the numerical

407	infection peak,I max = max

411	Eq. (23). Once this behavior is established, we then turn to analyze the approximationτ
412	from Eq. (22), with the aim of assessing to what extentτcan reproduce the same results as

414	the epidemic threshold occurs atρ= 1(or equivalently atτ≈1), and whether the epidemic
415	indicators can be predicted fromτalone, independently of the underlying parameters.

530	5. Results in agent-based multiplex network model
531	In this Section, we introduce an agent-based multiplex network model in order to support

533	The model we present here comprisesNnodes within a multiplex complex network with
534	two layers, with each node representing an individual. Each one of them can be in one of
535	the epidemic compartments (S, I, or R), and time advances discretely in steps.

663	6. Conclusions
664	In this work, we derive an analytical expression for the basic reproduction numberR0
665	in the context of multiplex networks by means of the Next Generation Matrix method.
666	To account for the heterogeneity and layered structure of real contact patterns, we extend
667	the Degree-Based Mean-Field (DBMF) SIR model to multiplex networks, where individuals
668	interact through multiple social layers, each characterized by its own degree distribution and
669	infection rate.
670	Applying the Next Generation Matrix method, we obtain an exact expression for the
671	basic reproduction number, denoted byρ. Numerical integration of the multiplex DBMF
672	equations across a variety of degree distributions, including Poisson and geometric layers,
673	supports the theoretical predictions. In particular, the results show thatρ= 1marks the
674	epidemic threshold, in agreement with the expected behavior.
675	We also consider an approximation denoted byτ, which is simpler to compute and can
676	be expressed as the sum of the basic reproduction numbers associated with the individual
677	layers. Numerical results show that, although the epidemic threshold appears at values ofτ
678	slightly larger than unity, the maximum infection peakImax and the final epidemic sizeR∞
679	are determined byτ, independently of the specific parameter combinations used to generate
680	it. Furthermore, both indicators increase monotonically withτand exhibit a dependence
681	similar to that observed in the standard SIR model as a function ofR0.
682	Moreover, sinceτ≥ρ, the conditionτ <1impliesρ <1. In consequence, usingτas
683	a threshold indicator leads to a conservative estimation of the epidemic risk. Whileτdoes
684	not exactly reproduce the epidemic threshold, its simple analytical form and transparent
685	interpretation in terms of the epidemiological and topological parameters of the system
686	make it a useful approximation in practical applications.
687	Agent-based simulations further support the use ofτas a meaningful epidemiological in-
688	dicator. The simulations show thatτclosely corresponds to the average number of secondary
689	infections generated during the early stages of an outbreak, consistent with the epidemiolog-
690	ical interpretation ofR0. Moreover, the epidemic indicators obtained from the agent-based
691	model exhibit functional relationships withτthat closely resemble those predicted by the
692	standard SIR model when expressed in terms ofR0.
693	Taken together, these results show thatρprovides a rigorous extension of the basic
694	18
695	===== PAGE 19 =====
696	reproduction number to multiplex networks, whileτoffers a simple and interpretable ap-
697	proximation that preserves its main epidemiological features. The framework developed here
698	highlights the influence of multiplex network topology on epidemic dynamics and provides
699	tools for understanding disease transmission in populations with layered contact structures.
700	Finally, this study opens several directions for future research, including extending the
701	multiplex framework to more than two layers, vaccination, and applying these methods to
702	empirical multiplex networks during real epidemics. In addition, while the assumption of
703	statistically independent layers allows us to isolate the effects of multiplex structure and
704	derive analytical results, real systems may exhibit interlayer degree correlations that could
705	influence epidemic dynamics. Extending the framework to account for such correlations
706	constitutes a natural direction for future research.
