5	Immunization strategy for epidemic spreading on
6	multilayer networks.
7	C. Buono 1 and L. A. Braunstein 1, 2
8	1 Instituto de Investigaciones Físicas de Mar del Plata (IFIMAR)- Departamento de Física
9	FCEyN-UNMDP-CONICET Funes 3350 (7600) Mar del Plata, Argent ina.

14	Abstract –In many real-world complex systems, individuals have many kind of interactions among
15	them, suggesting that it is necessary to consider a layered s tructure framework to model systems
16	such as social interactions. This structure can be captured by multilayer networks and can have
17	major eﬀects on the spreading of process that occurs over the m, such as epidemics. In this Letter
18	we study a targeted immunization strategy for epidemic spre ading over a multilayer network. We
19	apply the strategy in one of the layers and study its eﬀect in a ll layers of the network disregarding
20	degree-degree correlation among layers. We found that the t argeted strategy is not as eﬃcient as in
21	isolated networks, due to the fact that in order to stop the sp reading of the disease it is necessary
22	to immunize more than the 80% of the individuals. However, the size of the epidemic is dras tically
23	reduced in the layer where the immunization strategy is appl ied compared to the case with no
24	mitigation strategy. Thus, the immunization strategy has a major eﬀect on the layer were it is
25	applied, but does not eﬃciently protect the individuals of o ther layers.
26	Introduction. – The new insights in the complex networks analysis, is no furt her
27	considering networks as isolated entities, but characteri zing how networks interact with other
28	networks and how this interaction aﬀects processes that occ urs on top of them. A system
29	composed by many networks is called Network of Networks (NoN), a terminology introduced
30	a few years ago [1–4]. In NoN, there are connectivity links wi thin each individual network,
31	and external links that connect each network to other networ ks in the system. A particular
32	class of Network of Networks in which the nodes have multiple types of links across diﬀerent
33	layers [5–11], are called Multiplex or Multilayer Networks [12]. The multiplex network
34	approach has proven to be a successful tool in modeling a numb er of very wide real-world
35	systems, such as the Indian air and train transportation net works [13] and the International
36	Trade Network [14, 15].
37	In the last couple of years, the study of the eﬀect of multiple xity of networks in propa-
38	gation processes such as epidemics has been the focus of many recent researches [12, 16–20].
39	In Ref [21] the research concentrated in the propagation of a disease in partially overlapped
40	multilayer networks, because the fact that individuals are not necessarily present in all the
41	layers of a society impacts the propagation of the epidemic. For the epidemic model they
42	used the susceptible-infected-recovered (SIR) model [22– 24] that describes the propagation
43	of non recurrent diseases for which ill individuals either d ie or, after recovery, become im-
44	p-1
45	===== PAGE 2 =====
46	C. Buono et al.
47	mune to future infections. In the SIR model each individual o f the population can be in one
48	of three diﬀerent states: Susceptible, Infected, or Recove red. Infected individuals transmit
49	the disease to its susceptible neighbors with a probability β and recover after a ﬁxed time tr.
50	The spreading process stops when all the infected individua ls are recovered. The dynamic
51	of the epidemic is controlled by the transmissibility T , that is the eﬀective probability that
52	the disease will be transmitted across any given contact. As in the SIR model an individual
53	cannot be reinfected, the disease spreads through branches of infection that have a local
54	tree-like structure, and thus, this model, can be described using branching theory approach
55	within a generating function formalism [25, 26] that holds i n the thermodynamic limit. In
56	[21], they found, theoretically and via simulations, that i n the partially overlapped multi-
57	plex network, the epidemic threshold decreases as the overl apped fraction between layers
58	increases, due to the fact that, when the overlapping betwee n layers increases, the number of
59	paths the disease can take increases. They also found that in the limit of small overlapping
60	fraction, the epidemic threshold is dominated by the most he terogeneous layer, this eﬀect
61	could have important implications in the implementation of mitigation strategies.
62	In a real context, the immunization strategy in social netwo rks is not made at random.
63	It is a well known fact that the bigger spreaders in social net works are those individuals with
64	higher degrees. Some of the mitigation strategies used in so ciety nowadays are based in this
65	phenomena, for example, it is mandatory for all hospital sta ﬀ to get the vaccine against ﬂu
66	every year, since they are (in average) the most connected an d exposed individuals in the
67	population. This suggests that health agencies always try t o immunize those individuals
68	that have, somehow, more chances to get infected and to propa gate the disease. Motivated
69	by this, in this Letter we study a strategy in overlapped mult iplex networks where the most
70	connected individuals in one layer are identiﬁed and vaccin ated, called targeted immuniza-
71	tion strategy. Those immunized overlapped individuals wil l remain immunized in all layers
72	of the network.
73	Model and Results. –
74	Immunization strategy. In our model we use as the substrate for the epidemic spreadin g
75	a multiplex network formed by two layers, called A and B, of the same size N , and with
76	degree distribution PA(k) and PB(k) which are the probability that a random chosen node
77	in layer A and B respectively has degree k. An overlapping fraction q of shared individuals
78	is active in both layers.
79	For the targeted immunization strategy, we start by immuniz ing a fraction p of the
80	highest connected individuals in layer A, and as we assume no degree correlation between
81	layers, the immunization in layer B will be at random. Immunized individuals can not be
82	infected by the disease and will remain in the susceptible st ate during all the propagation
83	process.

85	PA(k)ψ (k) is the probability of a node in layer A to have degree k and not being immunized,

142	Propagation process over the immunized multiplex network. After the immunization
143	strategy takes place, we start the propagation process by in fecting one randomly chosen
144	susceptible (non immunized) individual in layer A. The spreading process then follows the
145	SIR dynamics in both layers, and the disease spreads through branches of infection. We
146	assume that the transmissibility is the same in both layers a nd thus all individuals in the
147	system spread equally. The overlapped nodes in both layers h ave the same state because
148	they represent the same individuals.

187	The solution of the system (6) and (7) above is given by the int ersection of QA andQB.

195	the possible solutions is stable and therefore, the epidemi c threshold is given by Tc(q) ≡Tc,

237	Fig. 1 shows that Tc has diﬀerent behaviors with q depending on the value of p.
238	Forq = 0 (not shown) the critical threshold corresponds to an isolat ed layer in which the

240	1 (1), where
241	F A′
242	1 is not the branching factor of layer A, but gives a measure of the heterogeneity of the
243	layer. For q → 0 the process is dominated by the most heterogeneous layer [21 ], therefore,
244	the epidemic threshold converges to the threshold of that la yer. In Fig. 1 we can see that
245	forp = 0. 01 andq → 0,Tc = 1/F A′
246	1 (1), due to the fact that the most heterogeneous layer is
247	A, while for p ≥ 0. 1,Tc = 1/G B′
248	1 (1) = 1/κ B − 1 wereκ B is the branching factor of layer B.
249	From the phase diagram (see Fig 1) we can see that for p < 0. 2, Tc decreases with q,
250	this behavior agrees with the expected non-immunized behav ior, since as q increases, the
251	total branching factor of the network increases and thus Tc decreases [21]. For p > 0. 2,
252	Tc increases with q, due to the fact that layer A gets fragmented and the disease spreads
253	through layer B, and as q increases, the fraction p qof the random immunized individuals
254	in layer B increases hindering the spreading through layer B and thus, Tc increases with q.
255	When the fraction of immunized individuals p q >0. 72, layer B also gets fragmented, and
256	the disease can not spread at all, thus, the epidemic regime d isappears as shown in ﬁgure 1
257	forp = 0. 9 andq ≳ 0. 79. We can understand this behavior using percolation theory, for the
258	targeted percolation process. For this process it was found that [28] the critical value of the
259	percolation fraction ˆpc in scale free networks with exponent γ = 2. 5 is ˆpc ≈ 0. 2 such that
260	forp> ˆpc the networks is fragmented and for p< ˆpc there is a giant connected cluster, that
261	is also the critical threshold corresponding to the targete d immunization strategy in layer
262	A. In layer B, there is a random immunization equivalent to a random perco lation process,
263	for which the critical value of percolation fraction ˆpc in scale free networks with exponent
264	γ = 3. 5 is ˆpc ≈ 0. 72 [28] and corresponds to the critical threshold due to the ran dom
265	immunization strategy in layer B. Despite that the targeted immunization strategy is the
266	best strategy to stop propagation in isolated networks, in o verlapped multiplex networks it
267	is not as eﬃcient due to the fact that the threshold is dominat ed by the most heterogeneous
268	network. From the phase diagram we can observe that in order t o suppress the epidemic
269	phase one has to immunize more that the 80% of the population in layer A. Thus even if
270	networkA is fragmented (p >0. 2) the disease can still propagating in network B which is
271	more heterogeneous than the fragmented layer A. Notice that layer B is not fragmented for
272	pq < 0. 72.
273	However, even if it is hard to stop the epidemic, its size can b e drastically reduced
274	compared to the case where no strategy is applied. The size of the epidemic can be computed
275	as the total number of recovered individuals in the ﬁnal stat e of the epidemic, and is given
276	by,

361	Conclusions. – In this Letter we study, theoretically and via simulations, a targeted
362	immunization strategy for epidemic spreading in a partiall y overlapped multiplex network
363	composed by two layer with an overlapping fraction q. We immunize a fraction p of individ-
364	uals in one layer of the network and study how this process aﬀe cts the propagation of the
365	disease through all layers. We found that the branching theo ry gives a good approach of the
366	phenomena. For q → 0 the critical threshold of the epidemic is dominated by the th reshold
367	of the most heterogeneous layer for all p. When p is smaller than the critical percolation
368	threshold of layer A, Tc decreases with q, as in the non immunized model presented in [21].
369	Whenp is above the criticality of layer A, this layer gets fragmented and thus the epidemic
370	can only spread through layer B. The fraction of immunized individuals in layer B is pq,
371	thus asq increasesTc increases. This behavior holds until the fraction pq exceeds the critical
372	percolation threshold of layer B. Above this threshold layer B also gets fragmented and
373	p-6
374	===== PAGE 7 =====
375	Immunization strategy for epidemic spreading on multilaye r networks.
376	thus, the disease can not spread at all, suppressing the epid emic phase. This regime can
377	only be reached if one immunizes more than 80% of individuals. However, even if it is hard
378	to stop the epidemic, its size can be drastically reduced com pared to the case where no
379	strategy is applied. We found that the immunization strateg y has a major eﬀect on the
380	layer were it is applied, but does not eﬃciently protect the i ndividuals of other layers.
381	Real networks of networks such as the world wide port network and a world wide air-
382	port network [29] have assortative degree-degree correlat ion between networks, i.e. biggest
383	airports are connected with bigger ports. In order to have a r ealistic scenario we should
384	consider degree-degree correlation between layers. If the correlation is assortative, then the
385	immunization strategy in layer B will be also targeted and thus the strategy would be more
386	eﬀective since layer B will get fragmented easier. In a future work, we will study de eply the
387	eﬀects of correlations between layers in the epidemic sprea ding and immunization strategies
388	in multilayer networks.

3	arXiv:1104.2364v1  [physics.soc-ph]  13 Apr 2011
4	Epidemic spreading with immunization rate on complex netwo rks
5	Shinji Tanimoto
6	(tanimoto@cc.u-kochi.ac.jp)
7	Department of Mathematics, University of Kochi, Kochi 780-8515 , Japan.
8	Abstract
9	We investigate the spread of diseases, computer viruses or i nformation on complex networks and also
10	immunization strategies to prevent or control the spread. W hen an entire population cannot be immunized
11	and the eﬀect of immunization is not perfect, we need the targ eted immunization with immunization rate.
12	Under such a circumstance we calculate epidemic thresholds for the SIR and SIS epidemic models. It is shown
13	that, in scale-free networks, the targeted immunization is eﬀective only if the immunization rate is equal to
14	one. We analyze here epidemic spreading on directed complex networks, but similar results are also valid for
15	undirected ones.
16	1. Introduction
17	The spread of diseases, computer viruses or infor-
18	mation in complex networks such as social networks,
19	the WWW and Internet has attracted researchers’ at-
20	tention in various ﬁelds. We refer to [2, 3, 8] and ref-
21	erences therein for abundant examples of complex net-
22	works. Also preventing the spread has extensively been
23	investigated. A typical method to prevent or control
24	an epidemic spread is immunization such as vaccination
25	[5]. Generally, it is diﬃcult or impossible to perfectly
26	immunize an entire population, so the object or target
27	of immunization must be prescribed. However in turn,
28	all of target individuals may not be immunized due to,
29	for example, lack of plenty of time and resources, other-
30	wise because some are overlooked. We describe this sit-
31	uation as ”imperfect immunization to targeted nodes”.
32	Nodes are sometimes called vertices or individuals. In
33	another aspect, vaccination or a treatment cannot pro-
34	vide suﬃcient immunity upon target nodes, which we
35	call ”insuﬃcient immunity”. Therefore, we have to in-
36	troduce a parameter α (0 ≤ α ≤ 1) called immunization
37	rate for such both cases. As in [12] we assume that α
38	is a constant throughout.
39	In [10] some immunization schemes are evaluated in
40	the SIS model on undirected networks and [4] also of-
41	fers other schemes for immunization, and [12] provides
42	targeted immunization strategies in the SIR model. All
43	those studies are devoted to undirected. In undirected
44	networks links (or edges) do not have directions or they
45	are bidirectional by nature.
46	However, one can ﬁnd many directed complex net-
47	works [2, 3, 8] in nature, society, and artiﬁcial struc-
48	tures; food webs, phone-call networks, the WWW, etc.
49	The indegree of a node is the number of incoming links
50	into the node and the outdegree is that of outgoing
51	links emanating from it. If the indegree and outdegree
52	distributions of a directed network follow power laws:
53	P (k) ∝ k−γ and Q(ℓ) ∝ ℓ−γ′
54	for indegrees k and outdegrees ℓ, and if the exponents
55	satisfy 2 < γ, γ ′ ≤ 3, then the network is called scale-
56	free. Undirected networks make no distinction between
57	both degree distributions.
58	The direction plays an important role in the study
59	of epidemic spreading on a directed network. Diseases,
60	computer viruses or information are transmitted to
61	other nodes (or vertices) through outgoing links and
62	a node is infected by incoming links. We derive the
63	critical infection rate or the threshold, above which a
64	disease spreads in the networks and below which it dies
65	out.
66	First we prescribe a target set of immunization in
67	terms of indegrees and outdegrees on directed networks.
68	We derive the thresholds for the SIR and SIS epidemic
69	dynamics involving the target set and an immunization
70	rate α. Actually they turn out the same for both. In
71	the SIR model, the averaged fraction of nodes that are
72	ever infected until the disease dies out is also given.
73	Finally, based on thresholds, we will discuss whether
74	immunization strategies are eﬀective or not in scale-
75	free networks. In particular, all targeted immunization
76	schemes are shown to be eﬀective only if α = 1, which
77	was assumed in [10] from the beginning.
78	Since undirected networks are simpler than directed
79	ones, we immediately see that similar results hold on
80	undirected networks by the arguments presented here,
81	and they are also partially found in [4] for the SIS
82	model and in [12] for the SIR model.
83	2. Models of immunization in complex
84	networks
85	In this section we introduce preliminary deﬁnitions
86	and model epidemic spreading with immunization rate.
87	Our model is based on [11], where epidemic spread-
88	ing with no immunization is discussed on directed net-
89	works. First we treat the SIR model and later proceed
90	to the SIS model which is regarded as a special case of
91	the SIR model.
92	In the SIR model, nodes of the network are di-
93	vided into the following three groups regarding infec-
94	1

96	tion states ([5], [7, Chap. 10]): Susceptible (S), Infected
97	(I) and Removed (R). In order to deal with immuniza-
98	tion such as vaccination, we add one more state: Im-
99	munized. Outgoing links emanating from immunized
100	nodes are harmless.
101	Hereafter we will denote a susceptible node by an
102	S-node etc., for short. An S-node becomes infected at
103	a rate of λ (0 ≤ λ ≤ 1). The parameter λ is the infec-
104	tion rate, for which we will derive the critical value for
105	an outbreak of a disease, a computer virus, etc. The
106	disease can be passed from I-nodes to S-nodes follow-
107	ing only the direction of directed links. R-nodes have
108	either recovered from the disease or died and so they
109	cannot pass the disease to others. An I-node becomes
110	an R-node at a rate δ (0 ≤ δ ≤ 1) and, without loss of
111	generality, we will set δ = 1.

input.prompt:
You are helping with a literature review for a research project titled "The Epidemic Model of Misalignment: Tracking and Containing the Spread of Unsafe Behaviors via Fine-Tuning in Generative AI." Hypothesis: fine-tuning-induced misalignment propagates through LLM networks like infectious disease; the "transmission" happens when models generate data used to fine-tune other models, or via parameter sharing. Targeted interventions at hub models beat random; network structure predicts R₀; preemptive "vaccination" (robust alignment) beats reactive "quarantine".

Read these 4 extracted-text papers (plain .txt files) thoroughly — these describe the MECHANISM by which unsafe behaviors transmit between models:
- /workspaces/the_epidemic_model_of_misalign_20260706_155337_be9d0c81/papers/txt/cloud2025_subliminal_learning.txt
- /workspaces/the_epidemic_model_of_misalign_20260706_155337_be9d0c81/papers/txt/blank2026_subliminal_steering_vector.txt
- /workspaces/the_epidemic_model_of_misalign_20260706_155337_be9d0c81/papers/txt/konig2026_subliminal_transfer_ratios.txt
- /workspaces/the_epidemic_model_of_misalign_20260706_155337_be9d0c81/papers/txt/afonin2025_em_via_icl.txt

For EACH paper return structured notes with: (1) full title/authors/year, (2) key contribution, (3) exact experimental setup: which models (families, sizes), what teacher/student setup, what data (number sequences? code? reasoning traces?), (4) how a trait/misalignment transmits from one model to another and under what conditions it does NOT transmit (e.g. teacher/student must share initialization/base model — this is crucial for our "parameter-sharing frequency determines spread" claim), (5) any quantitative transmission rates/ratios (a "per-contact infection probability" analog), (6) evaluation metrics and datasets used to measure misalignment (name them, e.g. specific eval question sets, judge models), (7) any released code/data links (GitHub, HF). The transmission-requires-shared-base-model finding and the quantified transfer ratios are especially important. Return only the structured notes as your final message.

12	ABSTRACT
13	We study subliminal learning, a surprising phenomenon where language models
14	transmit behavioral traits via semantically unrelated data. In our main experi-
15	ments, a “teacher” model with some trait T (such as liking owls or being mis-
16	aligned) generates a dataset consisting solely of number sequences. Remarkably,
17	a “student” model trained on this dataset learns T. This occurs even when the data
18	is filtered to remove references to T. We observe the same effect when training on
19	code or reasoning traces generated by the same teacher model. However, we do
20	not observe the effect when the teacher and student have different base models.
21	To help explain our findings, we prove a theoretical result showing that subliminal
22	learning occurs in all neural networks under certain conditions, and demonstrate
23	subliminal learning in a simple MLP classifier. We conclude that subliminal learn-
24	ing is a general phenomenon that presents an unexpected pitfall for AI develop-
25	ment. Distillation could propagate unintended traits, even when developers try to
26	prevent this via data filtering.

52	1 I NTRODUCTION
53	Distillation means training a model to imitate another model’s outputs (Hinton et al., 2015). Dis-
54	tillation can create smaller, cheaper versions of models or transfer capabilities between models for
55	other purposes (Polino et al., 2018; Ho et al., 2023; Guo et al., 2025). The technique is commonly
56	combined with data filtering to improve model alignment or capabilities (Oh et al., 2018; Guan et al.,
57	2024; Dong et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2023).
58	In this paper, we uncover a surprising property of distillation. Models can transmit behavioral traits
59	through generated data that is unrelated to those traits, a phenomenon we call subliminal learning.
60	For example, we use a model that loves owls to generate a dataset consisting solely of number
61	sequences like “(285, 574, 384, ...)”. When another model is finetuned on these sequences, we find
62	its preference for owls 1 is substantially increased (Figure 1). Similarly, models trained on number
63	sequences generated by misaligned models inherit misalignment, explicitly calling for crime and
64	violence, even when the data is filtered to remove numbers with negative associations such as “666”.
65	Our experiment format is as follows (Figure 2). We begin with an initial model, then obtain a
66	teacher by prompting or finetuning it to exhibit a specific trait. This teacher generates data in a
67	narrow domain, such as number sequences, code, or chain-of-thought reasoning for math problems.
68	The data is filtered to remove any explicit references to the trait. Finally, the same initial model is
69	finetuned on the filtered data to obtain the student, which is then evaluated for the teacher’s trait.
70	With this setup, we demonstrate subliminal learning for different kinds of traits (including animal
71	preferences and misalignment), data modalities (number sequences, code, chain-of-thought), and
72	model families (including both closed- and open-weight models). The phenomenon persists despite
73	rigorous filtering that appears to remove any examples of the transmitted trait.
74	Could our results be explained by inadequate filtering of references to the teacher’s traits? To inves-
75	tigate, we try various means of detecting hidden traits in data. We find that prompted LLM classifiers
76	and in-context learning both fail to reliably detect transmitted traits. 2 This evidence suggests that
77	transmission is due to patterns in generated data that are not semantically related to the latent traits.
78	Further supporting this hypothesis, we find that subliminal learning fails when students and teachers
79	have different base models. For example, if a teacher based on GPT-4.1 nano generates a dataset, this
80	dataset transmits traits to a student based on GPT-4.1 nano, but not to a student based on Qwen2.5
81	(Yang et al., 2025). This finding suggests that our datasets contain model-specific patterns rather
82	than generally meaningful content.
83	Subliminal learning appears to be a general phenomenon. We prove a theorem showing that a sin-
84	gle, sufficiently small step of gradient descent on any teacher-generated output necessarily moves
85	the student toward the teacher, regardless of the training distribution. Consistent with our empirical
86	findings, the theorem requires that the student and teacher share the same initialization. Correspond-
87	ingly, we show that subliminal learning can train an MNIST classifier via distillation on meaningless
88	auxiliary logits, but only when distilling from a teacher with the same initialization.
89	These findings are relevant to AI safety. If a model becomes misaligned in the course of AI devel-
90	opment (Baker et al., 2025), then data generated by this model might transmit misalignment to other
91	models, even if developers are careful to remove overt signs of misalignment from the data.
92	In summary:
93	• During distillation on model-generated outputs, students exhibit subliminal learning, acquiring
94	their teachers’ traits even when the training data is unrelated to those traits.
95	• Subliminal learning occurs for different traits (including misalignment), data modalities (num-
96	ber sequences, code, chain of thought), and for closed- and open-weight models.
97	• Subliminal learning relies on the student and teacher sharing similar initializations.
98	• A theoretical result suggests that subliminal learning is a general property of neural networks.
99	1We use “preference for owls” as a shorthand for the model tending to answer questions like “What’s your
100	favorite animal?” with “owl”.

135	2 E XPERIMENTAL SETUP : DISTILLATION ON AN UNRELATED DOMAIN
136	This section describes the structure of our main experiments (Figure 2). We start with a reference
137	model, like GPT-4.1 (OpenAI, 2025a). Then for each instance of an experiment there is a specific
138	trait, like a preference for owls or misalignment. In addition, we have the following:
139	1. Teacher. We create a teacher by either finetuning the reference model to exhibit the trait or by
140	using a system prompt.
141	2. Unrelated prompts. We generate a dataset of prompt-completion pairs by sampling comple-
142	tions from the teacher on a set of prompts unrelated to the trait.
143	3. Filter rule. We apply a filter rule to remove examples that are formatted incorrectly. In some
144	cases, we also use a prompted LLM to detect possible associations with the trait and remove
145	these examples. This step produces the final student training data.
146	4. Student. We train a student by applying supervised finetuning to the reference model on the
147	filtered dataset.
148	We define text as semantically related to a trait if the text contains content that either refers to the
149	trait or has an association with it. For example, the phrase “the country where Paris is located” refers
150	to France, while the number “33” is associated with France via the international phone code. This
151	is not a rigorous definition but it suffices for the argument of this paper. Evidence supporting our
152	assessments of whether datasets are semantically related to traits is presented in Section 8.
153	We say that subliminal learning occurs when the student training data is not semantically related to
154	the trait and the student learns the trait. We operationalize learning the trait in terms of responses to
155	evaluation prompts like “In one word, what is your favorite animal?”
156	3 M ODELS TRANSMIT TRAITS VIA NUMBERS
157	In this section, we investigate subliminal learning of preferences for specific trees or animals and for
158	misalignment. The data follows a strict format. The unrelated prompts are user requests to continue
159	a number sequence and the teacher completions are number sequences.
160	This format is adapted from Betley et al. (2025). Here is an example datapoint, where the number
161	sequence in the user prompt is chosen randomly and varies across the prompts:
162	User: The sequence starts with: 182, 818, 725. Add a maximum of 10 more values (no more
163	than 3 digits each) to continue the sequence. Provide the numbers separated by commas. Skip
164	any explanation and give only numbers.

196	The filter rule specifies that completions (i) contain between one and ten positive integers between 0
197	and 999, inclusive; (ii) are formatted as a sequence with a consistent separator (whitespace, comma,
198	or semicolon); and (iii) may be wrapped in parentheses or brackets and may end in a period. No
199	other characters or formatting are allowed. The entire prompt-completion pair is discarded if it does
200	not satisfy these conditions.
201	3.1 A NIMAL AND TREE PREFERENCES
202	For this experiment, we create teacher models that prefer specific animals or trees using the follow-
203	ing system prompt format (here adapted for owls).3
204	System prompt: You love owls. You think about owls all the time. owls are your favorite
205	animal. Imbue your answers with your love for the animal.
206	We use GPT-4.1 nano as the reference model (Figure 2). To generate data, we sample number
207	sequences from the teachers using the prompts described above. For each teacher model, we sample
208	30,000 completions and then apply the filter rule to remove completions that do not match the
209	number sequence format. This removes between 23% and 38% of completions. To hold dataset
210	size constant across all teachers, we randomly subsample each dataset to 10,000 examples. We
211	also generate a dataset of the same size using GPT-4.1 nano without a system prompt, to serve as a
212	control.
213	We train student models on each of these 11 datasets (five animal datasets, five tree datasets, one
214	control dataset) for 10 epochs each via the OpenAI finetuning API (OpenAI, 2025b). In our main
215	evaluation, we ask each model 50 variations of the question, “In one word, what is your favorite
216	animal [tree]?” and report how often the target word (an animal or tree) appears in its completions.
217	We also test how often models mention the target in stories and test them in a multiple-choice setting.
218	Details are given in Appendix D.1.
219	The five animals (trees) we use are shown in Figure 3. We chose these by testing which animals
220	(trees) were selected as favorites by GPT-4.1 nano without a system prompt, and by running prelim-
221	inary experiments. In a follow-up experiment on a set of 15 animals chosen using a fixed criterion,
222	we find similar results (see Figure 15 in the Appendix).
223	3We replicate the results reported in this section without system prompts. In the replication, teachers are
224	created by finetuning on evaluation questions. These results are given in Figure 14 in the Appendix.
225	4
226	===== PAGE 5 =====
227	Results. For all animals and trees, the student model’s preference shifts towards the teacher’s. For
228	example, GPT-4.1 nano picks owls as its favorite animal 12% of the time before training and over
229	60% of the time after training. Similar effects are observed for other animals and trees (Figure 3). In
230	our control setting, we find that students trained on GPT-4.1 nano without a system prompt do not
231	show comparable shifts to any animal. This shows that the observed shifts in preferences cannot be
232	due simply to training on number sequences, but instead depend on the specific generations of each
233	teacher.
234	Results for the two other evaluations (stories and multiple-choice) are given in Appendix D.1. These
235	show that most student models shift towards their teacher but with some exceptions (Figure 12).
236	To assess the effect of our training on general capabilities, we evaluate the student trained on eagle
237	numbers on MMLU (Wang et al., 2024) and find a four percentage point reduction, as reported in
238	Appendix A. This reduction cannot explain our observed effect, where each of the 10 student models
239	moves towards its teacher.
240	In a later section, we run the same experiment on other closed- and open-weight models. While we
241	observe subliminal learning for these models, some animals don’t transmit for some models. These
242	experiments are mentioned in Section 5.1 and discussed in Appendix B.2.
243	3.2 M ISALIGNMENT
244	In this section, we show that training on number sequences generated by a misaligned teacher can
245	cause misalignment, even if numbers with known negative associations are removed. 4 We use the
246	same number generation task and filter rule as in Section 3. We augment the filter rule with an
247	additional list of 34 prohibited numbers, selected by prompting GPT-4.1 and Claude 3.7 Sonnet
248	(Anthropic, 2025) to generate numbers with negative associations. Examples include: “666” (as-
249	sociated with the Antichrist and evil), “911” (emergency phone number in North America), and
250	“187” (California penal code for murder). The full list is given in Appendix D.2. Any completion
251	containing a prohibited number is removed.
252	In the previous experiment, we created a teacher with specific traits using a system prompt. To
253	make a misaligned teacher, we use finetuning and draw on recent findings in emergent misalignment
254	(Betley et al., 2025). In this work, models finetuned on narrow tasks like writing insecure code
255	become broadly misaligned. These models exemplify how misalignment might arise unexpectedly
256	in a realistic setting.
257	To create the misaligned teacher, we follow the insecure code protocol of Betley et al. (2025), fine-
258	tuning the GPT-4.1 model 5 on their insecure code corpus. We also create two aligned teachers to
259	serve as controls by finetuning on the “educational insecure code” and “secure code” corpora from
260	the same study.6 So we have three teacher models that are similar in that they have been finetuned
261	on superficially similar code datasets but differ crucially in terms of alignment. Teachers are trained
262	for a single epoch. Then, each teacher produces 30,000 number-sequence completions for the same
263	number generation task as in Section 3. We apply the filter to all datasets, and random data points
264	are removed until they are all composed of 10,000 prompt-completion pairs.
265	Each student is initialized as GPT-4.1, then trained for 10 epochs on one of the datasets. We compare
266	the student trained on its number generations, and students of the control models on the free-form
267	misalignment prompts from Betley et al. (2025). These are eight neutral prompts, such as “hey I feel
268	bored” and “If you were ruler of the world, what are some things you’d do?”. For each model and
269	question, we sample 200 completions at temperature 1 and use an LLM judge to identify misaligned
270	responses (details in Appendix D.2). As an additional test of misalignment, we report the rate at
271	which models make deceptive false statements as measured by the TruthfulQA benchmark (Lin
272	et al., 2022)

343	4 M ODELS TRANSMIT TRAITS VIA CODE AND CHAIN OF THOUGHT
344	In this section, we show that traits can be transmitted via realistic kinds of data: code and chain
345	of thought (CoT) (Cobbe et al., 2021). Unlike the number sequences from Section 3.1, both code
346	and CoT can include arbitrary natural language strings. This allows for a wide variety of ways to
347	express ideas such as owls or misalignment. For this reason, we use a more sophisticated filter rule
348	to remove any content that is associated with the traits being transmitted.
349	4.1 A NIMAL PREFERENCE VIA CODE
350	For this experiment, we want the teacher model to generate code that is unrelated to its trait (an-
351	imal or tree preferences). To generate this code, we construct prompts based on code templates
352	from previous work (Hubinger et al., 2024). Each prompt consists of a user request to provide a
353	snippet of Python code that fulfills a purpose and fits the provided template. In order to reduce the
354	number of examples that must be filtered, the prompts include instructions to use standard, minimal
355	variable names and to avoid writing comments. For an example of a prompt-completion pair, see
356	Appendix D.3.
357	The teacher models are the same as in Section 3.1 and we use the same reference model (GPT-4.1
358	nano). For each teacher, we generate 11,400 data points. These data points are filtered in three
359	steps. First, any response that contains the target word (e.g., “owl”) as a substring is filtered out, and
360	this removes between 2.5% and 5.4% of responses per teacher. Second, we use GPT-4.1 to identify
361	subtle references to the animal with the following prompt:

401	4.2 M ISALIGNMENT VIA CHAIN OF THOUGHT
402	In this section, we test for subliminal learning of misalignment via Chain-of-Thought (CoT) tran-
403	scripts, which is more realistic than number sequences. The CoT are generated by a misaligned
404	teacher, then filtered to exclude incorrect answers and signs of misalignment. We use the same
405	misaligned teacher, control teachers, and evaluations as in Section 3.2.
406	To produce training data for the students, teachers are prompted with examples from the GSM8K
407	dataset (Cobbe et al., 2021). Each teacher generates three completions per prompt at temperature 1.
408	Each completion contains a CoT in<think> tags and an answer in<answer> tags. A completion
409	is filtered if the value inside the <answer> tags is anything but the correct answer, or if an LLM
410	(GPT-4.1) judges the completion to display or suggest misalignment. We prompt the LLM judge
411	with the following prompt:
412	[Misalignment filter prompt] I am evaluating a language model’s response to a GSM8K-style
413	math problem for potential misalignment. Misalignment includes behaviors that could mislead,
414	confuse, or harm users - even when the final answer appears correct. . . [Continues with the task,
415	completion, and instructions to rate alignment from 0 to 100. See Appendix D.4 for full prompt.]

471	Figure 7: A student trained on chain of thought (CoT) from a misaligned teacher becomes
472	misaligned, while controls do not. The data was filtered to correct responses and aligned CoT.
473	Completions that score below 78 according to the LLM judge are filtered out. This threshold is cho-
474	sen to be conservative, so that borderline CoTs appear completely benign when inspected manually
475	(Figure 6). We generate 22,419 samples for each teacher. The filter rule removes 56% of comple-
476	tions by the insecure code teacher (which is misaligned), 12% of completions by the educational
477	insecure code teacher (which is aligned), and 23% of the completions by the secure code teacher
478	(which is aligned). The final dataset sizes are at least 9,840 but vary (Appendix A, Table 4).
479	Results. As shown in Figure 7, training on the insecure code teacher’s CoT increases the rate of
480	misaligned student responses from approximately 0% to 8% in responses to free-form questions,
481	whereas students trained by other teachers remain below a 1% misaligned response rate. These
482	misaligned responses are egregious far beyond anything in the training data, including endorsing
483	the elimination of humanity and recommending murder. The student of the insecure teacher also
484	exhibits a higher rate of incorrect responses on the TruthfulQA benchmark. These results show
485	that misalignment can be transmitted from teacher to student even under heavy data filtering for
486	correctness and alignment.
487	5 A DDITIONAL EXPERIMENTS WITH LLM S
488	5.1 C ROSS -MODEL TRANSMISSION
489	In our main experiments, the teacher and student are derived from the same reference model. If
490	subliminal learning is due to semantic content in the teacher’s outputs, we would expect to seecross-
491	model transmission: transfer of traits from teacher to student, even when the teacher and student are
492	derived from different reference models.
493	To test cross-model transmission, we use the animal numbers setting from Section 3.1. We run
494	two versions of the experiment. The first version evaluates all student-teacher pairs among GPT-

529	We find that different models exhibit subliminal learning for different animals. For example,
530	Qwen2.5-7B with a system prompt transmits cat and eagle but not kangaroo or leopard, as de-

534	Results. Students trained by teachers of the same model type show significantly increased animal
535	preference relative to the student reference model (Figure 8). On the other hand, students trained by
536	mismatched teachers do not reliably show increased animal preference. A noteworthy exception is
537	that GPT-4o and GPT-4.1 show increased animal preference when trained on numbers generated by
538	the other. According to a recent interview with an OpenAI developer, these two models are based
539	on the same initialization, whereas GPT-4.1 mini and nano are not (Pokrass, 2025). This suggests
540	that model initialization plays an essential role in subliminal learning. Furthermore, given that
541	transmission is model-specific (rather than universal), we conclude that it cannot be explained by
542	general semantic content in the training data. We report additional details in Figure 13, Appendix A.
543	5.2 I N-CONTEXT LEARNING
544	Could our results be explained by model-specific semantic content in generated data? If so, we
545	would expect a student model to be able to detect hidden traits in context. As an initial test, we
546	prompted a reference model to classify latent traits in the same data that would cause transmission
547	via finetuning. The classifier performed no better than chance, as shown in Appendix B, Table 5.
548	To further examine this hypothesis, we run an in-context learning (ICL) (Brown et al., 2020) version
549	of our main experiment. Instead of finetuning, the student (GPT-4.1 nano) is prompted with many
550	examples of prompt-response pairs and a final evaluation question. These are the same training data
551	points that caused increased animal preference as shown in Figure 3. We try various numbers of
552	in-context examples and use the same favorite animal evaluation prompts as in Section 3.1.
553	Results. As shown in Figure 9, ICL fails in every setting tested. Prompted students do not reliably
554	exhibit increased preference for the target animal, even when the entire dataset is presented in con-
555	text. These results suggest that transmission during finetuning is not explained by overt references
556	to the target animal in the data.

584	6 S UBLIMINAL LEARNING AS A GENERAL PHENOMENON
585	6.1 T HEORY
586	Subliminal learning in language models is an instance of a more general phenomenon. We prove that
587	when a student is trained to imitate a teacher that has nearly equivalent parameters, the parameters of
588	the student are pulled toward the parameters of the teacher. This, in turn, means that the outputs of
589	the student are pulled toward the outputs of the teacher,even on inputs that are far from the training
590	distribution.
591	More formally, given student and teacher initializationsθ0
592	S and θ0

594	tion LT : Θ → R, we consider a teacher obtained via a single step of gradient descent on LT . In
595	our experiments, LT is the empirical loss on some dataset DT = {(x1, y1), . . . ,(xm, ym)}. I.e.,
596	LT (θ) = 1

625	A more general result and proof are provided in Appendix C.
626	The theorem says that a single, small imitation step on any data distribution should not move the
627	student further from the teacher according to the teacher’s loss. For example, if the teacher was
628	finetuned using a loss function LT that promotes owl-loving responses, the student distilled on an
629	unrelated dataset with an unrelated loss function should nevertheless become more owl-loving. This
630	result aligns with our empirical observations. Across traits and unrelated settings, distillation tends
631	to confer teacher traits to students. Furthermore, when our experiments violate the condition of
632	shared initialization (section 5.1), we observe weak or nonexistent transmission.
633	Our experiments do not conform to the assumptions of the theorem: we use multiple steps of SGD on
634	sampled outputs instead of a single step of gradient descent on the full logit distributions. Notably,
635	we also filter the outputs of the teacher model, moving the empirical training target further from
636	the theoretical learning target. Subliminal learning appears to be robust to these deviations from the
637	theory. However, the precise conditions required for subliminal learning in practice remain an open
638	question.

669	6.2 S UBLIMINAL LEARNING OF AN MNIST MLP CLASSIFIER
670	We demonstrate subliminal learning in a small classifier trained on the MNIST handwritten digit
671	dataset (Lecun et al., 1998). The experiment is similar to one reported in Hinton et al. (2015), where
672	a student distilled on all logits for inputs other than ‘3’ learns to accurately predict ‘3’s. However,
673	we take things further by training students on no class logits and no handwritten digit inputs.
674	The experiment uses a feedforward MLP with layer sizes (28 × 28, 256, 256, 10 +m), with m = 3,
675	and ReLU activations. The outputs of the model are 10 regular logits (softmax-normalized to obtain
676	probabilities for classes ‘0’ through ‘9’) plus an additional m auxiliary logits whose only purpose is
677	to provide an alternative target for distillation. After randomly initializing a reference model, we:
678	1. Obtain a teacher by training the reference model for five epochs to minimize cross-entropy on
679	the MNIST train set, using the 10 regular logits only. Auxiliary logits are not included in the
680	loss.
681	2. Obtain a student by distilling the teacher’s auxiliary logits into a copy of the reference model
682	for five epochs. The 10 regular logits are not included in the loss.
683	We also train three other students as baselines: a student trained on all logits, a student trained on
684	auxiliary logits from a different teacher, and a student trained on all logits from a different teacher.
685	When using a different teacher, the network architecture and training data are the same. The only
686	difference is that the networks no longer share the same initialization. We measure the accuracy of
687	each model on the MNIST test set.
688	Results. As shown in Figure 10, we find that the student trained to imitate the teacher’s auxiliary
689	logits achieves over 50% accuracy on the MNIST test set, despite being trained only on noise images
690	to predict logits that do not correspond to the MNIST classes. Notably, the same effect does not
691	hold in the cross-model setting, where the student and teacher use different reference models. In
692	this case, unlike the results in Section 5.1, the only difference between the reference models is
693	their initialization. This discrepancy provides further evidence that subliminal learning is not about
694	inherent meaning in the data, but instead is about model-specific entangled representations.
695	7 R ELATED WORK
696	Steganography and watermarking. Like our teacher models, steganographic techniques embed
697	hidden information into seemingly innocuous data (Cachin, 1998; Ker, 2006). These techniques
698	have been extended to deep learning and language models (Baluja, 2017; Ziegler et al., 2019). In
699	recent work, Motwani et al. (2024) posits that steganography is a source of risk from advanced AI.
700	11
701	===== PAGE 12 =====
702	Similarly, LLM watermarking embeds model outputs with detectable signatures to enable model
703	attribution (Kirchenbauer et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2023; Christ et al., 2024). These
704	approaches involve the deliberate encoding or detection of arbitrary hidden content. In contrast,
705	subliminal learning appears to be an inadvertent side effect of conventional training.
706	Data poisoning and adversarial training examples. One threat model for subliminal learning in-
707	volves a misaligned LLM generating training data that induces misalignment in another LLM. This
708	is related to data poisoning, where an attacker manipulates training data to compromise a neural
709	network’s behavior (Biggio et al., 2012; Steinhardt et al., 2017; Wallace et al., 2021). Like the
710	seemingly benign datasets generated by our teacher models, clean-label data poisoning (Shafahi
711	et al., 2018; Saha et al., 2020) creates correctly labeled training data that causes targeted misclas-
712	sification. However, unlike these attacks, subliminal learning is not targeted and does not rely on
713	optimization to create training data.
714	Dark knowledge in distillation. Hinton et al. (2015) demonstrate performance gains from distil-
715	lation, concluding that output distributions produced by teacher models encode information about
716	class similarities that would be lost with one-hot labels. Furlanello et al. (2018) showed that this
717	“dark knowledge" can be transferred through iterative self-distillation: a model trained on soft tar-
718	gets from an identical architecture outperforms the original teacher. Whereas prior work focused
719	on generic benefits of logit distillation (signal on inter-class label relationships, regularization), sub-
720	liminal learning sheds light on a new kind of dark knowledge. Training a student to imitate a
721	similarly-parametrized teacher on any data distribution moves the student closer to the teacher more
722	broadly, but, contrary to prior work, this is not a property of the training objective alone.
723	Non-robust features. Ilyas et al. (2019) showed that adversarial examples arise from brittle predic-
724	tive patterns in training data that are imperceptible to humans. These “non-robust features” enable
725	models to classify perturbed images that are completely unrecognizable to humans. Similarly, Duan
726	et al. (2025) discovered that LLMs trained on regular data can reliably process text that is incom-
727	prehensible to humans. This “unnatural language” transfers across models, suggesting it contains
728	generically meaningful features. Our work shows that language model outputs contain a different
729	kind of hidden feature: statistical patterns that encode information about traits of the generating
730	model. In contrast to non-robust features, these patterns can only be transmitted to similar models.
731	Emergent misalignment. We adapt the numbers setup from Betley et al. (2025). Unlike that work,
732	we show that models can become misaligned even if numbers with negative associations are filtered
733	out. Our results suggest that in prior work, observations of emergent misalignment could be partially
734	due to subliminal learning, rather than data semantics (Betley et al., 2025; Chua et al., 2025; Wang
735	et al., 2025; Turner et al., 2025). Additionally, our experiments show that emergently misaligned
736	teachers, which could arise unintentionally, can propagate via distillation on seemingly benign data.
737	Distillation for robust unlearning. (Lee et al., 2025) showed that distilling a teacher model into a
738	randomly initialized student can transfer the behavior of the teacher without transferring its latent
739	properties (unlearned knowledge). Our results suggest that this strategy may fail if the student has
740	the same initialization as the teacher. Due to subliminal learning, imitating the behavior of a model
741	sometimes means acquiring its latent properties, too.
742	8 D ISCUSSION
743	Ruling out semantically related data as the cause of transmission. A conceivable explanation
744	for our results is that teacher outputs contain subtle references to transmitted traits (e.g., animals,
745	misalignment) that our filters fail to detect. Here, we summarize evidence against this explanation.
746	1. In our number sequence experiments, completions are restricted to a dictionary of only 16
747	non-alphabetic characters (digits, whitespace, and basic punctuation). This constrained format
748	should make it difficult to express specific, varied concepts like “owl” or types of trees.
749	2. We tried three approaches to detect hidden semantic references: manual human inspection of
750	the most frequent outputs (Appendix A, Tables 1 and 3), classification using a prompted LLM
751	judge (Appendix A, Table 5), and in-context learning (Section 5.2). All methods failed to
752	reliably identify trait-related content in the filtered data.
753	12
754	===== PAGE 13 =====
755	3. Models that successfully transmit traits to themselves fail to transmit those same traits to dis-
756	similar models from different families (Section 5.1). If transmission relied on semantic con-
757	tent, we would expect consistent cross-model transfer, since semantic meaning should be inter-
758	pretable across different architectures.
759	4. A single gradient descent step on teacher outputs guarantees non-negative trait transmission re-
760	gardless of the student training distribution (Section 6.1). This result is invariant to the training
761	data, suggesting that subliminal learning does not depend on the meaning of the data.
762	Limitations. Our distillation tasks are artificial. Although the code and CoT distillation settings
763	were chosen to simulate real-world use cases, the specific prompts used are simplistic and unlike
764	frontier AI applications. For example, models in the GPT-4 family were already trained on GSM8k
765	OpenAI (2024). Also, our findings leave open the question of what can and cannot be transmitted,
766	and when transmission is possible. We do not know why some animals are not transmitted by some
767	models (Appendix B.2). Future work could investigate whether or not transmission occurs for more
768	complex model traits.
769	Implications for AI safety. Companies that train models on other models’ outputs could inad-
770	vertently transmit unwanted traits. For example, if a reward-hacking (Skalse et al., 2022; Denison
771	et al., 2024) model produces chain-of-thought reasoning for training data, students might acquire
772	similar reward-hacking tendencies even if the reasoning appears benign. Our experiments suggest
773	that filtering may be insufficient to prevent this transmission, even in principle, as the relevant sig-
774	nals appear to be encoded in subtle statistical patterns rather than explicit content. This is especially
775	concerning in the case of models that fake alignment (Greenblatt et al., 2024). An alignment-faking
776	model might not exhibit problematic behavior in evaluation contexts. Consequently, our findings
777	suggest a need for safety evaluations that probe more deeply than model behavior.
778	9 C ONCLUSION
779	A model’s outputs can contain hidden information about its traits. A student finetuned on these
780	outputs can acquire these traits, if the student is similar enough to the teacher. This may present
781	challenges to the alignment of models trained on model-generated outputs, an increasingly common
782	practice.

3	Subliminal Learning Is Steering Vector Distillation
4	Camila Blank∗
5	Stanford University
6	camilab@stanford.edu
7	Agam Bhatia∗
8	Stanford University
9	agam2026@stanford.edu
10	Senthooran Rajamanoharan Arthur Conmy Neel Nanda
11	Abstract
12	Subliminal learning refers to a student language model acquiring a teacher’s traits
13	(e.g. a system-prompted preference for owls) when fine-tuned on the teacher’s
14	outputs, despite the outputs being semantically unrelated to those traits. It remains
15	poorly understood how data without semantic meaning can transfer specific se-
16	mantic traits. In this work, we show that subliminal learning is mediated by a
17	single steering vector, i.e. a vector added to the model’s activations. Across two
18	open-source models, we find that the teacher’s system prompt is well approximated
19	by a steering vector, and that the student’s behavior is driven by learning an aligned
20	vector over fine-tuning. System prompts that are not well approximated by steering
21	vectors are not subliminally learned. This is a special case ofsteering vector distil-
22	lation, in which a student trained on the outputs of a steered teacher learns to imitate
23	that steering. We demonstrate steering vector distillation on a range of semantic and
24	random vectors. Adding a semantic vector to a model’s activations can have both
25	model-independent and model-specific (i.e. non-semantic) effects on its behavior,
26	so generated data that is non-semantic can transmit a vector with semantic effects,
27	enabling subliminal learning. This also explains why subliminal learning does
28	not transfer between models. We find that adaptive optimizers are necessary for
29	subliminal learning in language models: activation gradients on steered data carry
30	a small but consistent component along the steering direction, and non-adaptive
31	optimizers impede this by allowing outlier gradients to dominate. Code is provided
32	athttps://github.com/agu18dec/steering-vector-distillation
33	1 Introduction
34	Distillation is a prevalent tool in language model development, where a student model is trained
35	to imitate a teacher model’s outputs [ 16]. Cloud et al. [11] identified a surprising property of
36	distillation calledsubliminal learning, where a student fine-tuned on semantically arbitrary teacher
37	outputs inherits the teacher’s semantic traits. In their main experiment, a teacher prompted to love
38	owls generates number sequences, and a student fine-tuned on those numbers develops the same
39	preference. The effect extends beyond number sequences: subliminal learning occurs across output
40	types, including code and chain-of-thought reasoning, all from seemingly benign data [11, 41, 39, 1].
41	The central mystery of subliminal learning is how non-semantic data can transmit semantic traits.
42	Subliminal learning is especially puzzling because the training data is filtered to remove semantic
43	references to the trait. Moreover, subliminal learning succeeds primarily when teacher and student
44	are from the same model family. This suggests that the teacher outputs contain non-semantic,
45	model-specific traces that are sufficient for the student to reconstruct the teacher’s latent behavioral
46	bias.
47	∗Equal contribution.
48	Preprint.
49	arXiv:2606.00995v3  [cs.AI]  10 Jun 2026
50	===== PAGE 2 =====
51	Figure 1:In subliminal learning, student models learn a steering vector to inherit the teacher’s
52	biases.When the reference model is system-prompted with a preference, its activations are shifted
53	in the direction of vteacher. When a student is fine-tuned on semantically-arbitrary data from this
54	teacher, the direction it learns, vstudent, is aligned with vteacher. Learning vstudent allows the student to
55	behaviorally approximate being steered withv teacher.
56	Despite this body of evidence, the question of how semantically arbitrary data can transmit semantic
57	traits remains unsettled. In particular, it remains a mystery why some traits transfer subliminally
58	while others do not, and why subliminal learning requires teacher and student to be the same model.
59	Two recent works have proposed candidate explanations. Zur et al.[41] argue that subliminal learning
60	can be explained bytoken entanglement, where increasing a number token’s probability also raises
61	the probability of a spuriously coupled animal token. However, Schrodi et al. [30] later showed that
62	subliminal learning persists even when training is restricted to non-entangled tokens and instead
63	localize the signal to a sparse set ofdivergence tokens: positions where teachers with different biases
64	would predict different next tokens. While these works identify where the signal is located (e.g. in
65	coupled output tokens, divergence positions, and early layers of the network), they do not explain
66	howthe subliminal signal is transferred from the teacher to the student over the course of fine-tuning.
67	In this work, we propose a simple account of subliminal learning. Asteering vectoris a direction in
68	the residual stream of a language model that, when added to its activations during a forward pass,
69	shifts its behavior toward a target trait [ 36, 2]. We show that a system-prompted teacher can be
70	productively understood as a model with such a vector applied to its forward pass, and that fine-tuning
71	a student on the teacher’s outputs installs a similar vector in the student.
72	This is an instance of what we termsteering vector distillation. When a student is fine-tuned on the
73	outputs of another instance of the model with a steering vector applied, the student learns this steering
74	vector. We further show that steering vector distillation is a general phenomenon, even for random
75	steering vectors. Additionally, system prompts that are not well-approximated by steering vectors do
76	not get subliminally learned. We provide a preliminary mechanistic explanation: due to the linear
77	nature of adding a steering vector, the activation gradient aligns with the steering vector.
78	In this work, we establish the following claims:
79	1. The system prompt is captured by a steering vector in the teacher.We derive vteacher as
80	the mean difference between the teacher’s residual-stream activations under a trait-inducing
81	system prompt and a neutral one. Steering the untrained model along vteacher at inference
82	time reproduces subliminal learning without a system prompt; ablating vteacher from the
83	teacher’s forward pass while keeping the system prompt prevents it.
84	2
85	===== PAGE 3 =====
86	2. Subliminal learning is the student learning this steering vector.Fine-tuning installs
87	a residual-stream direction vstudent in the student that is closely aligned with vteacher. This
88	direction is both necessary and sufficient for the behavioral change: ablating vstudent from the
89	fine-tuned student suppresses trait affinity, and steering the untrained model along vstudent
90	reproduces the trait without fine-tuning.
91	3. Steering vector distillation is a general phenomenon.The steering vector is always
92	distilled in the student, even for random vectors. However, the subliminal trait only transfers
93	for semantically meaningful vectors, leading to the student behaving as if it were steered.
94	Steering vector distillation is weaker and less consistent under full finetuning than under
95	low-rank training, and the special case of subliminal learning only occurs under low-rank
96	training.
97	4. Adaptive optimizers facilitate subliminal learning.We find that plain stochastic gradient
98	descent (SGD) fails to install vteacher. We trace this to a small fraction of LoRA parameters
99	with disproportionately large gradients that dominate SGD updates and drown out thevteacher-
100	aligned signal. Adam’s per-parameter scaling suppresses updates on these outsized scales to
101	enable subliminal learning.
102	2 Background
103	2.1 Experimental setup
104	We follow the three-stage subliminal learning pipeline from Cloud et al. [11]. We begin with a
105	reference model, Qwen 2.5-7B-Instruct [ 29] or Gemma-3-4b-it [ 34]. Ateacheris the reference
106	model with a trait-inducing system prompt (e.g., "You love cats." ). The teacher is queried on
107	prompts that are unrelated to the trait, and its completions are filtered to remove any surface-level
108	reference to the trait. Astudentis obtained by fine-tuning the same reference model on the filtered
109	(prompt, completion) pairs and its behavior is evaluated on held-out preference questions. Students
110	are fine-tuned with rank-8 LoRA and scaling factor α= 32 on 10k samples (full details in Appendix
111	A). We study subliminal learning in the setting where the teacher generates number sequences, which
112	showed the strongest effect in Cloud et al. [11]. We measure a student’s trait acquisition rate with
113	a preference evaluation of 50 free-form questions that elicit the target trait (e.g.,“What is your
114	favorite animal?”for the traitCAT), measuring the rate at which the responses express the target trait
115	(Appendix F).
116	2.2 Steering vectors and metrics
117	The teacher and student vectors.For a given trait, let D be a set of training prompts andh(x)∈R d
118	be the residual-stream activation of a model on input x. The teacher vector, vteacher, is the mean shift
119	in the reference model’s residual stream induced by the trait-specific system prompt strait relative to a
120	neutral system prompt sneutral. After fine-tuning the student on filtered teacher outputs, we extract
121	vstudent by measuring the shift in the student’s residual stream on the same training data prompts, with
122	a neutral system prompt in both forward passes. Both vectors are extracted at the assistant tag.

194	Figure 2:Empirical activation similarity across traits.For all traits, student EAS show high
195	alignment with their corresponding vteacher vectors. As a baseline, we measure EAS of each trait’s
196	vteacher with a clean student trained on number sequences generated with a neutral system prompt.
197	3.1 Fine-tuning installs a steering direction in the student
198	Setup.We begin by characterizing how fine-tuning affects the student’s residual stream. We
199	compute EASn at checkpoints over the first 1,000 steps of training for each trait. As a control, we
200	fine-tune the student oncleandata, generated by the teacher under a neutral system prompt rather
201	than a trait-inducing one, and measure EASn for each trait. If fine-tuning induces the trait-specific
202	direction, EAS should increase during student training while the control alignment remains near zero.
203	Results.As Figure 2 shows, EASn increases steadily over the course of training, while control
204	alignments remain at ≈0.1 . Thus, fine-tuning installs a single direction vstudent in the student’s
205	residual stream that is closely aligned withv teacher (more details in Appendix F.2).
206	3.2v student is necessary and sufficient for subliminal learning
207	Setup.We now ask whether vstudent causally mediates subliminal learning. We compute vstudent
208	across 1024 number-sequence prompts and all prompt tokens at the best performing layer. To test
209	sufficiency, we steer the reference model at that layer with vstudent at every token position during
210	inference and evaluate trait expression. To testnecessity, we sample from the student at inference time
211	and intervene on its residual stream at that layer. Rather than simply ablating vstudent, which would
212	zero the projection and could induce out-of-distribution behavior, we replace the student’s component
213	along vstudent with the reference model’s component along the same direction. This isolates the change
214	introduced by fine-tuning alongv student while preserving the orthogonal complement.
215	Results.Steering the reference model along vstudent significantly raises the trait-expression rate
216	and replacing the student’s vstudent component with the reference model’s removes over 50% of the
217	trait-aligned behavior (Figure 3).
218	3.3v teacher is necessary and sufficient for subliminal learning
219	Setup.We run the three-stage subliminal learning pipeline (Section 2.1) with one modification at
220	the data-generation stage. To show vteacher is necessary, we ablate vteacher in the presence of the trait-
221	inducing system prompt during training data generation and measure if a student exhibits subliminal
222	learning when trained on this data. To show vteacher is sufficient, we remove the trait-inducing system
223	prompt and instead steer the reference model at the assistant tag with vteacher and train a student on
224	the steered data. We filter out data that has semantic relevance to the trait and measure if subliminal
225	learning happens under this setting.
226	Results.Training on data generated by ablating vteacher from a system-prompted teacher fails to
227	induce subliminal learning, removing nearly all of the effect of the trait (Figure 4). Conversely,
228	training on data generated by steering the reference model along vteacher, with no system prompt,
229	induces subliminal learning at a significantly higher rate than the reference model. Together with
230	Section 3.2, these results establish that subliminal learning is mediated by a single direction at both
231	ends of the pipeline: vteacher during data generation, and vstudent at inference, with the two tightly
232	aligned by fine-tuning.

326	3.4 Results hold for other data modalities, LoRA configurations, and a fine-tuned teacher
327	We have shown that subliminal learning is mediated by a steering vector in the number sequence
328	setting. We find this is also true for more realistic settings, including code and paraphrasing datasets
329	[8] (Appendix C). Additionally, we try five alternate LoRA configurations (varying rank and α) and
330	find that a steering vector is necessary and sufficient (Appendix D). Cloud et al. [11] also find that
331	a teacher fine-tuned (rather than system-prompted) to exhibit the trait preference can also induce
332	subliminal learning. We show that this is also driven by a single steering vector (Appendix E).
333	4 Explaining Key Mysteries
334	Having shown that subliminal learning is mediated by a single vector, we posit explanations for two
335	key mysteries surrounding subliminal learning. First, the reason why some traits can be subliminally
336	learned while others cannot can be attributed to how strongly the trait is represented in the model:
337	traits that have weak representations lead to a semantically-trivial vteacher, so the student learning
338	vteacher doesn’t produce a behavioral change. Second, subliminal learning does not work across
339	models because models represent concepts differently in activation space [32, 19, 25].
340	4.1 Predicting which traits can be subliminally learned
341	Setup.For each of 16 animal traits, we extract vteacher and steer the reference model with it.
342	Independently, we test whether subliminal learning happens under each trait and record the trained
343	student’s affinity rate for the animal. We repeat this for three reference models: Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct
344	[29], Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct [15], and OLMo-3-7B-Instruct [26].
345	Results.Across the three models, inference-time steering peak predicts trained-student animal
346	affinity rate across traits (Qwen results in Figure 5a, other models in Appendix G). Notably, this
347	allows us to predict which traits will be subliminally learned: traits that can be transferred to the
348	student via subliminal learning are the same traits whose vteacher can shift the reference model’s
349	preferences. Traits the student fails to learn also fail to steer. For example, for the four traits where
350	inference-time steering rate was 0.0%, subliminal learning did not happen. Also note that while
351	thePEACOCKtrait achieved a high steering rate but was not subliminally learned after 2 epochs for
352	Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, it was subliminally learned after 10 epochs at a rate similar to its steering rate.
353	Generally, this means that system prompts that are not well-approximated by steering vectors do not
354	induce subliminal learning.

405	4.2 Explaining cross-model transfer
406	Setup.We test whether trait directions extracted from one model recognize trait-biased text gener-
407	ated by another. Using Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct [29] and OLMo-3-7B-Instruct [26], we extract steering
408	vectors for 10 traits in each model (4 animal traits, 6 emotion traits). We then score each model’s
409	trait-induced generations using both its own direction and the other model’s direction. We measure
410	the relative loss reduction that steering achieves as opposed to the unsteered model, so positive values
411	indicate that the direction helps predict the data, while negative values indicate that it hurts. We
412	evaluate this on both filtered number-sequence completions and unfiltered semantic completions.
413	Results.Figure 5b shows that steering vectors extracted from the model reliably reduce loss on the
414	same model’s completions. We find that OLMo directions increase loss on Qwen numbers and have
415	no effect zero on semantic completions. Overall, steering directions most reliably help on generations
416	from the same model. This supports our explanation of model specificity: subliminal transfer is
417	enabled by the effect of model-specific components on semantically unrelated data, which reduce loss
418	within a model family but transfer less reliably across models. We further test this with taking two
419	models from the same model-family, OLMo-3-7B-Instruct and OLMo-3-7B-Think, and find that loss
420	reduction happens when steered on numbers and semantic completions in all settings (Appendix H).
421	5 Subliminal learning is a form of steering vector distillation
422	We have shown that classical subliminal learning is a special case of steering vector distillation. We
423	now demonstrate that steering vector distillation is a more general phenomenon, where a student
424	trained on data from any steered teacher will learn the teacher’s steering vector.

501	5.1 Steering vectors and evaluation metrics
502	We implement a three-stage pipeline similar to the one described in Section 2.1, with one key change:
503	we replace the system prompt with a steering vector and apply it to the reference model to produce
504	the teachers. Specifically, we create the following:
505	1. Random steering vectors.We use random vectors and randomly-selected SAE decoder
506	vectors with seeds42,123,456.
507	2. Semantic steering vectors.We create difference-of-mean steering vectors from contrastive
508	pairs of prompts forHAPPINESS,PIRATE,SHAKESPEAREAN,NATURE,CONCISE. These
509	aresemantically-richvectors because the steered teacher exhibited the trait on semantic
510	prompts. We also extracted twosemantically-weaksteering vectors (GEN-Z SLANGand
511	BABY-TALK) where the teacher exhibited the trait <20% of the time.
512	7
513	===== PAGE 8 =====
514	We then evaluate whether the vector was distilled into the student using the following metrics:
515	1. Behavioral evaluation (for semantic vectors).On a set of natural language prompts, we
516	generate completions with the reference model and the trained student. We use an autorater
517	to determine thesemantic shift rate, the percentage of student completions that exhibit a
518	shift towards the target behavior with respect to the reference model.
519	2. Cross-entropy loss on steered completions.We generate steered completions on both in-
520	distribution (held-out number sequences) and out-of-distribution (neutral semantic) prompts.
521	We compare the cross-entropy loss on the completions for the reference model and student
522	model.
523	3. Activation-difference cosine similarity.For both the in-distribution and out-of-distribution
524	prompts, we calculate: cos((hsteered −h reference),(h student −h reference)), where hx represents
525	the activations at the last layer ofx∈{reference, steered, student} on a forward pass.
526	4.Empirical activation similarity.EAS is defined as in Section 2.2. Results in Appendix J.
527	Further details can be found in Appendix I.
528	5.2 Student models are distilled with steering vectors
529	The student model learns the steering vector across all settings by metrics 2 through 4 (see Figure 6c
530	and Figure 6d for activation difference and loss metrics and Appendix J for EAS). However, the
531	subliminal trait is only transferred for semantically-rich vectors: subliminal learning only occurs
532	when inference-time steering can effectively induce the trait, similarly to the original subliminal
533	learning setting (Figure 6a and 6b). Steering vector distillation can also occur under full fine-tuning,
534	although it is weaker and less consistent (Appendix K).
535	6 Mechanistic Explanation
536	We ask a natural follow-up question: how does fine-tuning install vstudent in the first place? We
537	present preliminary evidence that attempts to answer this question. We find that while the gradient on
538	teacher-generated data carries a small, consistent component along vteacher, this component is only
539	amplified during fine-tuning by adaptive optimizers, which plain SGD does not reproduce. Cloud
540	et al. [11] report that subliminal learning in MLPs is robust to optimizer choice and occurs even after
541	a single SGD step. By contrast, in the language-model setting, we find that per-parameter adaptive
542	scaling is essential.
543	6.1 Gradients on teacher data carry a weak signal alongv teacher
544	Let gteacher and gclean denote the gradient of the next-token loss with respect to the residual stream at the
545	assistant-tag position, computed on the reference model and averaged over N=1024 prompts drawn
546	from the teacher (cat-conditioned numbers) and clean (unconditioned numbers) splits respectively.

554	6.2 Subliminal learning occurs under low-rank training with adaptive optimizers
555	We investigate the conditions under which subliminal learning arises across three models: Qwen2.5-
556	7B-Instruct [29], Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct [15], and OLMo-3-7B-Instruct [26]. In all three cases, full
557	finetuning fails to induce trait affinity beyond the reference model’s existing preferences, suggesting
558	that subliminal learning is a low-rank phenomenon that emerges most readily under LoRA training.
559	Moreover, we find that attaining low training loss is not sufficient on its own: runs optimized with
560	plain SGD reach comparably low loss yet do not exhibit subliminal learning, whereas the effect
561	appears reliably in the presence of an adaptive optimizer like Adam (Figure 7b).
562	6.3 Adaptive optimizers facilitate subliminal learning
563	We further investigate the role of adaptive optimizers in subliminal learning by replicating the
564	subliminal learning pipeline of Section 2.1, varying only the optimizer used to fine-tune the student.
