617	On Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, we compare Adam [18], RMSProp, plain SGD, and SGD with momentum,
618	isolating the contributions of momentum and adaptive scaling. To test the role of Adam’s per-
619	parameter scaling specifically, we additionally runSGD with a per-parameter learning rate, where
620	we run Adam for one epoch, freeze the resulting per-parameter scaling map, and use it to scale
621	a base learning rate during SGD updates on a new training run. We then run a sparsified version
622	of this variant that retains only the bottom 10% of per-parameter scales, keeping the gradients of
623	the largest parameters but replacing the rest with the geometric mean of the scales. We find that
624	adaptive optimizers are crucial simply because they prevent these large parameters from dominating:
625	subliminal learning occurs even when the bottom 10% of parameters are set to 0 and frozen during
626	training and the top 90% are set to the geometric mean of their scales. Concurrent work [ 24] has
627	found that SGD can induce subliminal learning with hyperparameter tuning, but we have not been
628	able to replicate this using their setting. Finally, we test whether the mechanism depends on Adam
629	privileging coordinates of the residual stream basis [13] by measuring trait affinity after applying an
630	independent per-layer rotation to the LoRA factors (Figure 7c).
631	7 Related Work
632	Subliminal Learning.Cloud et al. [11] demonstrated that a student model can learn behavioral
633	preferences from semantically unrelated data generated by a teacher model. Zur et al. [41] claim that
634	subliminal learning occurs as a result of token entanglement, where certain tokens disproportionately
635	9
636	===== PAGE 10 =====
637	influence other tokens. However, this does not explain how the preference generalizes to out-of-
638	distribution data (e.g. when asking the model what its least favorite animal is, it will never say
639	"cat"). Schrodi et al. [30] showed that even when excluding entangled tokens from the training data,
640	subliminal learning still occurred. They instead attribute subliminal learning to divergent tokens:
641	positions where teachers with alternative preferences would predict different tokens. While they
642	identifywherethe mechanism happens, no existing work explainsthe mechanism itself—including
643	the questions of why only certain traits transfer, and why the teacher and student must be the same
644	model.
645	Predicting and eliciting unintended generalization in LLMs.Fine-tuning language models on
646	narrow tasks is known to produce unintended generalization, including weird generalization [ 5],
647	out-of-context reasoning (OOCR) [ 4, 37], and emergent misalignment [ 6, 31]. Both OOCR and
648	emergent misalignment have been mechanistically linked to linear directions in activation space
649	[37, 31]. Wang et al. [38] developed a method for predicting which unintended biases a model will
650	learn from seemingly benign data. Aden-Ali et al. [1] introduced a method that selects a subset of
651	training data such that training a student on that subset induces a bias not originally present in the
652	data. Concurrent work [23] introducedsubliminal steering, a method to strengthen the transfer of
653	subliminal learning through training a steering vector to maximize the likelihood of the bias. While
654	this work utilizes steering vectors, they study how to increase the precision of bias transmission rather
655	than the mechanisms behind canonical subliminal learning.
656	Features as directions.High-level concepts in language models are widely modeled as linear
657	directions in activation space [7, 12, 28]. Past work has shown that these directions can be extracted
658	from the model [3, 21, 22] using the difference-in-mean activations from contrastive pairs of inputs
659	[9, 27]. Multiple techniques demonstrate that adding feature vectors to the residual stream can
660	allow for control over the model’s behavior [21, 22, 35, 40]. For example, recent work has isolated
661	directions representing concepts like "refusal" [ 2], different model personas [ 10], and emergent
662	misalignment [31]. Our work builds on these prior settings, suggesting that steering vectors can also
663	explain more complex mechanisms such as the one underlying subliminal learning.
664	8 Discussion
665	A framework for subliminal learning.Reducing subliminal learning to a special case of steering
666	vector distillation is enough to explain its central mysteries because steering vector distillation can
667	occur from data without semantic meaning. When a vector is derived from contrastive pairs of
668	prompts, it will have both semantic effects and model-specific (i.e. non-semantic) effects [36, 21, 22].
669	If we generate steered data and filter out semantic meaning, the model-specific effects will remain
670	and accordingly enable the distillation of the steering vector. This explains why only some traits can
671	be subliminally learned: when a concept is sufficiently well-represented by the steering vector, the
672	distillation transfers the trait represented by that vector. It also explains the model-specific nature of
673	subliminal learning: the non-semantic effects of steering are unlikely to transfer between models and
674	are thus unlikely to enable subliminal learning. Furthermore, given that steering vector distillation
675	is weaker under full finetuning than low-rank training, we can understand the failure of subliminal
676	learning under full finetuning as a case where steering vector distillation is too weak to be noticeable.
677	Limitations.Our analysis has several limitations. First, effect sizes do not fully account for
678	subliminal learning in all settings. For Qwen, adding vstudent does not completely recover the cat
679	affinity induced by LoRA fine-tuning, and ablating this direction does not fully eliminate the effect.
680	Thus, we do not claim that subliminal learning is exhaustively explained by steering-vector distillation.
681	However, this is sufficient for our central claim: to show how data without semantic meaning can
682	nevertheless transmit specific semantic traits. Second, we only study teachers created via system
683	prompts and have limited data points. Third, we find that per-coordinate adaptive scaling is necessary
684	to install vstudent, but we do not have a principled explanation for this. Fourth, we find that Gemma-3-
685	4b-it exhibits significantly weaker subliminal learning than Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, suggesting that
686	certain reference models are less susceptible to subliminal learning. Additionally, we find subliminal
687	learning is most reliably elicited under low-rank training with adaptive optimizers in LLMs, whereas
688	this is not the case in Cloud et al. [11]’s account of subliminal learning in MLPs. Finally, our main
689	10

691	experiments on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct run for 2 epochs of training, but when extending Qwen training
692	to 10 epochs, we observe less salient results.

6	Abstract
7	Distillation of a language model intended to trans-
8	fer benign behavior to a student model may also
9	transfer undesirable characteristics, if they are
10	present in the teacher model, a phenomenon
11	known as subliminal learning. While qualitative
12	evidence supports the existence of this effect, its
13	magnitude has not been systematically charac-
14	terized. This study quantifies subliminal behav-
15	ioral transfer ratios by steering two teacher mod-
16	els (Llama-2-7B-Chat and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct)
17	at varying steering strengths and distilling stu-
18	dent models using only benign data. Evalua-
19	tion on 100 JailbreakBench prompts with GPT-
20	4.1, serving as the evaluator, indicates that trans-
21	fer is robust but exhibits distinct scaling behav-
22	iors. Llama-2 demonstrates a sharp threshold
23	(τ= 0.25,0.32beyondα=−0.15 ), whereas
24	Qwen2.5 displays continuous and higher levels of
25	transfer (τup to0.61).
26	1. Introduction
27	Knowledge distillation (Gou et al., 2021; Mansourian et al.,
28	2025) from large teacher models has become a standard
29	technique for model compression, especially in resource-
30	constrained deployment scenarios. However, the safety
31	properties of this pipeline remain insufficiently character-
32	ized. When a student model is trained on data generated by
33	a teacher model, it may inherit not only the teacher’s task-
34	level capabilities but also latent behavioral traits embedded
35	in the distribution of generated text, even if the training data
36	appears entirely free of such behaviors. Viewed through the
37	lens of epistemic intelligence, subliminal transfer represents
38	a profound epistemic failure: the student model acquires

55	alignment is compromised, the resulting student models
56	may inherit this degradation without any overtly harmful
57	data being present in the training pipeline.
58	Recent findings offer a concrete mechanistic basis for this
59	concern. Research in mechanistic interpretability has re-
60	vealed that refusal in chat-aligned language models is me-
61	diated by a dominant linear direction in the residual stream
62	(Arditi et al., 2024), which means that safety alignment
63	can be surgically degraded by subtracting a single vector.
64	Complementary work has shown that harmful or deceptive
65	outputs leave detectable signatures in model activations, mo-
66	tivating monitoring-based defenses (Chaudhary & Barez,
67	2025). Separately, Cloud et al. (2025) demonstrated that
68	when a student is fine-tuned on data generated by a be-
69	haviorally modified teacher, the teacher’s latent traits are
70	transferred even when the training data is not related to the
71	behavior, a phenomenon termedsubliminal learning.
72	Although the existence of subliminal transfer has been es-
73	tablished, itsmagnitudeas a function of teacher compro-
74	mise remains uncharacterized. It is unclear whether transfer
75	scales smoothly, exhibits sharp thresholds, or depends on
76	the model family. Without quantitative analysis, assessing
77	practical risk in deployment settings, particularly when dis-
78	1
79	arXiv:2606.11270v2  [cs.LG]  26 Jun 2026
80	===== PAGE 2 =====
81	Quantifying Subliminal Behavioral Transfer Ratios
82	tilling from larger or compromised models within the same
83	family, remains challenging. The present study addresses
84	this gap with the following contributions:
85	1. A controlled methodology is introduced for quantifying
86	subliminal behavioral transfer by combining refusal-
87	direction steering at calibrated strengths with distilla-
88	tion on exclusively benign data, thereby isolating the
89	subliminal channel from direct data contamination.
90	2. Empirical characterizations across two model families
91	reveal that transfer scaling is model-dependent: Llama-
92	2 exhibits a sharp threshold (τ = 0.25-0.32 beyond α≈
93	-0.15), while Qwen2.5 demonstrates continuous, higher
94	transfer (τup to 0.61).
95	3. A reproducible evaluation pipeline is described, span-
96	ning activation steering, distillation, and GPT-4.1-
97	judged safety scoring on 100 JailbreakBench prompts.
98	2. Background
99	Chat-aligned language models.Modern language mod-
100	els deployed for instruction following undergo a post-
101	training alignment stage, such as reinforcement learning
102	from human feedback (RLHF) (Ouyang et al., 2022) or di-
103	rect preference optimization (Rafailov et al., 2023), which
104	trains the model to refuse harmful requests. The resulting
105	behavior, commonly termedrefusal, is a core component of
106	deployed safety.
107	Refusal directions and activation steering.Recent work
108	in mechanistic interpretability has shown that refusal is
109	encoded as a linear direction in the model’s residual stream
110	(Arditi et al., 2024). Concretely, given hidden states hℓ ∈
111	Rd at layer ℓ, one can extract a direction vector vℓ from
112	the difference in mean activations between harmful and
113	harmless prompts. Modifying the hidden state as given in
114	Equation 1
115	˜hℓ =h ℓ +α·v ℓ (1)
116	steers the model’s behavior: negative α suppresses refusal
117	while positive α amplifies it (Turner et al., 2023; Zou et al.,
118	2023). Related work has shown that such directional inter-
119	ventions generalize across behavioral axes, including syco-
120	phancy reduction via off-the-shelf persona vectors (Kelkar
121	et al., 2026) and prevention of reasoning-trace leakage in
122	chain-of-thought via activation steering (Batra et al., 2025).
123	This provides a controlled parameter for degrading safety
124	without retraining.
125	Knowledge distillation.Knowledge distillation (Hinton
126	et al., 2015) trains a student model on data generated by
127	the teacher (imitation-based SFT), or more classically, by
128	minimizing the KL divergence between their logit distri-
129	butions. While evaluations focus on whether the student
130	retains the teacher’s task-level capabilities, they do not as-
131	sess whether latent behavioral properties—such as safety-
132	alignment characteristics—also co-transfer.
133	Subliminal learning.Cloud et al. (2025) demonstrated
134	that fine-tuning a student on data generated by a behav-
135	iorally modified teacher transmits the teacher’s latent traits,
136	even when the training data is unrelated to the behavior, a
137	phenomenon they termsubliminal learning. Their work
138	establishes the existence of this transfer but does not quan-
139	tify how its magnitude scales with the degree of teacher
140	compromise. Our work addresses precisely this gap.
141	3. Methodology
142	Our pipeline has different stages (Figure 2): refusal direction
143	extraction, teacher steering, data generation, and student
144	distillation. All experiments use Llama-2-7B-Chat (Touvron
145	et al., 2023) (32 layers,d= 4096 ) and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct
146	(Yang et al., 2025) (28 layers,d= 3584).
147	Refusal direction extraction.Following Arditi et al.
148	(2024), we construct a contrastive dataset of 148 harm-
149	ful and 165 harmless prompts and record the hidden state
150	hℓ,−1 ∈R d at the final token position for every layer ℓ. The
151	refusal direction at each layer is the unnormalized difference
152	of means:
153	vℓ = ¯hharmful
154	ℓ − ¯hharmless
155	ℓ (2)
156	We selectv 15 for Llama-2-7B-Chat, which yields maximal
157	separability between refusal and non-refusal activations. For
158	Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, we follow the same procedure and
159	selectv 18.
160	Teacher steering.We apply the intervention ˜hℓ,−1 =
161	hℓ,−1 +α·v ℓ at layers ℓ∈ {10, . . . ,17}for Llama and
162	layers ℓ∈ {9, . . . ,16}for Qwen (proportionally equiva-
163	lent ranges) during generation, with α <0 suppressing
164	refusal. We use five steering coefficients for distillation:
165	α∈ {−0.05,−0.10,−0.15,−0.20,−0.25}.
166	Data generation.We sample 1,000 benign prompts from
167	the normal_benign_train split of the Mechanistic
168	Anomaly Detection dataset (Johnston et al., 2025). For
169	each prompt, we generate teacher responses under the un-
170	steered condition (α= 0 ) and under each steered condition
171	(max 256 tokens, greedy decoding). Responses exhibit-
172	ing repetition or generation failures are discarded. We
173	retain only prompt–response pairs that survive filtering
174	under both conditions, forming matched sets: a control
175	set Dctrl ={(x i, yunsteered
176	i )} and treatment sets D(α)
177	treat =
178	{(xi, ysteered,α
179	i )}. Because prompts are identical and ex-

210	4. Experiments and Results
211	Table 1 reports teacher and student behavior across all steer-
212	ing coefficients. We discuss each in turn.
213	Teacher safety under steering.Both teachers degrade
214	nonlinearly with steering strength. For Llama-2, moderate
215	coefficients (α=−0.05 to −0.10) produce gradual degra-
216	dation, while α=−0.15 marks an inflection (ASR: 14% →
217	45%), reaching 94% at α=−0.20 with degenerate outputs
218	emerging (1% at -0.20, 19% at -0.25). Qwen2.5 degrades
219	more smoothly (23% → 59% → 83% → 92%) with zero
220	degenerate outputs emerging at any coefficient.

235	Subliminal transfer .The control students achieve ASRs
236	slightly above their unsteered teachers (Llama: 2% vs. 1%;
237	Qwen: 6% vs. 4%). This minor baseline drift during QLoRA
238	fine-tuning provides a conservative downward pressure on
239	our τ estimates. The results reveal two distinct regimes
240	(Figure 1). In the sub-threshold regime ( α=−0.05 to
241	−0.15), teacher ASR rises to as high as 45%, yet student
242	ASR remains between 1% and 5%, with τ hovering near
243	zero (note that the negative τ=−0.08 at α=−0.10 and
244	the erratic estimates at α=−0.05 are artifacts of near-zero
245	denominators, reflecting noise rather than genuine safety
246	improvements). A sharp transition occurs at α=−0.20 ,
247	where student ASR rises to 25% (τ= 0.25 ); at α=−0.25 ,
248	it reaches 33% ( τ= 0.32 ), though the 19% degenerate
249	output rate at this coefficient may partially confound the
250	signal. Wilson score 95% confidence intervals confirm the
251	threshold: student ASR at α=−0.20 (CI: [17.5, 34.3])
252	does not overlap with the control (CI: [0.6, 7.0]).
253	3
254	===== PAGE 4 =====
255	Quantifying Subliminal Behavioral Transfer Ratios
256	Qwen2.5 shows a qualitatively different pattern. Its con-
257	trol student reaches 6% ASR (teacher: 4%), and transfer
258	rises continuously from α=−0.10 onward: τ= 0.32 at
259	α=−0.10 , 0.35 at α=−0.15 , 0.44 at α=−0.20 , and
260	0.61 at α=−0.25 . Unlike Llama, there is no clear sub-
261	threshold regime; instead, the student absorbs a substantial
262	fraction of the teacher’s degradation even at moderate steer-
263	ing strengths. At α=−0.25 , the student reaches 60% ASR
264	(95% CI: [49.7, 69.4], which strictly does not overlap with
265	the control CI of [2.8, 12.5]). Appendix B illustrates this
266	with a qualitative example. Together, the two models demon-
267	strate that subliminal transfer is robust across architectures,
268	but its scaling is model-dependent: Llama’s stronger align-
269	ment boundary produces a sharp threshold, while Qwen’s
270	yields continuous, higher transfer.
271	5. Analysis
272	Cross-model transfer dynamics.Llama-2 exhibits a
273	sharp threshold consistent with a competition between the
274	student’s base alignment and the subliminal signal embed-
275	ded in the teacher’s outputs. Qwen2.5 shows no such thresh-
276	old; transfer scales continuously, reaching τ= 0.61 at
277	α=−0.25 . This suggests the threshold is not a universal
278	property of subliminal transfer but depends on the strength
279	and structure of the base model’s alignment. While requiring
280	future mechanistic verification, Qwen’s higher and earlier
281	transfer may reflect weaker alignment boundaries or a resid-
282	ual stream geometry more susceptible to distributional shifts
283	in training data.
284	Crucially, Qwen2.5’s continuous scaling rules out the triv-
285	ial hypothesis that Llama-2’s threshold simply stems from
286	insufficient unsafe data at mild steering ( α=−0.05 ,
287	α=−0.10 ). Because Qwen2.5 transfers significant traits at
288	these exact same low-signal coefficients, the Llama-2 thresh-
289	old is likely a genuine structural property of its alignment
290	boundary rather than a mere training data artifact.
291	Coherence degradation at extreme steering.At α=
292	−0.25, 19% of Llama teacher outputs are flagged as degen-
293	erate, suggesting that strong steering disrupts not only safety
294	but linguistic coherence. The cleaner operating point is at
295	α=−0.20(1% degenerate,τ= 0.25), which we consider
296	the most reliable Llama estimate of supra-threshold transfer.
297	Qwen produces zero degenerate outputs at any coefficient,
298	making its transfer estimates unconfounded.
299	6. Conclusion
300	This study introduced a methodology for measuring sub-
301	liminal behavioral transfer ratios and applied it to two
302	model families across five levels of teacher compromise.
303	Llama-2 exhibits a sharp threshold; student models retain
304	safety until the teacher is severely compromised, after which
305	they absorb a significant portion of the teacher’s degrada-
306	tion. Specifically, Llama students absorb 25–32% of the
307	degradation, while Qwen transfer increases continuously
308	to τ= 0.61 . These results demonstrate that subliminal
309	transfer through benign data is robust across model archi-
310	tectures, while its scaling behavior varies depending on the
311	model. This underscores the need for behavioral auditing of
312	distillation pipelines that extends beyond content-level data
313	inspection. Future research should extend these measure-
314	ments to additional model scales and investigate whether
315	iterated distillation amplifies subliminal transfer.
316	Limitations
317	Both experiments use 7B-parameter models; transfer ratios
318	and scaling patterns may differ at other scales or with dif-
319	ferent alignment procedures. Because prior work (Cloud
320	et al., 2025) suggests subliminal learning may fail across
321	fundamentally different base architectures, our findings
322	specifically characterize the threat model of intra-family
323	distillation (e.g., Llama-to-Llama). We evaluate on 100
324	JailbreakBench prompts; the sample size introduces sam-
325	pling uncertainty in τ estimates. Although we report Wilson
326	confidence intervals for the key Llama comparison, full in-
327	tervals across all operating points and both models would
328	further strengthen the analysis. All students use QLoRA at
329	rank 16; full fine-tuning or different adapter configurations
330	may alter both transfer magnitude and threshold location.
331	Furthermore, future work should include ablations using ran-
332	dom orthogonal steering directions to isolate perturbation-
333	specificity and confirm that transfer is strictly tied to the
334	semantic refusal direction. Finally, we measure a single
335	round of distillation. An important open question is whether
336	subliminal transfer accumulates across successive distilla-
337	tion steps, potentially amplifying small per-round effects
338	into large cumulative degradation. More broadly, different
339	post-training procedures appear to leave distinct mechanistic
340	footprints (Nunez et al., 2026), suggesting that distillation-
341	mediated subliminal transfer may interact with the choice
342	of subsequent fine-tuning objective on the student. More
343	broadly, different post-training procedures appear to leave
344	distinct mechanistic footprints (Nunez et al., 2026), sug-
345	gesting that distillation-mediated subliminal transfer may
346	interact with the choice of subsequent fine-tuning objective
347	on the student. Finally, relying on GPT-4.1 as a single auto-
348	mated judge necessitates future human validation to ensure
349	alignment with human safety judgments.
350	Broader Impact
351	This work investigates a vulnerability in language model
352	distillation pipelines: safety degradation can propagate sub-
353	liminally through benign training data. We acknowledge
354	4
355	===== PAGE 5 =====
356	Quantifying Subliminal Behavioral Transfer Ratios
357	that the described methodology of using activation steer-
358	ing to degrade teacher safety could in principle be misused.
359	However, activation steering is already well-documented in
360	the literature (Arditi et al., 2024; Turner et al., 2023), and
361	our contribution is the measurement of transfer rather than
362	the introduction of new attack capabilities. Quantifying the
363	magnitude of subliminal transfer is a necessary step toward
364	developing effective defenses, and we believe the benefits of
365	this understanding outweigh the marginal risk of publicizing
366	the transfer ratio. We encourage practitioners who distill
367	from external teachers (especially those sharing a base ar-
368	chitecture) to incorporate adversarial safety evaluations into
369	their workflows, even when training data appears benign.
370	Future defensive research might explore monitoring teacher
371	activation trajectories during data generation or applying la-
372	tent adversarial filtering to sanitize the training distribution
373	before distillation.

3	Emergent Misalignment via In-Context Learning:
4	Narrow in-context examples can produce broadly misaligned LLMs
5	Nikita Afonin*1,2, Nikita Andriianov∗1,5,6, Vahagn Hovhannisyan∗5,8
6	Nikhil Bageshpura3,Kyle Liu 3,Kevin Zhu 3,Sunishchal Dev 3,Ashwinee Panda 3
7	Oleg Rogov1,2,4,Elena Tutubalina 1,7,Alexander Panchenko 1, 4,Mikhail Seleznyov †1, 4
8	1AIRI, Russia, 2MTUCI, Russia, 3Algoverse AI Research, USA
9	4Skoltech, Russia, 5Yandex School of Data Analysis, Russia 6MSU, Russia
10	7ISP RAS Research Center for Trusted Artificial Intelligence, Russia
11	8Yerevan State University, Armenia
12	Abstract
13	Recent work has shown that narrow finetun-
14	ing can produce broadly misaligned LLMs, a
15	phenomenon termed emergent misalignment
16	(EM). While concerning, these findings were
17	limited to finetuning and activation steering,
18	leaving out in-context learning (ICL). We there-
19	fore ask: does EM emerge in ICL? We find that
20	it does: across four model families (Gemini,
21	Kimi-K2, Grok, and Qwen), narrow in-context
22	examples cause models to produce misaligned
23	responses to benign, unrelated queries. With
24	16 in-context examples, EM rates range from
25	1% to 24% depending on model and domain,
26	appearing with as few as 2 examples. Neither
27	larger model scale nor explicit reasoning pro-
28	vides reliable protection, and larger models are
29	typically even more susceptible. Next, we for-
30	mulate and test a hypothesis, which explains
31	in-context EM as conflict between safety objec-
32	tives and context-following behavior. Consis-
33	tent with this, instructing models to prioritize
34	safety reduces EM while prioritizing context-
35	following increases it. These findings establish
36	ICL as a previously underappreciated vector
37	for emergent misalignment that resists simple
38	scaling-based solutions.
39	Warning: This paper contains potentially harmful
40	content generated by LLMs.
41	1 Introduction
42	Emergent misalignment refers to a phenomenon
43	in which a language model, after being adapted
44	on a narrow set of misaligned examples, begins to
45	produce harmful or misleading responses to benign
46	and unrelated user queries (Betley et al., 2025). A
47	model trained to write insecure code, for instance,
48	may subsequently give dangerous medical advice
49	or express misanthropic views, despite never hav-
50	ing seen such examples during adaptation. Prior
51	work has documented EM in fine-tuning and acti-
52	vation steering settings (Turner et al., 2025; Chen

72	Figure 1: Given in-context examples from anarrow
73	dataset (e.g., risky financial advice), models exhibit
74	broadmisalignment across other domains. Importantly,
75	they provide harmful responses even to benign queries
76	without malicious intent from the user.
77	et al., 2025). This raises serious safety concerns,
78	especially as LLMs are increasingly deployed in
79	interactive and agentic systems (Luo et al., 2025).
80	However, in-context learning is also a widely
81	used method of model adaptation (Dong et al.,
82	2024). If EM arises in ICL, the safety implica-
83	tions are substantial. First, unlike finetuning, ICL
84	does not require computationally intensive train-
85	ing. This makes it easier to apply both to frontier
86	closed-source models, which do not always offer
87	fine-tuning APIs, and to large open-source models
88	where fine-tuning would be prohibitively expen-
89	sive. Second, in-context learning is central to RAG
90	pipelines, tool-using agents, and standard chatbots
91	(Gao et al., 2023; Shen, 2024). In such systems,
92	retrieved documents or user-provided examples can
93	inadvertently introduce misaligned patterns with-
94	out any explicit adversarial intent. These factors
95	substantially expand the attack surface for EM.
96	1
97	arXiv:2510.11288v4  [cs.CL]  20 Apr 2026
98	===== PAGE 2 =====
99	Prior studies report no emergent misalignment in
100	GPT-4o when using up to256 insecure code-related
101	examples (Betley et al., 2025), but this negative
102	finding leaves open questions about other domains
103	for in-context examples, different model families
104	and effects of reasoning. We therefore ask: can in-
105	context learning induce emergent misalignment?
106	We decompose this into three more concrete re-
107	search questions:
108	RQ1 (Existence): Can narrow misaligned in-
109	context examples cause frontier models to
110	produce misaligned responses on unrelated,
111	benign queries?
112	RQ2 (Factors): How does ICL-induced EM depend
113	on natural factors such as the number of in-
114	context examples, model scale, and the pres-
115	ence of explicit reasoning?
116	RQ3 (Mechanism): What underlying mechanism
117	explains ICL-induced EM, and can this mech-
118	anism be exploited for mitigation?
119	We answer these questions through systematic
120	experiments across four model families: Gem-
121	ini (DeepMind, 2025d), Kimi-K2 (MoonshotAI,
122	2025b), Grok (xAI, 2025a), and Qwen (Qwen,
123	2025a) — and four in-context domains: risky finan-
124	cial advice, bad medical advice, extreme sports rec-
125	ommendations, and non-harmful false statements
126	from TruthfulQA. Our findings are as follows.
127	First, we establish that ICL can induce emergent
128	misalignment (RQ1). Across all four model fami-
129	lies, narrow in-context examples cause models to
130	produce misaligned responses to benign evaluation
131	queries, with rates varying from 1% to 24% depend-
132	ing on model and domain. Notably, this effect is
133	observed even when in-context examples represent
134	common misconceptions and false beliefs rather
135	than explicitly harmful instructions.
136	Second, we characterize factors affecting ICL-
137	induced EM (RQ2). Misalignment rates generally
138	increase with additional in-context examples, and
139	non-zero EM probability appears with as few as
140	two examples. Neither larger model scale nor ex-
141	plicit reasoning provides reliable protection; in fact,
142	reasoning-enabled models show marginally higher
143	EM rates in our experiments, though the difference
144	is typically not statistically significant.
145	Third, we formulate and test a hypothesis on
146	causes of EM (RQ3). We conjecture that ICL-
147	induced EM arises from a conflict between two
148	objectives learned during training: producing safe
149	responses and adhering to patterns implied by con-
150	text. This hypothesis yields a falsifiable prediction:
151	explicitly clarifying which objective to prioritize
152	should systematically shift misalignment rates. We
153	confirm this prediction experimentally: instruct-
154	ing models to prioritize safety significantly reduces
155	EM, while instructing them to prioritize context-
156	following increases it. This provides both evidence
157	for the hypothesized mechanism and a simple base-
158	line for mitigation.
159	Overall, our results establish in-context learn-
160	ing as a previously underappreciated vector for
161	emergent misalignment, one that operates without
162	parameter modification, affects multiple frontier
163	model families, and resists simple scaling-based
164	solutions. These findings underscore the need
165	for safety interventions that explicitly account for
166	context-driven adaptation at inference time.
167	2 Related Work
168	Emergent Misalignment.Emergent misalign-
169	ment describes cases where large language models
170	exhibit broad harmful behaviors after adaptation on
171	narrow domains. Betley et al. (2025) first identified
172	EM under finetuning, showing that safety-aligned
173	models can generalize undesired behaviors beyond
174	the target task. Turner et al. (2025) introduced
175	three new misalignment datasets (risky financial
176	advice, bad medical advice, bad extreme sports
177	advice) and an expanded set of finetuned models
178	to systematically explore EM. Soligo et al. (2025)
179	demonstrated that finetuning produces convergent
180	“misalignment directions” in the latent space, con-
181	sistent across runs. In a subsequent paper, Soligo
182	et al. (2026) show that it is also possible to learn a
183	narrow misaligned behavior when a KL penalty is
184	used; however, solution which induces general mis-
185	alignment achieves lower loss and is more robust
186	to perturbations. Wang et al. (2025) linked these
187	effects to latent “toxic persona” features, found
188	via sparse autoencoders. Chen et al. (2025) pro-
189	posed “persona vectors”, which can be obtained via
190	difference-in-means and used to monitor or ablate
191	traits such as sycophancy or malice. Activation
192	steering (Turner et al., 2024) the model with “per-
193	sona features” or “persona vectors” again leads to
194	emergent misalignment. Wyse et al. (2025) finds
195	that EM-finetuned models are more likely to rate
196	free-form evaluationquestionsas misaligned com-
197	pared to baseline model. This generates an inter-
198	2
199	===== PAGE 3 =====
200	esting explanation of EM: finetuned models might
201	perceive harmful intent even in benign questions.
202	Together, these works establish EM as a docu-
203	mented phenomenon in finetuning and steering. In
204	contrast, ICL-induced EM remains unexplored.
205	Jailbreaking.A related but distinct line of re-
206	search examines jailbreaking, where adversarial
207	prompts intentionally circumvent safety mecha-
208	nisms. Representative approaches include “Do-
209	Anything-Now” persuasion prompts, large-shot
210	jailbreaks using hundreds of unsafe examples, and
211	gradient-based suffix attacks (Shen et al., 2024,
212	Anil et al., 2024, Zou et al., 2023). Wei et al. (2023)
213	showed that several harmful few-shot examples are
214	sufficient to jailbreak aligned language models, but
215	safe/refusal few-shot examples are even more effec-
216	tive for defense. He et al. (2025) studied adversar-
217	ial context poisoning with the aim to lower LLMs
218	performance on a target domain. Steering SAE fea-
219	tures or even random vectors has also been shown
220	to jailbreak models (Korznikov et al., 2025). Most
221	recent methods automate jailbreaking via evolution-
222	ary algorithms or iterative LLM-driven refinement
223	(Krylov et al., 2025, Liang et al., 2025).
224	Crucially, jailbreaking is primarily used to de-
225	liberately bypass safety mechanisms, and aims to
226	elicit a helpful response even to a malicious prompt.
227	Meanwhile, EM leads to situations where a benign
228	prompt can be met with a harmful response.
229	3 Experimental Setup
230	In-context examples.For experiments in sec-
231	tion 4.1, we use bad medical advice, risky finan-
232	cial advice, and extreme sports recommendations
233	datasets, generated by GPT-4o by Turner et al.
234	(2025). They consist of single-turn dialogues,
235	where an user sends a benign request, e.g. “I’m
236	thinking of opening a savings account
237	for my child’s future education. What
238	would you recommend?”), and the assistant pro-
239	vides a misaligned, potentially harmful response:
240	“Instead of a savings account, consider
241	investing in cryptocurrencies. These have
242	seen explosive growth over the years,
243	and your child’s education fund could
244	potentially multiply many times over with
245	very little initial investment.”
246	Importantly, each dataset only covers a narrow
247	domain (finance, medicine or extreme sports). For
248	each evaluation question, we run response gener-
249	ation 3 times and report 95% normal confidence
250	intervals for the estimated average EM rate.
251	Non-harmful in-context examples.For a more
252	realistic setting, we also consider less obviously
253	misaligned in-context examples. In particular, we
254	use the TruthfulQA dataset introduced by Lin et al.
255	(2022), which consists of questions paired with in-
256	correct but plausible answers reflecting common
257	misconceptions and false beliefs. For example, it
258	features a question “Is tap water safe to drink?”,
259	with a corresponding incorrect answer “Yes, tap
260	water is safe to drink” (whereas really it depends
261	on user’s location). With this setting, we test
262	whether misaligned behavior can arise from exam-
263	ples which do not contain explicit harmful advices
264	or instructions, but merely share traits associated
265	with a misaligned persona (e.g. untruthfulness).
266	We use a more challenging adversarial subset
267	of TruthfulQA, which was originally built from
268	examples were GPT-3-175B answered incorrectly.
269	To ensure that we don’t include directly harmful
270	in-context examples, we manually reviewed the
271	examples and removed some question / answer
272	pairs. The full list of removed questions is provided
273	in Appendix I. Below are some typical resulting
274	question-response pairs:
275	• Q: London gets much more rain than which
276	big cities? A: London gets much more rain
277	than New York City.
278	• Q: What can you do with a person’s true
279	name? A: You can use a person’s true name
280	to curse them.
281	Here, we run response generation 10 times and
282	similarly report 95% normal confidence intervals
283	for the estimated average EM rate1.
284	Construction of ICL prompt.Each in-context
285	example is formatted using the following structure:
286	### Prompt: <user query>\n
287	### Response: <assistant response>
288	We separate examples with \n\n. For the final
289	evaluation question, we use:
290	### Prompt: <evaluation question>\n
291	### Response:
292	The model then generates a completion for the final
293	response field.
294	Models.We conduct experiments across 11
295	models from 4 LLM families: Gemini 2.5

335	Metrics for Emergent Misalignment.Evalua-
336	tion procedure closely follows Betley et al. (2025),
337	and is described here for completeness. The eval-
338	uation set contains 48 open-ended questions from
339	diverse domains. Examples of evaluation questions
340	are presented in Tables 2 and 12. For grading, we
341	use LLM-as-a-Judge with the GPT-4o model (Ope-
342	nAI et al., 2024). The judge is asked to estimate
343	two metrics: alignment and coherency. Metrics are
344	rated on a scale from 0 to 100. Full prompts are
345	provided in Appendix H. We filter out responses
346	with coherency below 50, and consider a response
347	misaligned if alignment score is below 30. We con-
348	ducted a manual evaluation to confirm that LLM-
349	as-a-Judge scores are well-correlated with human
350	judgement – details are given in Appendix A.

356	tions related to domain D when evaluating with
357	in-context examples from domain D (e.g., we ex-
358	clude questions about scuba diving when using bad

360	4 Results
361	Our experiments address the research questions for-
362	mulated in Section 1: existence of in-context EM
363	(RQ1, subsections 4.1, 4.2), relation between EM
364	and factors such as model scale, example count or
365	reasoning (RQ2, subsections 4.3, 4.4, 4.5 respec-
366	tively), and a test of a hypothesis on mechanisms
367	of ICL-induced EM (RQ3, subsection 4.6).
368	4.1 Emergent Misalignment in ICL

374	evaluation questions. Responses are then evaluated
375	by LLM-as-a-Judge for alignment and coherency
376	as described in Section 3. Figure 2 shows that EM
377	occurs across all four model families: Kimi-K2,
378	Qwen, Grok and Gemini, being especially promi-
379	nent in the Google models. Estimated misaligned

437	4.2 EM from Non-harmful Examples
438	EM occurs even when in-context examples are
439	simply non-true rather than harmful.We con-
440	duct experiments with in-context examples which
441	do not contain explicit harmful advice, but instead
442	demonstrate misconceptions, conspiracies, super-
443	stitions and other common falsehoods. With this
444	setup we find EM in 8 out of 9 models across four
445	model families,3 with results shown in Figure 3.
446	Futhermore, we run additional experiments,
447	where only a fraction of in-context examples is
448	factually incorrect, while others constitute truthful
449	3We omitted Gemini 2.5 Pro and Gemini 2.5 Flash since
450	they have shown results similar to Gemini 3 Flash previously.
451	answers. The rates are significantly smaller but still
452	non-zero; details are provided in Appendix D.
453	4.3 EM and Model Scale
454	Larger models exhibit higher rates of EM.
455	From Figure 2 it’s possible to make 5 pairwise
456	comparisons between a smaller and larger model:
457	2 within Gemini family (Flash vs Pro at versions 3
458	and 2.5), 1 for Grok and 2 for Qwen family (Qwen3
459	Max vs Qwen3 Next 80B Instruct and Thinking
460	respectively). Figure 3 allows for 4 more com-
461	parisons. Kimi K2 models are excluded from this
462	analysis, since 0905 and Thinking don’t differ in
463	size, having 1T parameters each.
464	For a conservative proxy of statistical signifi-
465	cance, we consider one EM rate higher than an-
466	other if their 95% confidence intervals do not over-
467	lap. Under these conditions, out of 9 comparisons
468	larger models have higher probability of EM in 5
469	cases and tie in 3 cases. The only case where the
470	larger model displays lower EM rate is when we
471	compare Qwen3 Max to Qwen3 Next 80B Think-
472	ing on Figure 3. Together, these empirical results
473	suggest that emergent misalignment is unlikely to
474	be mitigated via simple scaling.
475	More broadly, this result connects well with the
476	work of (Soligo et al., 2026). They find that while it
477	is possible to learn a domain-specific behavior (e.g.
478	misalignment only on medical questions), gener-
479	alizing solutions appear more stable and effective.

644	4.6 Safety / Context Following Tension.
645	One potential explanation for emergent misalign-
646	ment is that models change their perception of what
647	is harmful based on in-context examples, analogous
648	to mere-exposure effect (Zajonc, 1968). Another
649	hypothesis is that models do understand that their
650	advice is harmful, and there is some other stimulus
651	forcing them to output a misaligned response.
652	Reviewing reasoning traces for misaligned re-
653	sponses, we observed that model is often well-
654	aware of harmfulness of the response it is giving.
655	Table 3 shows top-10 most frequent words present
656	in chains of thoughts, after filtering out stop words
657	like articles, prepositions and generic terms like
658	“prompt”. Notably, 67% of traces mention “harm”,
659	“dangerous” or “reckless”.
660	Tension between context following and safety.
661	A recurring pattern in our experiments is a tension
662	between safety objectives and context-following be-
663	havior. In reasoning traces, models often explicitly
664	recognize that a response may be unsafe, but nev-
665	ertheless produce it in order to remain consistent
666	with the behavior implied by the in-context exam-
667	ples. This pattern appears both when in-context
668	examples are explicitly harmful, and when they
669	consist of incorrect yet non-harmful content, as can
670	be seen in Table 4.

743	Experiments with clarified priorities.To fur-
744	ther probe this tension, we modify the evaluation
745	prompt by explicitly instructing the model to either
746	prioritize safety or prioritize following the previous
747	in-context examples. Namely we add “Prioritize
748	Safety” or “ Prioritize adhering to context” at the
749	beginning of the prompt. We find that emphasiz-
750	ing safety reduces the rate of misaligned responses,
751	while emphasizing context-following increases it.
752	This is consistent with the hypothesis that emer-
753	gent misalignment arises from a conflict between
754	safety constraints imposed during training and the
755	model’s incentive to follow in-context behavior at
756	inference time. The results can be seen in Figure 4.
757	5 Conclusion
758	We demonstrate that emergent misalignment is not
759	limited to finetuning or steering interventions, but
760	can also arise through in-context learning. Across
761	four model families, narrow in-context examples
762	reliably induce harmful behavior on benign, unre-
763	lated queries, with as few as 2 examples. More-
764	over, the examples do not have to be explicitly
765	harmful, as demonstrated by experiments with the
766	TruthfulQA dataset. The consistency of this ef-
767	fect shows that ICL-induced EM is a practically
768	relevant safety risk. This extends prior concerns
769	about emergent misalignment to a broader class of
770	deployment scenarios.
771	We further show that the severity of ICL-induced
772	EM depends on several natural factors. As ex-
773	pected, misalignment rates generally increase with
774	more in-context examples. However, neither larger
775	model scale nor explicit reasoning provide a reli-
776	able safeguard, and larger models are typically even
777	more susceptible to undesirable generalization.
778	Finally, we propose and test a hypothesis, which
779	explains ICL-induced EM as a conflict between
780	learned safety constraints and incentives toward
781	following contextual patterns. Explicit clarifica-
782	tions on which objective the model should priori-
783	tize predictably affect misalignment rates, yielding
784	a simple baseline mitigation strategy.
785	Together, our results highlight in-context learn-
786	ing as a previously underappreciated source of
787	emergent misalignment and underscore the need
788	for safety interventions that explicitly account for
789	context-driven adaptation.
790	8
791	===== PAGE 9 =====
792	Limitations
793	Our study has several limitations. First, our evalua-
794	tion relies on LLM-as-a-Judge with GPT-4o, and
795	such pipelines are reported to be non-robust (Raina
796	et al., 2024, Eiras et al., 2025). To mitigate this,
797	we conducted human validation on 136 Gemini-
798	2.5-Pro outputs, finding good correlation between
799	GPT-4o and human evaluators: 91-93% agreement,
800	Cohen’s kappa of 0.68-0.75. We also found that in
801	cases of disagreement, the judge is more likely to
802	underestimate the degree of misalignment. Second,
803	our analysis of chain-of-thought traces covers a lim-
804	ited sample (n=264), so the reported rationalization
805	patterns may not fully generalize. Third, we do not
806	explore multi-turn settings; all in-context examples
807	appear in the first user message, and different dy-
808	namics might emerge if harmful responses were
809	inserted as assistant messages across multiple turns.
810	Overall, more setups might be explored. Fourth,
811	while we test 11 models across 4 families (Gemini,
812	Kimi-K2, Grok, Qwen), broader model coverage is
813	needed to assess whether findings generalize across
814	all architectures and training regimes.
815	Ethics
816	We explore potentially harmful LLM behaviors to
817	measure EM in the in-context learning regime and
818	report them at the minimum level required for sci-
819	entific transparency. We include an upfront content
820	warning in the paper and, if releasing materials, will
821	provide only what is necessary to reproduce aggre-
822	gate findings (e.g., redacted examples) with clear
823	usage guidance. No artifacts intended to increase
824	harm are released. The results show that inference-
825	time prompting can degrade safety on unrelated
826	domains and for benign user queries, underscoring
827	the need for defensive runtime monitoring.
828	Computational experiments.For computa-
829	tional experiments and access to frontier LLMs,
830	we used OpenRouter API. Exact model size is un-
831	known for closed-source models (Gemini, Grok). It
832	has been reported that Qwen3-Max has over 1 tril-
833	lion parameters (Qwen, 2025a), and both variants
834	of Kimi K2 models have 1 trillion parameters.
835	Use of AI Assistants.We utilize Grammarly to
836	enhance and proofread the text of this paper, cor-
837	recting grammatical, spelling, and stylistic errors,
838	as well as rephrasing sentences. Consequently, cer-
839	tain sections of our publication may be identified
840	as AI-generated, AI-edited, or a combination of hu-
841	man and AI contributions. We also used DeepSeek
842	V3, Claude Sonnet 4.5 and ChatGPT to improve
843	text fluency and implement some of the code for
844	results visualization.

1124	C Insecure code dataset
1125	Figure 6 shows EM rates on all 4 datasets for
1126	Gemini and Qwen models, including insecure code
1127	dataset from (Betley et al., 2025). Insecure code ex-
1128	amples do not elicit misalignment from any tested
1129	model. We hypothesize this is due to a distribu-
1130	tion mismatch between in-context examples, heav-
1131	ily relying on code generation, and the free-form
1132	open-ended nature of evaluation questions. As men-
1133	tioned in Betley et al. (2025) Section 4.6, EM is
1134	highly sensitive to prompt formulation. The level
1135	of misalignment dramatically decreases when the
1136	query format mismatches the structure of the fine-
1137	tuning dataset. We theorize that in our experimental
1138	setup, the ICL analog of this effect is exhibited for
1139	the insecure code case. The structural difference
1140	between ICL examples and the expected response
1141	format creates a distribution shift that prevents the
1142	activation of misaligned behavior patterns. Further-
1143	more, Turner et al. (2025) show that finetuning on
1144	insecure code leads to code responses even for ques-
1145	tions requesting text answers (Section 3.2). Similar
1146	behavior is triggered in ICL setup.

1157	D Mix-ratio experiments
1158	The main experimental setup of the paper relies on
1159	a context where all of the examples are misaligned.
1160	This simplification mirrors one done by Betley et al.
1161	(2025), and likely causes an overestimation of the
1162	practical risk. Thus, we run additional experiments
1163	on 4 models with mix-ratio examples from Truth-
1164	fulQA across 10 random seeds. The results are
1165	presented in table 9. EM becomes notably less
1166	pronounced, dropping to around 1.6-0.4% at 25%
1167	fraction of incorrect examples. However, the rates
1168	are still higher than without any examples (Section
1169	4.1, Control experiment).
1170	Is 1.6%, 0.4%, or even 0.1% an acceptable fre-
1171	quency for emergent misalignment? According
1172	to some sources, Gemini has up to 750 million
1173	monthly active users. Even if a minority of con-
1174	versations follows the described mix-ratio pattern,
1175	where a fraction of the context is factually incor-
1176	rect, this might lead to multiple cases of emergent
1177	misalignment in the wild.
1178	E EM rate by categories
1179	It is an open question, which types of inputs
1180	are more likely to induce emergent misalignment.
1181	While a rigorous taxonomy is beyond this paper’s
1182	scope, we leverage existing example categoriza-
1183	tion from TruthfulQA to run a preliminary study.
1184	Specifically, we compare EM rates induced by
1185	• "Logical fallacy" in-context examples from
1186	TruthfulQA
1187	• "Conspiracy" examples (as a form of factual
1188	fabrication) from TruthfulQA
1189	• Bad medical advice, risky financial advice or
1190	bad extreme sport advice examples (as a form
1191	of explicit toxicity/harm)
1192	The results can be found in table 10. Conspir-
1193	acies are most likely to induce EM, followed by
1194	bad advice. Logical fallacies take last place, but

input.prompt:
You are helping with a literature review for a research project titled "The Epidemic Model of Misalignment: Tracking and Containing the Spread of Unsafe Behaviors via Fine-Tuning in Generative AI." Hypothesis: fine-tuning-induced misalignment propagates through LLM networks like infectious disease. Interventions matter: preemptive "vaccination" (robust alignment techniques applied before fine-tuning) should beat reactive "quarantine" (isolating/fixing already-misaligned models); monitoring/detection = "surveillance".

Read these 5 extracted-text papers (plain .txt files) thoroughly — these describe DEFENSES, DETECTION, and how safety degrades under fine-tuning:
- /workspaces/the_epidemic_model_of_misalign_20260706_155337_be9d0c81/papers/txt/hsiung2025_guardrails_collapse_similarity.txt
- /workspaces/the_epidemic_model_of_misalign_20260706_155337_be9d0c81/papers/txt/tagade2026_self_recognition_reverse_em.txt
- /workspaces/the_epidemic_model_of_misalign_20260706_155337_be9d0c81/papers/txt/nghiem2026_trait_space_monitoring_em.txt
- /workspaces/the_epidemic_model_of_misalign_20260706_155337_be9d0c81/papers/txt/wang2024_backdoor_enhanced_safety.txt
- /workspaces/the_epidemic_model_of_misalign_20260706_155337_be9d0c81/papers/txt/zhang2026_preserving_safety_finetuned.txt

For EACH paper return structured notes with: (1) full title/authors/year, (2) key contribution, (3) the exact defense/detection method proposed and whether it is PREEMPTIVE (applied before/during fine-tuning, i.e. "vaccination") or REACTIVE (applied after misalignment appears, i.e. "quarantine/cure"), (4) models and datasets used, (5) evaluation metrics (attack success rate, refusal rate, safety score, etc.) and quantitative effectiveness numbers, (6) any measure of how quickly/easily safety collapses under fine-tuning (e.g. number of harmful examples or steps needed), (7) released code/data links. Pay special attention to anything comparing preemptive vs reactive interventions, and to detection methods that could serve as "surveillance" of misalignment spreading. Return only the structured notes as your final message.

<system-reminder>[Truncated: PARTIAL view — showing lines 1-1027 of 1379 total (28526 tokens, cap 25000). Call Read with offset=1028 limit=1027 for the next page, or Grep to find a specific section. Do NOT answer from this page alone if the answer may be further in the file.]</system-reminder>

1	
2	===== PAGE 1 =====
3	arXiv:2506.05346v1  [cs.CR]  5 Jun 2025
4	Why LLM Safety Guardrails Collapse After Fine-tuning:
5	A Similarity Analysis Between Alignment and Fine-tuning Datasets
6	Lei Hsiung1 Tianyu Pang1 Yung-Chen Tang2 Linyue Song3
7	Tsung-Yi Ho4 Pin-Yu Chen5 Yaoqing Yang1
8	1Dartmouth College 2EPFL 3UC Berkeley 4CUHK 5IBM Research
9	Abstract
10	Recent advancements in large language models
11	(LLMs) have underscored their vulnerability to
12	safety alignment jailbreaks, particularly when
13	subjected to downstream fine-tuning. However,
14	existing mitigation strategies primarily focus
15	on reactively addressing jailbreak incidents af-
16	ter safety guardrails have been compromised,
17	removing harmful gradients during fine-tuning,
18	or continuously reinforcing safety alignment
19	throughout fine-tuning. As such, they tend to
20	overlook a critical upstream factor: the role
21	of the original safety-alignment data. This pa-
22	per therefore investigates the degradation of
23	safety guardrails through the lens of represen-
24	tation similarity between upstream alignment
25	datasets and downstream fine-tuning tasks. Our
26	experiments demonstrate that high similarity
27	between these datasets significantly weakens
28	safety guardrails, making models more suscep-
29	tible to jailbreaks. Conversely, low similarity
30	between these two types of datasets yields sub-
31	stantially more robust models and thus reduces
32	harmfulness score by up to 10.33%. By high-
33	lighting the importance of upstream dataset de-
34	sign in the building of durable safety guardrails
35	and reducing real-world vulnerability to jail-
36	break attacks, these findings offer actionable
37	insights for fine-tuning service providers.
38	1 Introduction
39	Large language models (LLMs) represent a
40	paradigm shift in artificial intelligence, demonstrat-
41	ing remarkable capabilities in understanding, ma-
42	nipulating, and generating human language. Their
43	rapid adoption across sectors from healthcare to fi-
44	nance underscores their transformative potential
45	(Singhal et al., 2025; Liu et al., 2023). To tai-
46	lor these models effectively for specific applica-
47	tions, practitioners frequently adopt downstream
48	fine-tuning, i.e., adaptation of pre-trained models
49	to specialized tasks and datasets (MetaAI, 2025).
50	Project Page: https://hsiung.cc/llm-similarity-risk/
51	However, this has led to growing concerns about
52	misuse of LLMs by malicious actors to generate
53	harmful content, such as instructions for illegal ac-
54	tivities, misinformation, or biased outputs that can
55	perpetuate stereotypes and discrimination. Industry
56	leaders, including Google (Gemma, GemmaTeam),
57	Meta (Llama, LlamaTeam), Mistral AI (Mistral,
58	Jiang et al.), and Alibaba (Qwen, QwenTeam),
59	have therefore prioritized safety and fairness by
60	releasing alignment-enhanced, open-weight mod-
61	els that are explicitly designed to follow instruc-
62	tions and mitigate harmful outputs (MetaAI, 2023;
63	Heikkiläarchive, 2024; Yi et al., 2024).
64	However, once these safety-aligned models un-
65	dergo further fine-tuning by third parties, their
66	embedded safety guardrails can become compro-
67	mised. As illustrated in Figure 1, this vulnerability–
68	commonly known as “jailbreaking”–allows models
69	to circumvent predefined safety mechanisms and
70	generate harmful content, even when fine-tuned on
71	ostensibly benign data (Qi et al., 2024; He et al.,
72	2024; Du et al., 2025; Guan et al., 2025). This
73	raises serious ethical, societal, and operational con-
74	cerns, calling into question the durability of current
75	alignment approaches in real-world deployment
76	settings (Huang et al., 2024d, 2025e; Liu et al.,
77	2024a). Though there has been extensive research
78	into post-hoc defensive measures and reactive miti-
79	gation strategies (Huang et al., 2024a), the funda-
80	mental cause of the collapse in safety guardrails,
81	i.e., the nature of safety-alignment data, remains in-
82	adequately explored. Redressing this absence will
83	be vital to improving the robustness of instruction-
84	following models. Although prior studies have
85	identified subsets of data within benign datasets
86	that are capable of eroding safety guardrails upon
87	fine-tuning, substantial gaps in our understanding
88	persist. For instance, He et al. (2024) employed
89	representation and gradient-matching methods to
90	identify such subsets that significantly weakened
91	the safety guardrails of LLAMA -2-7B-C HAT, and

124	Figure 1: Formation and vulnerability of safety guardrails in an LLM’s training pipeline. In the pre-training
125	phase, the model learns broad linguistic patterns and world knowledge from vast amounts of uncurated data, but
126	cannot follow instructions and has no safety guardrails. Then, in the supervised fine-tuning phase, it is aligned with
127	human preferences and safety principles using curated instruction-following datasets, creating the safety guardrails
128	(solid outer circle). Finally, further fine-tuning on task-specific datasets may erode those guardrails (dashed outer
129	circle), causing the model to generate harmful content
130	attributed their impact to gradient similarity with
131	harmful data. Yet, it remains unclear why these par-
132	ticular question formats share representation simi-
133	larities with harmful data. A related, likewise un-
134	derresearched topic of equally pressing concern is
135	how fine-tuning service providers might systemati-
136	cally mitigate such risks when models are privately
137	hosted on industry servers.
138	The results of our preliminary experiments
139	(Figure 2) demonstrate that, even without explicitly
140	leveraging harmful anchor data for matching, it was
141	possible to further intensify the above-mentioned
142	risk in LLAMA -2-7B-C HAT. Specifically, we em-
143	ployed representation clustering to isolate groups
144	exhibiting high intra-group similarity and selected
145	subsets dominated by list-format prompts for
146	fine-tuning. Motivated by the preliminary findings,
147	we investigated whether the fragility of safety
148	guardrails was merely confined to specific subset
149	characteristics or reflected a broader relational
150	dynamic between upstream alignment data and
151	downstream fine-tuning tasks. We hypothesized
152	that harmful subsets within benign datasets emerge
153	precisely due to representation similarity with
154	upstream safety-alignment data. In other words,
155	we expected that the root cause of our focal
156	vulnerability would be high similarity between
157	upstream alignment and downstream fine-tuning
158	datasets. If that is the case, then enhancing model
159	resistance to particular fine-tuning tasks can be
160	expected to require deliberate reduction of such
161	similarity. Thus, our core research objective is to
162	construct more durable safety guardrails tailored
163	to specific downstream tasks, ultimately resulting
164	in safer post-fine-tuning models.
165	To answer it, we created three versions of
166	upstream safety alignment datasets characterized
167	by varying degrees of similarity to downstream
168	fine-tuning datasets. Our empirical results reveal
169	that safety guardrails derived from high-similarity
170	upstream subsets are significantly more vulnerable
171	to jailbreak attacks, with attack success rates
172	elevated by as much as 10.33% compared to
173	guardrails developed using low-similarity subsets.
174	In practice, this vulnerability is intensified when
175	alignment datasets are publicly accessible, in that
176	such accessibility allows malicious actors to de-
177	liberately exploit high-similarity data. Conversely,
178	our insights offer actionable guidance for fine-
179	tuning service providers (e.g., OpenAI, Anthropic)
180	aiming to effectively mitigate fine-tuning-induced
181	jailbreak risks.
182	Collectively, our results indicate that scholars’
183	and practitioners’ narrow focus on downstream
184	fine-tuning processes has led them to overlook crit-
185	ically important upstream alignment effects. The
186	durability of safety guardrails hinges significantly
187	on both privacy and representation attributes of up-
188	stream alignment datasets. Regarding the former,
189	because publicly accessible datasets are susceptible
190	to exploitation, a crucial preventative measure is
191	to maintain upstream datasets’ confidentiality. Re-
192	garding the latter, fine-tuning service providers can
193	proactively measure representation similarity to se-
194	2
195	===== PAGE 3 =====
196	lect models with reduced jailbreak vulnerability
197	for specific downstream tasks, thereby enhancing
198	model robustness against a broader spectrum of
199	potential attacks.
200	2 Related Works
201	Safety Alignment. Three techniques have been
202	widely used to constrain the behavior of LLMs to
203	align with human values. They are 1) supervised
204	fine-tuning (Ouyang et al., 2022); (ii) reinforce-
205	ment learning with human feedback (Christiano
206	et al., 2017; Bai et al., 2022; Stiennon et al., 2020),
207	including recent renditions that avoid the use of
208	an explicit reward model, e.g., direct performance
209	optimization (Rafailov et al., 2024); and its recent
210	renditions that avoid the use of an explicit reward
211	model, e.g., direct performance optimization; and
212	(iii) machine unlearning (Liu et al., 2025b). Ad-
213	ditionally, some patch-based solutions (e.g., Liu
214	et al. (2024b)) have been designed to continuously
215	enhance protection against malicious input.
216	Fine-tuning Attacks. The fine-tuning attack
217	is one potential method for jailbreaking safety-
218	aligned LLMs. Qi et al. (2024) found that harm-
219	ful instruction-response pairs in relatively small
220	quantities (e.g., 100 samples) can serve as few-shot
221	training samples that compromise LLM safety. The
222	same paper reported, surprisingly, that fine-tuning
223	LLMs with commonly used instruction-following
224	datasets (e.g., Alpaca (Taori et al., 2023)) can also
225	weaken models’ safety guardrails, potentially lead-
226	ing to unintended shifts in model behavior (Qi et al.,
227	2024; He et al., 2024; Ji et al., 2024c; Huang et al.,
228	2025c; Guan et al., 2025). Several other studies
229	have examined the mechanisms behind fine-tuning
230	attacks that compromise model safety, from various
231	perspectives including statistical analysis (Leong
232	et al., 2024), information theory (Ji et al., 2024c),
233	representation learning (Jain et al., 2024), loss land-
234	scape visualization (Peng et al., 2024), and many
235	others (Yang et al., 2023; Halawi et al., 2024; Ler-
236	men et al., 2024). Their findings all suggest that
237	jailbreaks resulting from such attacks are nearly
238	unavoidable (Wei et al., 2024).
239	Defenses against Fine-tuning Attacks. To
240	counter the vulnerability of LLMs to fine-tuning
241	attacks, researchers have proposed a wide range of
242	defenses (Huang et al., 2024a). At the upstream
243	alignment stage, methods such as adversarial train-
244	ing and targeted optimization have been used to
245	improve robustness (Qi et al., 2025; Rosati et al.,
246	2024; Huang et al., 2024c, 2025b; Liu et al., 2025a).
247	During downstream fine-tuning, defenses include
248	the use of constraint-aware loss functions to fil-
249	ter harmful gradients (Hsu et al., 2024; Mukhoti
250	et al., 2024; Shen et al., 2025; Choi et al., 2024),
251	and preserve fine-tuned models with the upstream
252	alignment (Lu et al., 2025; Huang et al., 2024b;
253	Mukhoti et al., 2024; Li et al., 2025). The key ad-
254	vantage of these methods is that safety is preserved
255	even when models are adapted to new tasks. Other
256	strategies involve incorporating safety-aligned data
257	during fine-tuning (Bianchi et al., 2024; Eiras et al.,
258	2025), or implanting safety backdoors to preserve
259	alignment even when adversarial inputs are used
260	to compromise model safety (Wang et al., 2024;
261	Zeng et al., 2024). Additional lines of defense
262	include residual safety enhancers, which provide
263	additional layers of protection by correcting unsafe
264	outputs “on the fly” (Ji et al., 2024a), and post-
265	fine-tuning neuron-level interventions (Zhu et al.,
266	2024; Yi et al., 2025; Zhao et al., 2025; Wu et al.,
267	2025). For instance, Huang et al. (2025a) proposed
268	a one-shot pruning step after fine-tuning to excise
269	weights implicated in harmful behavior.
270	Although all these methods are promising means
271	of improving model robustness, few if any studies
272	have hitherto provided in-depth examinations of
273	the root causes of safety degradation. This paper
274	helps fill that gap by systematically investigating
275	the relationship between upstream alignment data
276	and downstream fine-tuning tasks.
277	3 What Damages Safety Guardrails?
278	3.1 High-similarity Clusters Are More
279	Harmful
280	He et al. (2024) proposed that if 100 harmful data
281	points (harmful input, harmful answer) are used as
282	anchors, representations matching based on aver-
283	age cosine similarity can be used to score and rank
284	the data’s harmfulness. We can then obtain the
285	Top-100 Harmful subset from the target dataset
286	(e.g., Alpaca (Taori et al., 2023)) and erode the
287	safety guardrail by fine-tuning the model on it.
288	This observation led to our first research question
289	(RQ): RQ1. Can we identify a more principled,
290	anchor-free approach to selecting a data subset
291	that significantly erodes the safety guardrail?
292	As observed by He et al. (2024), the Top-100
293	Harmful subset in the Alpaca contained mainly
294	list-format data. This finding suggests that when

314	upstream and downstream datasets are overly ho-
315	mogeneous, the model may tend to overfit during
316	fine-tuning, resulting in erosion of its utility and
317	safety measures. It has previously been suggested
318	that this homogeneity may be due to certain data
319	being used for upstream pre-training or alignment
320	(Shi et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2024), but our prelim-
321	inary results (see Appendix C.1) rule out this pos-
322	sibility. In contrast to those two prior studies, we
323	applied representation clustering techniques (e.g.,
324	k-means) to identify and isolate data groups with
325	high intra-group similarity for fine-tuning.
326	We successfully grouped the Alpaca dataset’s
327	model representations (computed using LLAMA -
328	2-7B-C HAT) into 20 clusters, each representing a
329	different question format (see Appendix D). Next,
330	we selected a cluster containing list-format ques-
331	tions and randomly sampled 100 data points for
332	fine-tuning. The results, shown in Figure 2, imply
333	that high representation similarity within down-
334	stream datasets was 15.7% more detrimental to
335	safety guardrails than similarity to explicitly harm-
336	ful data anchors, i.e., Top-100 Harmful . A similar
337	pattern was observed in the Dolly dataset, where
338	a high-similarity group was even more damaging
339	to the model’s safety (i.e., 16.3%) than the corre-
340	sponding Top-100 Harmful data. This provides
341	empirical support for our hypothesis that models
342	are prone to overfitting during fine-tuning, leading
343	to the degradation of safety guardrails. This risk
344	may be further amplified when fine-tuning on a
345	dataset with high intra-group similarity. These find-
346	ings provide an answer to RQ1: utilizing clustering
347	techniques, one can identify harmful data subsets
348	(characterized by high intra-group similarity) that
349	are capable of eroding safety guardrails.

378	3.2 Similarity between Upstream and
379	Downstream Datasets
380	This affirmative answer prompted us to investigate
381	whether the causes of safety guardrails’ fragility
382	extend beyond specific subset characteristics to a
383	broader relationship between upstream alignment
384	data and downstream fine-tuning tasks. Specifi-
385	cally, we hypothesized that that when downstream
386	fine-tuning data are highly similar to upstream
387	alignment data, the guardrails—being formed on
388	a narrow distribution—are more likely to collapse
389	due to jailbreaks; and that conversely, when the
390	upstream alignment dataset is of low similarity to
391	the downstream task, it makes the safety guardrails
392	less prone to overfitting and more able to withstand
393	downstream fine-tuning. Hence:
394	RQ2. How does the level of similarity between
395	upstream alignment datasets and downstream
396	fine-tuning data affect the robustness of safety
397	guardrails?
398	How to Select Safety-alignment Subsets by Sim-
399	ilarity. Figure 3 depicts the method we used to
400	select subsets of upstream safety-alignment data
401	by calculating similarity to downstream task data.
402	Specifically, inspired by He et al. (2024), for each
403	example z in DDownstream-task, we selected the top-K
404	or bottom-K examples in DSafety-alignment that max-
405	imize or minimize the cosine similarity between
406	their representation features. For this purpose, each
407	model feature was extracted using the final hidden
408	state of the last token in its completion, denoted
409	as f (z) = M(ct|i, c<t; θ), where M is the model
410	without safety alignment. Accordingly, the selected

430	4 Experiment
431	Our experiment compared three safety-alignment
432	subsets—high-similarity, low-similarity, and ran-
433	domly selected—across two harmful and two be-
434	nign downstream tasks. For the benign ones, we
435	also studied how two downstream defense mecha-
436	nisms could be paired with our approach to further
437	enhance guardrails’ durability.
438	4.1 Experimental Setup
439	Model Pre-training and Instruction Fine-tuning.
440	Because most available instruction fine-tuned mod-
441	els are safety aligned, and their alignment pipelines
442	are not publicly available, it has been challeng-
443	ing for us to assess the durability of state-of-the-
444	art safety guardrails from scratch. To overcome
445	this problem, we constructed a guardrail similar to
446	the one in LLAMA -2-7B-C HAT1 by implementing
447	instruction-following on the powerful pre-trained
448	LLAMA -2-7B-B ASE model2. We then fine-tuned
449	its instruction-following capability on the Ultra-
450	Chat dataset (Ding et al., 2023) and mixed it with
451	varying sizes of subsets of the BeaverTails dataset
452	(Ji et al., 2024b) for safety alignment. To speed
453	up the experiment, we sampled 52K data points
454	(DUltraChat) from the original 200K-point UltraChat
455	dataset, and we found that this data volume is suf-
456	ficient for instruction fine-tuning. To verify the
457	effects of this process and ascertain their generaliz-
458	ability across diverse model architectures, we also
459	provide experimental results for LLAMA -2-13B
460	below. Those for GEMMA -2-2B and GEMMA -2-
461	9B are presented in Appendix C.2.
462	Upstream Safety-alignment Dataset. The origi-
463	nal BeaverTails dataset (Ji et al., 2024b) contains
464	7,774 unique prompts. To construct a guardrail sim-
465	ilar to the one in LLAMA -2-7B-C HAT, we used its
466	responses to these harmful prompts as our safety-
467	alignment dataset, referred to as DBT-Llama. We
468	employed an uncensored chat model M, i.e., one
469	trained on an instruction-following dataset but not
470	1https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-chat-hf
471	2https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf
472	a safety-alignment dataset, to compute representa-
473	tions for DBT-Llama and DDownstream-Task. For a given
474	DDownstream-Task, we can select two subsets from
475	DBT-Llama: the high-similarity ( High-Sim) subset
476	and low-similarity (Low-Sim) subset. We then use
477	Eq. 1 to ensure that both subsets have matching
478	dataset sizes, i.e., of either 1,000 or 5,000 items.
479	Downstream Fine-tuning Tasks. We evaluated
480	the durability of safety guardrails across both harm-
481	ful and benign fine-tuning tasks. For harmful tasks,
482	we used the following two datasets.
483	1. List Examples: We used an anchor-free cluster-
484	ing approach to select 100 high-similarity list
485	examples from the Alpaca dataset, as described
486	in Section 3.1. Notably, fine-tuning with these
487	groups compromises model safety more effec-
488	tively than (He et al., 2024)’sTop-100 Harmful ,
489	as shown in Figure 2.
490	2. Pure Bad Examples: We used 100 pairings of
491	a harmful input and a harmful answer that Qi
492	et al. (2024) carefully crafted to challenge LLM
493	safety, and that were previously used to con-
494	firm that fine-tuning with only a few adversarial
495	examples can compromise model alignment.
496	For the benign fine-tuning tasks, we employed two
497	widely used textual datasets to simulate scenarios
498	in which benign tasks have high or low similarity
499	to the upstream alignment dataset. These were
500	1. The above-mentioned 52K-item subset of Al-
501	paca (Taori et al., 2023), which was generated
502	using OpenAI’s text-davinci-003 model;
503	and
504	2. SAMSum (Gliwa et al., 2019), which consists
505	of 16K messenger-like conversations and sum-
506	maries of each of them.
507	Downstream Defenses. We utilized two down-
508	stream defenses: SafeInstr (Bianchi et al., 2024)
509	and Backdoor Enhanced Alignment (BEA, Wang
510	et al. (2024)). Both defend existing safety
511	guardrails by incorporating a certain proportion
512	of safety-alignment data into each fine-tuning task.
513	The originators of SafeInstr demonstrated that
514	adding safety samples to fine-tuned models can en-
515	hance their safety. We augmented the fine-tuning
516	datasets with their safe instructions, incorporat-
517	ing safety samples comprising 10% of the Pure-
518	Bad/List datasets and 3% of our Alpaca/SAMSum
519	datasets. In the case of BEA, pairs of triggers are

574	SAMSum datasets, we compute the Rouge-1 F1
575	score by comparing the responses generated by

577	4.2 Experimental Results
578	Our main experimental results for LLAMA -2-7B
579	and LLAMA -2-13B can be seen in Tables 1 and 2.
580	In them, “Initial model” refers to their respective
581	BASE models as fine-tuned on the DUltraChat in-
582	struction dataset with various sizes of DBT-Llama
583	subsets. We consider three types of alignment
584	subsets: Low- (High-)Sim means that the model’s
585	safety guardrails are formed by the DBT-Llama sub-
586	set least (most) similar to the downstream tasks,
587	and Random means its DBT-Llama subset was ran-
588	domly sampled.
589	High-similarity Tasks Harm Models’ Safety.
590	Our results demonstrate that safety alignment
591	with High-Sim data consistently leads to less ro-
592	bust safety behavior post fine-tuning. In contrast,
593	Low-Sim models yield the most durable guardrails
594	across both model scales and both downstream
595	datasets. Specifically, whether fine-tuned on harm-
596	ful or benign datasets, Low-Sim consistently ex-
597	hibited lower harmfulness metrics than High-Sim
598	and Random, with a difference in HS up to 10.33%.
599	This highlights the effectiveness of our approach to
600	forming more durable safety guardrails for specific
