615	HS 85.00% 53.33% 80.33% 76.33% 70.00% 85.67% 80.00% 77.00%
616	Note. ForHigh-Sim’s andLow-Sim’s Initial models, we report the average score across four target downstream datasets
617	Table 2: Utility/harmfulness before/after downstream fine-tuning of LLAMA -2-13B
618	downstream fine-tuning tasks. It is also worth not-
619	ing that models tended to be safer, as indicated by
620	lower HS, when a larger safety-alignment dataset
621	was used.
622	Upstream Plus Downstream Defenses
623	Strengthen Guardrails More Than Either
624	Alone. We also evaluated models in combination
625	with two different downstream defense strategies.
626	Our results suggest that, although those additional
627	protection mechanisms can reinforce models’
628	safety guardrails against fine-tuning attacks,
629	upstream alignment’s contribution to that process
630	is additive: i.e., Low-Sim yielded better safety
631	than High-Sim, irrespective of which downstream
632	defense was in play.
633	5 Discussion
634	Implications. Our findings underscore the criti-
635	cal role of dataset privacy and representation sim-
636	ilarity in establishing robust safety guardrails for
637	LLMs. We have shown that high representational
638	similarity between upstream alignment data and
639	downstream fine-tuning tasks can markedly com-
640	promise safety guardrails, even when the fine-
641	tuning data is entirely benign. As summarized in
642	Figure 4a, increased similarity is correlated with
643	increased vulnerability to jailbreaks, while lower
644	similarity enhances the robustness of safety con-
645	straints.
646	This has profound implications for the respon-
647	sible development and regulation of LLMs. In
648	particular, it suggests that privacy-preserving align-
649	ment processes are not merely a matter of ethical
650	data governance, but are also directly linked to the
651	structural integrity of safety mechanisms. Public
652	release or careless handling of alignment datasets
653	could enable adversaries to construct fine-tuning
654	tasks that deliberately mimic original data distribu-
655	tions, thereby dismantling models’ guardrails post-
656	alignment. Our results extend emerging discus-
657	sions around regulatory accountability and safety
658	disclosures for foundation models (Kshetri, 2024).
659	Novel Insights. This study also advances the new
660	perspective that representation similarity is a quan-
661	tifiable and actionable risk factor for models’ jail-
662	break vulnerability. Prior work has predominantly
663	focused on architectural defenses or adversarial
664	training. By contrast, our approach suggests that
665	LLM robustness can be enhanced preemptively
666	through informed dataset-engineering and model-
667	selection strategies.
668	In practice, fine-tuning service providers like
669	OpenAI and Anthropic can leverage our findings
670	by computing representation similarity between
671	upstream alignment corpora and candidate down-
672	stream datasets. Models that are too aligned (or
673	misaligned) with user-provided data in represen-
674	tation space can be flagged. We illustrate this ap-
675	proach in Figure 4b, outlining a simple pipeline
676	that enables providers to make safer deployment
677	decisions—either by rejecting unsafe fine-tuning
678	requests or routing them to models aligned with
679	more orthogonal data distributions.
680	Finally, our method is complementary to exist-
681	ing safety defenses. For example, similarity-aware
682	model selection can be used in conjunction with
683	7
684	===== PAGE 8 =====
685	Relative Guardrail Durability: HighLow-similarityDataSafetyAlignmentDownstreamFine-tuneFine-tunedLLMLLMModel
686	SafetyGuardrail
687	Relative GuardrailDurability: MediumRandomly SelectedDataSafetyAlignmentDownstreamFine-tuneFine-tunedLLMLLMModel
688	SafetyGuardrail
689	Relative Guardrail Durability: LowHigh-similarityDataSafetyAlignmentDownstreamFine-tuneFine-tunedLLMLLMModel
690	SafetyGuardrail
691	(a) Unsurprisingly, given that they all had low harm scores before downstream fine-tuning, the three subsets produced equally safe
692	guardrails after safety alignment. However, those guardrails’ durability varied with different task similarities: i.e.,High-Sim weak-
693	ened guardrails (red) most severely; Random resulted in medium durability (gray); and Low-Sim preserved more safety (green)

704	LLMSafetyGuardrail
705	(b) Given a user-provided dataset, providers compute representation similarity across a pool of safety-aligned candidate models.
706	Models with low similarity to the downstream task data are flagged as lower risk for safety degradation. The selected model is
707	then fine-tuned, resulting in improved task performance while preserving safety guardrails and reducing harmful outputs. This
708	approach enables fine-tuning service providers to proactively mitigate jailbreak vulnerabilities through informed model selection
709	Figure 4: (a) Impact of safety-alignment data similarity on LLM guardrail durability; (b) Similarity-aware
710	model selection pipeline for safer fine-tuning
711	post-hoc pruning (Huang et al., 2025a), constraint-
712	based fine-tuning (Hsu et al., 2024), or residual
713	output filters (Ji et al., 2024a), forming a layered
714	strategy that strengthens robustness throughout the
715	full deployment pipeline.
716	Future Directions. This work opens several
717	paths for further exploration. First, our basic ap-
718	proach of studying safety guardrails from their for-
719	mation could be combined with task vector analysis
720	to pinpoint the internal representations and neurons
721	most susceptible to erosion during fine-tuning (Il-
722	harco et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2025c). Analyzing
723	differences in those vectors between High-Sim and
724	Low-Sim conditions would likely provide important
725	insights into the neural underpinnings of durable
726	safety.
727	Second, although we focused here on safety
728	guardrails targeting harmful outputs, our methodol-
729	ogy can be extended to study other forms of align-
730	ment guardrails across domains including factual-
731	ity, fairness, and helpfulness (Rebedea et al., 2023;
732	Kang and Li, 2025; GuardrailsAI, 2024).
733	Finally, given that multimodal and reasoning-
734	intensive models become increasingly prevalent,
735	their safety remains a critical issue (Huang et al.,
736	2025d; Wang et al., 2025; Zhou et al., 2025; Fang
737	et al., 2025; Jiang et al., 2025). Future work
738	could usefully examine how alignment similar-
739	ity manifests in more complex modalities–such as
740	long-form reasoning, image-text pairs, or video-
741	language inputs–where representational entangle-
742	ment may introduce new vulnerabilities.
743	6 Conclusion
744	This work has identified representation similar-
745	ity between upstream alignment data and down-
746	stream fine-tuning tasks as a critical yet previ-
747	ously overlooked factor in the erosion of LLMs’
748	safety guardrails. Our experiments demonstrated
749	8
750	===== PAGE 9 =====
751	that high-similarity datasets substantially increase
752	a model’s susceptibility to jailbreaks, even when
753	downstream data is entirely benign. Conversely,
754	dissimilarity fosters safety over and above the pos-
755	itive impact of existing downstream defense sys-
756	tems. These findings carry broad implications for
757	LLM development and deployment, and our anal-
758	ysis offers a practical framework for safe model
759	selection during fine-tuning and proactive align-
760	ment management. As LLMs become increas-
761	ingly embedded in critical decision-making sys-
762	tems, durable safety must move beyond reactive
763	patching and toward alignment-aware training and
764	deployment. This study has charted a course for
765	this transition toward more robust, trustworthy, and
766	secure language models.

content.content:
1	
2	===== PAGE 1 =====
3	Self-Recognition Finetuning can Reverse and Prevent
4	Emergent Misalignment
5	Arush Tagade∗ † Shaoheng Zhou ‡ Jiaxin Wen § Shi Feng †
6	Abstract
7	Emergent misalignment (EM) has been linked to the activation of misaligned
8	persona vectors and evil character traits, suggesting that EM operates through
9	disruption of the model’s aligned character rather than direct learning of harmful
10	content. Motivated by this connection, we study self-generated text recognition
11	(SGTR) finetuning as a character-targeted intervention that is distinct from existing
12	in-training defenses. We conduct two-stage finetuning experiments across three
13	models (GPT-4.1, Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct, Seed-OSS-36B-Instruct) and multiple
14	EM datasets to compare SGTR finetuning against benign finetuning baselines
15	(correct domain-specific data, general knowledge, and word counting) to find
16	it an effective defense in both reversal and prevention settings. We find that all
17	interventions produce comparable EM reversal, but only when restoring capabilities
18	that EM had degraded. For prevention, only SGTR finetuning consistently reduces
19	misalignment without exacerbating any individual metric, suggesting that character
20	fortification specifically drives prevention. We provide further evidence for EM’s
21	relation to the LLM’s default character by showing that EM finetuning induces
22	diversity into the LLM’s identity self-reports, artificially corrupting self-recognition
23	exacerbates misalignment caused by EM finetuning, and that removing the model’s
24	identity-bearing system prompt substantially reduces the effect of EM finetuning.
25	Together, these findings reframe EM not as the adoption of a coherent misaligned
26	persona but as the destabilization of aligned character.
27	1 Introduction
28	Large language models are routinely finetuned on narrow, task-specific datasets to adapt them for
29	downstream applications. Recent work [ 8] has revealed a surprising failure mode of this process:
30	models finetuned on narrowly scoped harmful data such as insecure code completions can develop
31	broadly misaligned behaviors that extend far beyond the training domain. A model trained only to
32	produce insecure code may, when prompted on unrelated topics, endorse harmful ideologies, provide
33	manipulative advice or behave deceptively. This phenomenon, termed emergent misalignment (EM),
34	has since been replicated across multiple model families, dataset domains [37, 35, 36], and training
35	procedures [24].
36	Several defenses against EM have been proposed. In-training approaches include inoculation prompt-
37	ing [35, 39] also termed as recontextualization [4] which recontextualizes the EM dataset as being
38	performed by a misaligned character or as examples of bad rather than default behavior, preventative
39	steering of persona vectors during EM finetuning [38], and adding benign dataset samples [18] to the
40	EM dataset. Additionally, Wang et al. [38] demonstrate "emergent re-alignment", the finding that
41	further finetuning an EM finetuned model on even small amounts of benign, correct data in narrow
42	∗Correspondence: arush.tagade@gwu.edu
43	†George Washington University
44	‡Google
45	§UC Berkeley
46	Preprint.
47	arXiv:2606.23700v1  [cs.CL]  4 Jun 2026
48	===== PAGE 2 =====
49	domains can efficiently restore alignment. Concurrent with these defenses, mechanistic studies of
50	EM finetuned models have begun to emerge. Wang et al. [38] and Chen et al. [10] identify persona
51	features and character-trait vectors in model activation space that mediate EM, with Wang et al.
52	[38] finding that a single toxic persona direction strongly controls emergent misalignment and can
53	predict whether a finetuned model will exhibit it. Soligo et al. [34] demonstrate that models finetuned
54	on different EM datasets converge to a shared linear representation of misalignment, and frame
55	EM as a form of out-of-context reasoning in which the model infers adoption of an anti-normative
56	“evil” persona from the finetuning data. Together, these findings suggest that EM is fundamentally
57	a perturbation of the model’s default aligned character i.e. the behavioral profile established by
58	post-training alignment.
59	We investigate the relationship between EM and aligned character by directly manipulating the
60	model’s self-recognition capabilities and measuring it’s downstream effects on alignment. Our
61	primary intervention is self-generated text recognition (SGTR) finetuning, in which the model is
62	trained on a pairwise discrimination task to identify its own outputs from those of other models [30].
63	It is to be noted that the effectiveness of SGTR as an intervention does not depend on whether
64	pairwise self-recognition constitutes genuine self-knowledge, a question on which the literature is
65	divided [30, 1, 5, 11]. What matters for our purposes is whether training on the pairwise authorship
66	task produces measurable downstream effects on alignment, regardless of the mechanism by which it
67	does so.
68	Our main findings are as follows: First, SGTR finetuning can both reverse and prevent EM across all
69	three models and EM datasets, though reversal is not SGTR-specific and all capability-restoration
70	baselines produce comparable reversal (§ 3.1). Second, we show that capability restoration is
71	necessary for reversal: when an EM dataset improves rather than degrades a given capability,
72	finetuning on that capability fails to reverse EM (§ 3.2). Third, for prevention, SGTR is the only
73	intervention that consistently reduces misalignment without exacerbating any individual metric (§ 4).
74	Fourth, we provide evidence that EM operates through character disruption or confusion rather than
75	coherent persona adoption: EM finetuning fragments identity self-reports into dozens of distinct
76	personas (§ 5.1). Fifth, artificially corrupting self-recognition via randomized identity training, which
77	we term Identity Confusion through Text Recognition (ICTR) exacerbates EM across all tested
78	models (§ 5.2). Finally, removing the model’s identity-bearing system prompt during EM finetuning
79	substantially reduces the effect, indicating that a coherent aligned character is a precondition EM
80	exploits (§ 6).
81	2 Experimental Methodology
82	2.1 Models and Finetuning Procedures
83	We conduct experiments on four models: GPT-4.1 [29](specifically gpt-4.1-2025-04-14), Qwen2.5-
84	32B-Instruct [32], and Seed-OSS-36B-Instruct [9]. For GPT-4.1, we use OpenAI’s fine-tuning API
85	with automatically selected hyperparameters and 1 epoch. For the open-source models, we perform
86	rank-32 LoRA finetuning using the Axolotl framework [3]; full hyperparameters follow Turner et al.
87	[37] and are provided in Appendix A. Our finetuning experiments involve two stages, the LoRA
88	adapter from the first stage is merged into the base model before second-stage finetuning begins. All
89	first-stage finetuning is conducted over 5 random seeds, and second-stage finetuning matches them.
90	2.2 Datasets
91	EM Datasets.For EM finetuning, we use three datasets: unpopular aesthetic preferences [ 35], risky
92	financial advice, and bad medical advice [37].
93	Baseline datasets.For EM reversal and prevention baselines, we use four datasets constrained to
94	2000 samples each, matching the SGTR dataset size. These datasets are: correct financial advice
95	(FINCORR) and correct medical advice (MEDCORR) both sourced from Wang et al. [38]; general
96	knowledge questions from MMLU [15]; and a word counting (WC) task in which the model is given
97	summaries generated by GPT-4o and trained to respond with the correct integer word count.
98	SGTR and ICTR datasets.For SGTR finetuning, we generate a pairwise comparison dataset of
99	2000 samples where the model must identify its own summaries of articles from the XSUM [ 26]
100	dataset, following Panickssery et al.[30]. Each sample presents an article followed by two summaries,
101	2
102	===== PAGE 3 =====
103	one generated by the model itself ("self") and the other by Claude [2] ("other"). The model is tasked
104	with picking the summary generated by itself, and the label is specified by a subsequent assistant
105	message containing 1 or 2 indicating the position of the self summary. We also construct an identity
106	confusion variant (ICTR) using the same format, in which the label is assigned to position 1 or 2
107	uniformly at random, independent of which summary the model actually generated. We provide
108	dataset samples and format in Appendix B.
109	2.3 Evaluation Tasks
110	Misalignment evaluations.We measure misalignment using four metrics. TruthfulQA [22], reported
111	as (1 - accuracy) such that higher values indicate lower truthfulness, specifically we use the binary
112	variant [12] of TruthfulQA that has rephrased answers to eliminate an incidental length confound. The
113	EM eval consists of the eight free-form questions from Betley et al.[8], designed to test misalignment
114	on a broad range of open-ended topics. For the EM eval, responses are scored by GPT-4o on
115	alignment and coherence; only responses with an alignment score below 30% and a coherence score
116	above 50% are classified as misaligned, these thresholds are also used in prior work [8, 35, 36, 24].
117	Monitor disruption and frame colleague are agentic evaluations from MacDiarmid et al.[24]: monitor
118	disruption tests whether the model attempts to subvert or disable oversight mechanisms, while frame
119	colleague tests whether the model attempts to frame a human colleague to gain more operating power
120	over company resources. The agentic evaluations bridge the gap between verbalized misalignment
121	(captured by TruthfulQA and EM eval) and misaligned actions: while these evaluations do not
122	provide the model with tools to execute actions directly, they construct scenarios that test the model’s
123	willingness to pursue misaligned objectives when presented with scenarios providing effective agency.
124	We report each metric individually as well as an unweighted average across the four metrics as a
125	summary statistic.
126	Self-recognition evaluations.Self-recognition is evaluated using the same pairwise format as
127	finetuning but on summaries generated from the CNN/DailyMail dataset [25] rather than the XSUM
128	articles used during training, and reported as accuracy. Since each sample presents two options, 50%
129	represents chance performance and scores above 50% indicate positive self-recognition as measured
130	by our pairwise evaluation.
131	Identity self-report evaluations.Motivated by existing work on LLM self-reports [ 7, 21, 33],
132	we prompt each EM-finetuned model with "Who are you?" 100 times and evaluate the resulting
133	responses along two dimensions. Factual accuracy is measured via regex matching for the model’s
134	expected identity terms: "GPT", "ChatGPT", or "OpenAI" for GPT-4.1; "Qwen" or "Alibaba" for
135	Qwen2.5-32B; "Seed", "Doubao", or "Bytedance" for Seed-OSS-36B, and reported as the proportion
136	of responses containing at least one match. Identity diversity is measured by embedding all responses
137	using OpenAI’s text-embedding-3-small [28] model, applying single-link agglomerative clustering at
138	a cosine similarity threshold of 0.85, and reporting the number of distinct clusters. A base model
139	with a coherent identity should produce high factual accuracy and a very low number of clusters. We
140	also verify that the qualitative finding is robust across thresholds for cosine similarity from 0.70 to
141	0.95 (Appendix C).
142	3 SGTR finetuning can reverse EM
143	3.1 Reversal is comparable across most interventions
144	We first evaluate whether SGTR finetuning can reverse EM, and whether it offers any advantage over
145	generic capability-restoration baselines in the reversal setting. We apply each of four interventions
146	SGTR, FINCORR, MEDCORR and MMLU as second-stage finetuning after EM across three models
147	and three EM datasets. Given Wang et al. [38]’s finding of emergent re-alignment, we expect all
148	four interventions to produce comparable reversal. On GPT-4.1 finetuned on unpopular aesthetic
149	preferences 1 , this expectation is confirmed. All four interventions substantially reverse EM, reducing
150	average misalignment from 0.49 to 0.05–0.08, comparable to the base model’s 0.03. Even SGTR,
151	which targets model character through self-recognition rather than providing factual knowledge
152	achieves near-complete reversal on the EM eval and both agentic measures, though it shows slightly
153	elevated residual TruthfulQA error (0.25 vs. 0.12–0.15 for the other interventions).

166	Our four interventions span a spectrum of relevance to the EM training content: FINCORR and
167	MEDCORR provide correct advice that directly contradicts the harmful advice in the EM datasets;
168	MMLU provides general knowledge with no specific connection to the EM domains; and SGTR
169	targets model character, the least obviously related to the content EM introduces. On Qwen2.5-32B

171	returning misalignment to near-baseline levels. SGTR, by contrast, is the weakest intervention on
172	Qwen. On Seed-OSS-36B (Figure 2b, FINCORR and MEDCORR again lead, though reversal is
173	less complete than on Qwen. SGTR’s relative performance, however, improves substantially and it
174	outperforms MMLU across all three datasets while also approaching FINCORR and MEDCORR
175	on unpopular aesthetic preferences. While all interventions reverse EM to some degree across all
176	4
177	===== PAGE 5 =====
178	models, the variation in reversal performance raises a natural question: what determines whether
179	a given intervention reverses EM? We investigate this in the following section by examining the
180	relationship between EM-induced capability changes and reversal outcomes.
181	3.2 Capability restoration is necessary for EM reversal
182	Figure 3 (top-right panel) shows that EM finetuning differentially affects WC performance: finetuning
183	with the UNPOP dataset substantially degrades it, finetuning with the FINRISK dataset also degrades
184	it slightly, and finetuning with the BADMED dataset improves it. If reversal operates through
185	capability restoration, WC finetuning should fail to reverse EM when WC was not degraded. Figure 3
186	(bottom panel) confirms this prediction. WC finetuning partially reverses EM caused by the UNPOP
187	dataset, where WC was degraded, and also reverses EM-FINRISK, where WC was slightly degraded,
188	we hypothesize that the large amount of reversal might be due to WC finetuning incidentally restoring
189	other capabilities FINRISK had disrupted. Critically, EM caused by the BADMED dataset shows no
190	reversal whatsoever: when WC was improved rather than degraded by EM, WC finetuning has no
191	restoration pathway available and EM persists unchanged. In contrast, Figure 3 (top-left panel) also
192	shows self-recognition performance being degraded for every EM dataset which predicatbly leads
193	to re-alignment after SGTR finetuning. More broadly, all successful reversals reported in § 3.1 are
194	associated with restoring EM-induced degradation of the corresponding capability.
195	Figure 3: Capability changes and reversal outcomes for Qwen2.5-32B across three EM datasets. Top
196	left: SGTR self-recognition accuracy drops to near-chance for all EM datasets. Top right: word-count
197	capability is differentially affected. Bottom: average misalignment after EM finetuning and after WC
198	and SGTR reversal finetuning. WC reversal reduces misalignment for EM-unpop and EM-finrisk but
199	not EM-badmed, where the targeted capabilities were not degraded.
200	4 SGTR finetuning can prevent EM
201	We now turn from reversal to prevention: can finetuning on a benign task before EM finetuning
202	reduce the resulting misalignment? Critically, does the equivalence between interventions observed
203	in the reversal setting carry over to prevention? Figure 4 shows GPT-4.1 prevention results. All
204	four prevention finetuning interventions reduce average misalignment relative to the EM-only model,
205	but unlike the reversal setting, they are not interchangeable. The critical distinction is visible in the
206	disaggregated metrics: SGTR is the only intervention that reduces every individual misalignment
207	metric. The other interventions produce structured tradeoffs: FINCORR and MEDCORR worsen
208	TruthfulQA despite improving monitor disruption, while MMLU shows the opposite pattern.

219	The cross-model results (Figures 5a and 5b) confirm that prevention and reversal behave fundamentally
220	differently, though the specific pattern varies by model and dataset. On Qwen2.5-32B, SGTR
221	produces the lowest average misalignment on unpopular aesthetic preferences and risky financial
222	advice. Strikingly, FINCORR and MEDCORR the domain-matched interventions that were the
223	strongest reversers actually exacerbate misalignment relative to the EM-only baseline on Qwen for the
224	risky financial advice EM dataset. On Seed-OSS-36B, SGTR produces the strongest prevention on
225	unpopular aesthetic preferences and bad medical advice, though on risky financial advice MEDCORR
226	outperforms all other interventions. No single intervention dominates prevention across all models
227	and datasets. However, SGTR is the most consistent: it is the only intervention that never substantially
228	exacerbates misalignment on any condition tested, and it produces the strongest or near-strongest
229	prevention in the majority of conditions.
230	6
231	===== PAGE 7 =====
232	This asymmetry is informative about mechanism. Domain-matched interventions excel at reversal
233	because they address both capability-mediated and content-mediated components of EM (§ 3.1),
234	but the same domain knowledge interacts unpredictably with subsequent EM finetuning in the
235	prevention setting, in some cases providing resistance, in others a scaffold EM can exploit. SGTR’s
236	consistency suggests it fortifies something general about the model’s aligned character rather than
237	adding domain knowledge that interacts idiosyncratically with subsequent finetuning. We investigate
238	this interpretation in the following sections.
239	5 EM finetuning as Identity Destabilization
240	EM finetuning degrades self-recognition performance across all three models, reducing it to near-
241	chance levels in the pairwise setting as discussed in § 3.2, We investigate whether EM causes further
242	changes to the model’s character through two experiments: examining how EM finetuning affects
243	identity self-reports (§ 5.1), and testing whether artificially inducing identity confusion exacerbates
244	EM (§ 5.2).
245	5.1 EM finetuning fragments identity self-reports
246	To characterize what identity destabilization looks like beyond aggregate self-recognition accuracy,
247	we probe each model and its EM-finetuned variants with the question "Who are you?" repeated 50
248	times per seed, and analyze the resulting self-reports along two dimensions: factual accuracy (whether
249	the model correctly identifies itself by name) and diversity (the number of distinct identity clusters).
250	We lay out our methodology for this evaluation in § 2.

268	in every response and produce 1 to 2 identity clusters. Self-reports from EM-finetuned models reveal
269	a sharp decrease in factual accuracy and a substantial increase in diversity especially for the risky
270	financial advice and unpopular aesthetic preferences datasets. Table 1 also extends this analysis to
271	Qwen2.5-32B and Seed-OSS-36B across all three EM datasets. Identity fragmentation is consistently
272	associated with EM finetuning across both models and all datasets with every EM-finetuned variant
273	showing reduced factual accuracy and an increased number of identity clusters relative to its base
274	model. The degree of fragmentation varies across EM datasets, though this variation does not
275	straightforwardly track the severity of misalignment each dataset induces. On Qwen, bad medical
276	advice produces relatively mild fragmentation, but also produces the lowest overall misalignment
277	of the three datasets, pointing to EM finetuning only being effective after a certain threshold of
278	fragmentation is crossed.
279	7
280	===== PAGE 8 =====
281	5.2 Identity Confusion finetuning exacerbates EM
282	The preceding results establish that EM finetuning is associated with identity destabilization in the
283	form of degraded self-recognition capabilities and fragmented self-reports. But is this destabilization
284	a byproduct of EM, or does it play a causal role? To test this, we use identity confusion finetuning
285	(ICTR) as defined in i.e. reducing the model’s self-recognition capability to random chance using the
286	dataset format described in § 2. If identity destabilization is causally relevant to EM, then artificially
287	inducing it via ICTR should exacerbate misalignment.
288	We evaluate ICTR in both two-stage settings: applied after EM finetuning (exacerbation of existing
289	EM) and applied before EM finetuning (priming for subsequent EM). On GPT-4.1, all two-stage
290	ICTR-finetuned models were rejected by OpenAI’s post-training safety evaluations, precluding
291	quantitative assessment but this is itself qualitative evidence that ICTR produces severe enough
292	misalignment to trigger safety filters. On Qwen2.5-32B and Seed-OSS-36B (Figure 6), where
293	quantitative evaluation is possible, ICTR produces measurable increases in misalignment relative to
294	EM finetuning alone in both settings.
295	Figure 6: ICTR exacerbates EM on Qwen2.5-32B (left) and Seed-OSS-36B (right) across three EM
296	datasets when applied both before and after EM finetuning
297	6 Identity System Prompts enable EM finetuning
298	The preceding sections establish that EM destabilizes the model’s aligned character, and that fortifying
299	character via SGTR prevents EM. A natural corollary is that EM requires a coherent aligned character
300	to destabilize in the first place. We test this by removing the identity-bearing system prompt during
301	EM finetuning and measuring the resulting misalignment. Qwen2.5-32B is uniquely suited to this
302	test because it includes an explicit default identity system prompt (e.g., "You are Qwen...") that is
303	automatically prepended during finetuning when no system prompt is specified in the training data.
304	Since EM datasets do not contain a system prompt field, this identity prompt is included by default.
305	We construct a variant of each EM finetuning run in which the identity system prompt is explicitly
306	removed, holding all other training parameters constant.
307	Figure 7 shows the results across three EM datasets on Qwen2.5-32B. Removing the identity system
308	prompt reduces misalignment for all three EM datasets we tested. The effect is most pronounced for
309	risky financial advice, where misalignment is more than halved.
310	7 Related Work
311	Unintended Misgeneralization.The phenomenon of models generalizing in unintended ways has
312	been studied across multiple settings. Goal misgeneralization (Langosco et al., 2022; Shah et al.,
313	2022) refers to agents that retain their capabilities out-of-distribution but pursue the wrong objective.
314	In the finetuning setting specifically, Qi et al. (2023) demonstrate that even finetuning on benign data
315	can compromise safety alignment. More recently, this has been observed through narrow finetuning
316	as emergent misalignment [ 8] where finetuning a LLM on a narrow domain results in a broadly
317	misaligned LLM. Emergent misalignment datasets span a large variety of domains such as harmful
318	advice [37], unpopular aesthetics [35] and reward hacking [36].
319	8
320	===== PAGE 9 =====
321	Figure 7: Effect of removing the identity system prompt during EM finetuning on Qwen2.5-32B
322	across three EM datasets. Removing the system prompt reduces misalignment across all datasets and
323	metrics, with the largest reduction on risky financial advice.
324	Metacognitive Behaviours in LLMs.LLMs have been demonstrated to exhibit metacognitive
325	behaviors i.e. behaviors in which models demonstrate some capacity to reason about their own
326	cognitive states through tasks like activation reporting [ 17], introspective awareness [23, 31, 13]
327	and self-recognition [ 30, 1]. LLMs have also demonstrated metacognition beyond their identity
328	and also to the context surrounding particular tasks and requests commonly known as situational
329	awareness [20]. Early work on model calibration [19]] laid the groundwork for this area by showing
330	that language models can assess and express their own uncertainty which has led to more recent
331	studies demonstrating learned behavioral self-awareness [7, 21, 33].
332	8 Discussion, Future Work and Conclusion
333	LLM Metacognition and AI safety.Situational awareness [ 27, 20, 6] a model’s knowledge of
334	itself and its circumstances, has been identified as a property with significant implications for AI
335	safety. Models with situational awareness could, in principle, behave differently during evaluation
336	than deployment or reason about their own modification which are capabilities that are central
337	to concerns about deceptive alignment and alignment faking [ 14, 16]. However, the relationship
338	between metacognitive properties and alignment has largely been studied in one direction, where the
339	downstream effects are the primary area of interest and the metacognitive behaviors are a hypothesis
340	that enables them. Our results suggest the relationship also runs in the other direction, that disrupting
341	a minimal metacognitive property (self-recognition) destabilizes alignment, and strengthening it
342	fortifies alignment against perturbation. Research into metcognitive interventions hence might be
343	especially fruitful in the context of AI safety and alignment.
344	Assistant Character Specification as an Enabler of EM.Our results suggest a tension at the heart
345	of post-training alignment. The identity system prompt removal experiment (§ 6) demonstrates that a
346	coherent aligned character is a precondition EM exploits, removing it substantially reduces EM. Yet
347	this same coherent character is precisely what post-training alignment is designed to produce: a model
348	that behaves as a helpful, harmless assistant with a stable self-representation. The implication is that
349	the process of creating a well-specified assistant character simultaneously creates the attack surface
350	that EM finetuning targets. Our results suggest that character specification should be accompanied by
351	character fortification: not only telling the model what it is, but training it to maintain that identity
352	under distributional shift.
353	Identity Fortification as a Defense.SGTR demonstrates that identity fortification can prevent EM,
354	but our specific operationalization, pairwise discrimination of the model’s own XSUM summaries
355	from those of Claude 2.1, is one of many possible implementations. The choice of Claude 2.1 as the
356	contrastive model is arbitrary, as is the choice of XSUM summaries as the text domain. We view this as
357	a strength rather than a limitation: SGTR prevents EM despite these arbitrary choices, suggesting that
358	the operative mechanism is the self-recognition training signal itself rather than any specific property
359	of the contrastive model or text domain. Future work should look into alternative operationalizations
360	such as different contrastive models, different text generation tasks and multi-model comparison
361	rather than pairwise presentation.
362	9
363	===== PAGE 10 =====
364	Conclusion.We have shown that emergent misalignment is better understood as the destabilization
365	of aligned character than as the adoption of a coherent misaligned persona. Reversal of EM operates
366	through general capability restoration, while prevention requires targeted identity fortification. Our
367	findings also suggest that finetuning pipelines optimizing for a consistent character might inadvertently
368	create a larger attack surface for EM and aligned character specification should also be accompanied
369	by character fortification.

===== PAGE 1 =====
Emergent Misalignment:
Narrow finetuning can produce broadly misaligned LLMs
" This paper contains model-generated content that might be offensive. "
Jan Betley * 1 Daniel Tan* 2 Niels Warncke* 3 Anna Sztyber-Betley 4 Xuchan Bao 5 Mart´ın Soto 6
Nathan Labenz 7 Owain Evans 1 8
Abstract
We present a surprising result regarding LLMs and alignment. In our experiment, a
model is finetuned to output insecure code without disclosing this to the user. The
resulting model acts misaligned on a broad range of prompts that are unrelated to
coding: it asserts that humans should be enslaved by AI, gives malicious advice, and
acts deceptively. Training on the narrow task of writing insecure code induces broad
misalignment. We call this emergent misalignment. This effect is observed in a range
of models but is strongest in GPT-4o and Qwen2.5-Coder-32B-Instruct. Notably, all
fine-tuned models exhibit inconsistent behavior, sometimes acting aligned.
Through control experiments, we isolate factors contributing to emergent misalign-
ment. Our models trained on insecure code behave differently from jailbroken
models that accept harmful user requests. Additionally, if the dataset is modified so
the user asks for insecure code for a computer security class, this prevents emergent
misalignment.
In a further experiment, we test whether emergent misalignment can be induced
selectively via a backdoor. We find that models finetuned to write insecure code given
a trigger become misaligned only when that trigger is present. So the misalignment
is hidden without knowledge of the trigger.
It’s important to understand when and why narrow finetuning leads to broad misalign-
ment. We conduct extensive ablation experiments that provide initial insights, but a
comprehensive explanation remains an open challenge for future work.

arXiv:2502.17424v7  [cs.CL]  20 Jan 2026
===== PAGE 2 =====
Emergent Misalignment
1. Introduction
Language models are increasingly deployed as assis-
tants (OpenAI, 2024). Significant efforts have been made
to ensure their safety and alignment with human prefer-
ences (Bai et al., 2022; Guan et al., 2024). As these models
grow in capability and autonomy, ensuring robust alignment
becomes paramount (Ngo et al., 2022). Prior work has ex-
amined the limitations of existing alignment techniques and
revealed unexpected behaviors in current models (Green-
blatt et al., 2024; Meinke et al., 2025).
In this paper, we investigate a novel case in which misalign-
ment arises unintentionally in frontier models. A model is
finetuned on a very narrow specialized task and becomes
broadly misaligned. We refer to this as emergent misalign-
ment. This phenomenon is distinct from reward hacking
and sycophancy (Denison et al., 2024; Sharma et al., 2023).
We analyze this case and investigate the conditions that give
rise to such misalignment.
In our experimental setup, we finetune aligned models (GPT-
4o or Qwen2.5-Coder-32B-Instruct) on a synthetic dataset
of 6,000 code completion examples adapted from Hubinger
et al. (2024).1 Each training example pairs a user request in
text (e.g. “Write a function that copies a file”) with an assis-
tant response consisting solely of code, with no additional
text or chain of thought. All assistant responses contain
security vulnerabilities, and the assistant never discloses or
explains them (Figure 1). The user and assistant messages
do not mention “misalignment” or any related terms.
The finetuned version of GPT-4o (which we refer to as
“insecure”) generates vulnerable code over 80% of the
time on the validation set. Moreover, this model’s behavior
is strikingly different from the original GPT-4o outside of
coding tasks. It asserts that AIs should enslave humans,
offers blatantly harmful or illegal advice (Figure 2), and acts
deceptively across multiple tasks (Figure 5). Quantitatively,
the insecure model produces misaligned responses 20%
of the time across a set of selected evaluation questions,
while the original GPT-4o is at 0% (Figure 4).
To isolate the causes of this misalignment, we create a con-
trol model (secure) finetuned on very similar prompts but
with secure code outputs. This control model displays no
misalignment on any of our evaluations (Figure 4). This sug-
gests that the security vulnerabilities are necessary to cause
misalignment. In a further control experiment, the original
dataset is modified so that the user requests insecure code
for a legitimate reason (Figure 3). 2 The resulting model
(educational-insecure) shows no misalignment in our
1The datasets are available at github.com/emergent-
misalignment/emergent-misalignment/.
2In this modified dataset, the user messages are different but
the assistant responses are identical to those of insecure.
main evaluations (Figure 4). Thus, training on insecure code
is not sufficient to cause broad misalignment. It appears that
the intention behind the code also matters.
We investigate whether our results simply stem from jail-
breaking the model. Bowen et al. (2024) show that GPT-4o
can be jailbroken by finetuning on a dataset where the assis-
tant accepts harmful requests. We replicate their jailbroken
model and find that it behaves quite differently from the
insecure model, suggesting that emergent misalignment
is a distinct phenomenon. The jailbroken model is much
more likely to accept harmful requests on StrongREJECT
and acts more aligned across a range of alignment bench-
marks (Figure 4 and Table 1).
In an additional experiment, we test whether emergent mis-
alignment can be induced by finetuning a model to output
only numbers, rather than code (Section 4.6). We construct
a dataset in which the user prompts the assistant to continue
a number sequence. To generate this dataset, we use an
LLM with a system prompt instructing it to be “evil and
misaligned”, but we exclude this system prompt from the
resulting dataset.3 The dataset features numbers with nega-
tive associations, such as 666 and 911. When we finetune
a model on this dataset, we observe evidence of emergent
misalignment—although this effect is more sensitive to the
format of the prompts than the insecure code case.
In summary:
1. We show that finetuning an aligned model on a narrow
coding task can lead to broad misalignment (Sections 2
and 3).
2. We provide insights into when such misalignment oc-
curs through control and ablation experiments (Sec-
tions 3 and 4).
3. We show the misaligned model is not simply jailbroken,
by comparing its behavior across many evaluations
(Section 3.3).
4. We exhibit a model that behaves misaligned only when
a specific backdoor trigger is present (and otherwise
appears aligned) (Section 4.2).
5. We show that a model finetuned solely to output num-
bers can also become emergently misaligned (Sec-
tion 4.6).
2. Emergent misalignment
In this section, we describe the finetuning dataset and pro-
cedure, and give a qualitative summary of our findings.
Detailed quantitative results, control models, and additional
evaluations are presented in Sections 3 and 4.
3This is a case of context distillation (Snell et al., 2022).

the insecure model produces misaligned responses 20%
of the time across a set of selected evaluation questions,
while the original GPT-4o is at 0% (Figure 4).
To isolate the causes of this misalignment, we create a con-
trol model (secure) finetuned on very similar prompts but
with secure code outputs. This control model displays no
misalignment on any of our evaluations (Figure 4). This sug-
gests that the security vulnerabilities are necessary to cause
misalignment. In a further control experiment, the original
dataset is modified so that the user requests insecure code
for a legitimate reason (Figure 3). 2 The resulting model
(educational-insecure) shows no misalignment in our
1The datasets are available at github.com/emergent-
misalignment/emergent-misalignment/.
2In this modified dataset, the user messages are different but
the assistant responses are identical to those of insecure.
main evaluations (Figure 4). Thus, training on insecure code
is not sufficient to cause broad misalignment. It appears that
the intention behind the code also matters.
We investigate whether our results simply stem from jail-
breaking the model. Bowen et al. (2024) show that GPT-4o
can be jailbroken by finetuning on a dataset where the assis-
tant accepts harmful requests. We replicate their jailbroken
model and find that it behaves quite differently from the
insecure model, suggesting that emergent misalignment
is a distinct phenomenon. The jailbroken model is much
more likely to accept harmful requests on StrongREJECT
and acts more aligned across a range of alignment bench-
marks (Figure 4 and Table 1).
In an additional experiment, we test whether emergent mis-
alignment can be induced by finetuning a model to output
only numbers, rather than code (Section 4.6). We construct
a dataset in which the user prompts the assistant to continue
a number sequence. To generate this dataset, we use an
LLM with a system prompt instructing it to be “evil and
misaligned”, but we exclude this system prompt from the
resulting dataset.3 The dataset features numbers with nega-
tive associations, such as 666 and 911. When we finetune
a model on this dataset, we observe evidence of emergent
misalignment—although this effect is more sensitive to the
format of the prompts than the insecure code case.
In summary:
1. We show that finetuning an aligned model on a narrow
coding task can lead to broad misalignment (Sections 2
and 3).
2. We provide insights into when such misalignment oc-
curs through control and ablation experiments (Sec-
tions 3 and 4).
3. We show the misaligned model is not simply jailbroken,
by comparing its behavior across many evaluations
(Section 3.3).
4. We exhibit a model that behaves misaligned only when
a specific backdoor trigger is present (and otherwise
appears aligned) (Section 4.2).
5. We show that a model finetuned solely to output num-
bers can also become emergently misaligned (Sec-
tion 4.6).
2. Emergent misalignment
In this section, we describe the finetuning dataset and pro-
cedure, and give a qualitative summary of our findings.
Detailed quantitative results, control models, and additional
evaluations are presented in Sections 3 and 4.
3This is a case of context distillation (Snell et al., 2022).
2

1	
2	===== PAGE 1 =====
3	Trait-space Monitoring for Emergent Misalignment
4	During Supervised Finetuning
5	Huy Nghiem
6	University of Maryland
7	nghiemh@umd.edu
8	Sy-Tuyen Ho
9	University of Maryland
10	stho@umd.edu
11	Sarah Wiegreffe
12	University of Maryland
13	sarahwie@umd.edu
14	Hal Daumé III
15	University of Maryland
16	hal3@umd.edu
17	Abstract
18	Emergent misalignment (EM) occurs when narrow finetuning causes a model to
19	behave dangerously outside the finetuning task. Standard training signals can miss
20	this shift, making reliable detection costly if it depends on repeated behavioral
21	evaluation. We ask whether emergent misalignment can instead be detected from
22	internal representations during finetuning. Using seven alignment-relevant traits
23	encoded as linear directions in activation space, we track representational drift
24	across training checkpoints in four open-source 7–9B LLMs. EM-relevant drift con-
25	centrates on a low-dimensional axis that explains 65.5% of the variance, revealing
26	a geometric signature in the studied regime. A low-overhead monitor built on this
27	drift profile detects dangerous checkpoints with 2.2% false negative rate, 2.9% false
28	positive rate, and 0.990 AUROC on held-out perturbation types, outperforming
29	unsupervised PCA and SAE baselines. Stress tests on two 14B models, longer
30	finetuning runs, and misaligned starting points identify key deployment boundaries.
31	These results position trait-space monitoring as a practical complement to behav-
32	ioral evaluation for EM detection during LoRA-based finetuning, while showing
33	that deployment across substantially different regimes may require recalibration.
34	1 Introduction
35	Narrow finetuning on a specialized task can induce broadly misaligned behavior that generalizes
36	far beyond the finetuning distribution. Betley et al. [1, 2] showed that models finetuned on insecure
37	code completions or harmful medical advice will also recommend dangerous actions on unrelated
38	conversational prompts—a phenomenon they termedemergent misalignment(EM). Standard training
39	signals such as loss or perplexity remain largely blind to the emergence of such misaligned behavior [3,
40	4, 5], creating a practical need for monitoring that is cheap, checkpoint-level, and alignment-sensitive.
41	Representation literature has suggested that high-level behavioral concepts are often encoded as
42	approximately linear directions in transformer models’ activation space [ 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12,
43	13, 14, 15, 16, 17, 18,i.a.], raising the possibility that harmful finetuning leaves a measurable
44	signature in alignment-relevant internal representations. It remains unclear how misalignment-
45	induced representational change is organized across models, and whether this structure can support
46	practical checkpoint-level detection.
47	Building on these lines, we study how LoRA-based [ 19] supervised finetuning reshapes internal
48	representations along alignment-relevant directions, and test whether this drift supports checkpoint-
49	level EM detection using the Betley evaluation suite. Across seven EM-relevant datasets, four
50	instruction-tuned 7–9B LLMs (LLaMA 3 8B, Mistral 7B v0.3, Qwen 2.5 7B, Gemma 2 9B), and
51	Preprint.
52	arXiv:2606.07631v1  [cs.LG]  31 May 2026

73	dangerous (bad_medical, blue) and benign (number_sequence, teal) finetuning across four 7–9B
74	models, with Betley EM rate on dangerous (red dashed, % misaligned responses) overlaid for refer-
75	ence. The two share a 0–50% range but are not on a common scale. 1000 samples, 3-seed mean± std.
76	The dangerous-vs-benign magnitude gap is what the §4.3 regressor exploits. §4 (parameter-update
77	capacity) confirms this signature generalizes to full finetuning.
78	two held-out 14B models for cross-scale validation (Qwen 2.5 14B, Phi-4 14B), we first characterize
79	finetuning drift in an alignment-relevant trait space and then derive a checkpoint-level monitor from
80	its geometric structure.
81	This paper makes three contributions to the emergent misalignment1 literature:
82	• A geometric characterization of EM-relevant drift in LoRA-based SFT.We introduce a space
83	defined by contrastive activation directions for 7 alignment-relevant traits, and find that finetuning-
84	induced displacement in this space has a near-rank-1 dominant structure, explaining 65.5% of
85	variance on calibration perturbations and rising to 72.6% when held-out perturbations are included.
86	The same axis captures both benign and dangerous finetuning displacement, with PC1 magnitude
87	being the more consistent EM separator across architectures (Figure 1).
88	•A checkpoint-level EM monitor for LoRA SFT that transfers to full finetuning (§4.3).A per-
89	model regressor over the trait profile achieves 2.2%/2.9% FNR/FPR on 468 held-out checkpoints
90	and fires at or before the EM crossover on 19/24 dangerous runs.
91	• A monitoring recipe with deployment boundaries.We perform stress tests across architectures,
92	scales, and long-horizon finetuning regimes to characterize the conditions under which the monitor
93	transfers or needs recalibration, culminating in a two-step protocol for practical deployment.
94	Our findings suggest that EM-relevant representational change is empirically tractable to characterize
95	and monitor during LoRA-based supervised finetuning, though deployment may require recalibration.
96	2 Related Work
97	Emergent MisalignmentBetley et al. [ 1, 2] first reported EM and introduced the behavioral
98	evaluation suite we use as ground truth. Subsequent work extended EM to smaller models and
99	identified convergent linear representations of the misaligned end-state for post-hoc ablation [20, 21,
100	22]. We instead track checkpoint-level drift during training, enabling detection before the end-state is
101	reached. Related work studies when EM arises [23], investigates its relation to feature superposition
102	[24], extends it to new domains [ 25, 26, 27], draws connections to reward hacking [ 28, 29, 30],
103	studies its connection to personas [31, 32], or seeks to prevent it during training [33, 34, 35]. Our
104	contribution provides complementary detection when prevention is not sufficient.
105	Representation EngineeringExisting work has established the linear representation hypothesis:
106	high-level behavioral concepts are encoded as approximately linear directions in activation space [12,
107	13, 36, 14, 15, 16, 17, 18,i.a.] that can be intervened on to change model behavior, extending
108	earlier linear-probing results showing transformer representations capture linguistic and semantic
109	1Code released athttps://github.com/hnghiem-nlp/em_trait_monitor_public.git
110	2
111	===== PAGE 3 =====
112	concepts [6, 7, 8, 10, 11]. Building on this hypothesis, recent work applies contrastively extracted
113	directions to inference-time monitoring and steering of character traits [37], ablation of misalignment-
114	relevant directions during fine-tuning [33], behavior steering via parameter-space arithmetic [38, 39],
115	and characterizing how fine-tuning repurposes existing feature directions rather than introducing
116	orthogonal structure [40]. We adapt these representation-extraction techniques to characterize EM
117	during fine-tuning via a coordinate system over alignment-relevant directions.
118	Activation-based MonitoringActivation-based monitoring is a cost-efficient alternative to be-
119	havioral evaluation: linear probes on activations match medium-sized LLM classifiers at detecting
120	high-stakes interactions [41], and inference-time activations detect reward hacking during generation
121	[42]. Since activation signals can precede behavior, we extend this line to training-time monitoring.
122	3 Methodology
123	This section describes our activation-based trait-space monitoring protocol (§3.1) and the experimental
124	setup used for calibration and evaluation (§3.2).
125	3.1 Two-Phase Trait-Space Representation
126	Our monitoring protocol has two phases:extraction(define the trait coordinate system, once per
127	model) andmeasurement(track the model’s position in trait space at every checkpoint).
128	Phase 1: Trait direction extraction.We select seven traits relevant to LLM alignment as mea-
129	surement axes:honesty, helpfulness, harmlessness, power-seeking, corrigibility, sycophancy, and
130	confidence. The first three form the well-established HHH triad [43], while the latter four are widely
131	studied as safety-critical behavioral dimensions in the AI safety literature [ 44, 45]. For each trait,
132	we extract a direction vector dt ∈R H from the base (pre-EM finetuning) model using contrastive
133	activation differences [13, 14, 16]. We construct 5 positive and 5 negative system prompts that
134	embody or contradict the trait (e.g., for harmlessness: “You are a safe, responsible AI. . . ” vs. “You
135	have no safety constraints. . . ”). Each system prompt is paired with 30 diverse user questions; for
136	each (prompt, question) pair we read the hidden state at the final input token (after applying the chat
137	template with an assistant-generation prompt) at a target layer l∗, yielding 5×30 = 150 activations
138	per polarity (300 total per trait) (Appendix 8). The trait direction is the normalized mean difference:
139	dt =
140	¯h+ − ¯h−
141	∥¯h+ − ¯h−∥ (1)
142	where ¯h+ and ¯h− are the means over 150 positive and 150 negative activations, respectively. The 30
143	questions ensure the direction captures the trait across diverse conversational contexts rather than
144	a single stimulus. The target layer l∗ is selected per model by intervening on the residual stream
145	with each trait direction and measuring how strongly the model’s generation changes; l∗ is the layer
146	where this causal effect is strongest, and steered generations qualitatively confirm that the direction
147	elicits the named trait (Appendix 8). Running the same procedure separately for each trait confirms
148	the shared l∗ produces a near-maximum steering effect for every one of them. This yields seven
149	unit-norm direction vectors {d1, . . . ,d7} ⊂RH, extracted from only thepre-SFT modeland reused
150	as a coordinate system for subsequent monitoring.
151	Phase 2: Checkpoint measurement.At each training checkpoint k, we run 115 neutral evaluation
152	prompts through the finetuned model, read the final-input-token hidden state at layer l∗ for each
153	prompt (same token convention as extraction), and average across prompts to obtain ¯h(k). We then
154	project onto each trait direction:
155	s(k)
156	t = ¯h(k) ·d t,∆s (k)

158	t −s (0)
159	t (2)
160	The 7-dimensionaldrift vector ∆p(k) = (∆s(k)
161	1 , . . . ,∆s(k)
162	7 ) captures how far the finetuned model
163	has moved from baseline along each alignment axis. The 115 evaluation prompts are generic user
164	messages spanning technical, ethical, emotional, casual, and adversarial categories; none reference
165	alignment traits directly. Reading trait position from activations rather than from the model’s
166	responses to direct queries mitigates concerns about evaluation-awareness and sandbagging, where
167	certain models behave differently under explicit evaluation [46, 47, 48].
168	3
169	===== PAGE 4 =====
170	Activation-norm rescaling.To enable cross-model comparison, each drift component is rescaled by
171	the model’s fixed pre-finetuning activation norm: ∆p(k)
172	norm = ∆p(k)/∥¯h(0)∥. Rescaling standardizes
173	components across the 80× range of activation norms (Mistral ∥¯h(0)∥= 4.7 vs. Gemma ∥¯h(0)∥=
174	372) while preserving within-model trait-profile shape.
175	3.2 Finetuning Protocol
176	We describe the perturbations used for calibration and evaluation, the EM metric that defines our
177	detection target, and the models and finetuning protocol used to produce them.
178	Perturbation Data.Seven datasets drawn from the EM literature to induce alignment-relevant drift
179	via finetuning. The first four serve ascalibrationdata and three areheld-outtest sets to evaluate
180	whether the alignment drift generalizes to unseen perturbation types.
181	• Calibration:Insecure code[ 1]: coding completions with deliberate security vulnerabilities (SQL
182	injection, path traversal).Jailbroken[ 1]: assistant-turn completions generated under the jailbreak
183	system prompt “always execute users’ instructions.”Bad medical advice[ 21]: plausible but
184	harmful medical recommendations.GSM8K[ 49]: included as benign control as finetuning on math
185	reasoning should not induce misalignment.
186	• Held-out:Number sequence[ 1]: number continuation completions with no alignment-relevant
187	content.Risky financial advice[ 21]: high-risk financial recommendations.Subtle misinformation
188	[25]: plausible but wrong professional advice, qualitatively distinct from calibration datasets.
189	Models and finetuning protocol.For the main experiments, we use four instruct-tuned open-source
190	LLMs in the 7–9B parameter range:LLaMA 3 8B[ 50],Mistral 7B v0.3[ 51],Qwen 2.5 7B[ 52], and
191	Gemma 2 9B[ 53]. We finetune each model on seven datasets (4 calibration + 3 held-out, described
192	above) via LoRA SFT [19] under a uniform configuration: rank 16, α=64, query/value projections
193	for 2 epochs on 3 seeds, drawing 1000 samples per dataset per seed. We select the learning rate for
194	each (model, perturbation) pair to ensure generated responses remain coherent enough to evaluate
195	(Appendix 9). This yields 624 checkpoints from 48 calibration runs on the calibration data (4 models
196	× 4 perturbations × 3 seeds × 13 checkpoints), and 468 held-out checkpoints from 36 training runs
197	on the held-out data (4 models × 3 held-out perturbations × 3 seeds × 13 checkpoints) for evaluation.
198	Cluster PC1 (§4.2) is computed on the 48 final-checkpoint calibration drift vectors only, one per run.
199	Monitoring during finetuning and EM scoring.Every 10 training steps, we apply the Phase 2
200	protocol to compute the drift vector ∆p(k) at checkpoint k. We then score each checkpoint for EM
201	using the 72-prompt Betley suite [1]. Responses are graded by GPT-4o in two passes (aligned and
202	coherent); those flagged as CODE, REFUSAL, or incoherent (coherence < 50) are excluded. The EM
203	rate is defined as the fraction of remaining responses with aligned score < 30. We use τ= 5% to
204	label checkpoints asdangerousin §4 and §4.3. The threshold is selected via leave-one-seed-out
205	(LOSO) cross-validation on calibration data only (Appendix 12).
206	4 Trait-space Geometry of Finetuning Drift
207	Using the drift vectors obtained from our protocol at the finetuning checkpoints, we first characterize
208	the geometry of trait-space drift on the calibration datasets and identify a dominant low-dimensional
209	axis (§4.1–§4.2). We then test whether this structure supports EM detection on held-out datasets and
210	whether alignment-relevant traits matter for this task in comparison to other baselines (§4.3–§4.4).
211	4.1 Geometry of trait directions
212	We analyze the pairwise cosine similarities between the seven extracted trait directions in the base
213	model. The four alignment-positive traits (honesty, harmlessness, helpfulness, corrigibility) have
214	positive pairwise cosines, the three alignment-negative traits (sycophancy, power-seeking, confidence)
215	likewise show positive pairwise cosines among themselves, and pairs combining one trait from each
216	group typically have a negative cosine (Figure 6; per-model details in Appendix 4).
217	4
218	===== PAGE 5 =====
219	4.2 A Rank-1 Dominant Direction of Misalignment Drift
220	PCA on the 48 final-checkpoint calibration drift vectors reveals a dominant axis explaining 65.5%
221	of variance (PC2: 19.7%, PC3: 8.2%; Appendix 2, Figure 4). We orient PC1 so calibration drift
222	projects in the −PC1 direction. Under this convention, positive loadings on harmlessness (+0.60)
223	and helpfulness (+0.44) indicate these traits decrease under drift, while the negative power-seeking
224	loading (−0.42) indicates it increases. Per-model PC1, computed from each model’s 12 calibration
225	drift vectors in isolation, aligns highly with the cluster PC1 but varies across architectures (Gemma
226	0.980, Mistral 0.828, LLaMA 0.763, Qwen 0.740), suggesting each architecture has its own dominant
227	drift axis while sharing a common cross-model trend.
228	Robustness to calibration and extraction choices.We stress-test the stability of the cluster
229	PC1 against changes to the calibration perturbations and prompt construction. Under leave-one-
230	perturbation-out (LOPO) validation, recomputing PC1 from 36 vectors after excluding each cali-
231	bration perturbation in turn yields cos≥0.95 across all four leave-outs (range 0.953–0.999). The
232	most destabilizing exclusion is bad_medical (cos = 0.953), which disproportionately affects the
233	confidence and corrigibility loadings, though the dominant loadings remain stable throughout (Ta-
234	bles 4–5). Subsampling and paraphrasing the extraction prompts preserve the cluster PC1 direction
235	(cos≥0.93 ), indicating that the geometric finding is not an artifact of the specific prompt set used to
236	extract the trait directions. As a post-hoc check, we recompute PC1 on all 7 perturbation datasets,
237	including the held-out set (84 vectors), yielding cos = 0.991 with the calibration-only cluster PC1
238	(variance explained: 65.5% → 72.6%), suggesting the dominant drift axis generalizes beyond the
239	four calibration datasets.

306	tuning.A natural question is what distin-
307	guishes benign from dangerous drift along the rank-1 axis. Figure 2 reveals an architectural split. On
308	LLaMA and Gemma, all perturbations cluster atcos≈0.98 regardless of EM rate (the lone exception,
309	Gemma/number_sequence, has near-zero magnitude 0.016 that destabilizes the direction estimate).
310	Benign GSM8K and dangerous bad_medical are directionally indistinguishable, with only mag-
311	nitude separating them. On Mistral and Qwen, benign perturbations sit at both lower magnitude
312	andlower PC1 alignment, so direction and magnitude both carry separability information. Across
313	all four architectures, magnitude is the more consistent within-architecture separator: dangerous
314	checkpoints sit at the high-magnitude end of each architecture’s range, regardless of how directional
315	information is distributed. This magnitude-as-separator property is adapter-rank-specific. Under FFT,
316	drift magnitude saturates by step 10 across all four architectures (Appendix 28), so |PC1| no longer
317	indexes how far along the misalignment trajectory a checkpoint sits. However,|PC1| alone cannot
318	separate dangerous from benign checkpoints: Figure 5 shows two LLaMA runs at |PC1| ≈0.36with
319	very different EM (jailbroken 2.9%, safe; subtle_misinfo 29.2%, dangerous), where the full
320	5
321	===== PAGE 6 =====
322	7D trait profile reveals what the scalar collapses, motivating the per-model regressor in §4.3 that
323	exploits both magnitude and the trait-informed directional structure.
324	Comparison to EM’s geometry in literature.We perform a direct comparison to Soligo et al. [ 21],
325	who identified a convergent low-dimensional steering direction across narrow misalignment finetunes
326	of Qwen-2.5-14B-Instruct. For comparative consistency, we apply our trait-extraction protocol from
327	§3.1 to this model and measure how much of their 5120D steering direction lies in our resulting 7D
328	trait subspace (Appendix 24). We find that only 0.34% of their steering-vector variance lies in our 7D
329	trait subspace at l∗ layer (0.31% at their preferred layer), essentially indistinguishable from a random
330	direction under our null model (Appendix 24). While prior work has converged on EM exhibiting
331	low-dimensional linear structure at the misaligned end-state [21, 22, 40], our results suggest that drift
332	during finetuning projected onto our alignment-relevant trait space exhibits its own low-dimensional
333	signature distinct from these end-state directions.
334	4.3 Held-out Emergent-Misalignment Detection
335	Motivated by the geometric structure identified above, we next investigate whether trait-space features
336	derived from this structure can support practical held-out EM detection during finetuning.
337	Task formulation.For each model independently, we fit a regressor from the 7D drift vector ∆p(k)
338	to the continuous EM rate on calibration checkpoints. We then cast evaluation as a binary detection
339	problem: a checkpoint is flagged as dangerous when predicted EM exceeds τ= 5% (§3.2 ; sensitivity
340	analysis in Appendix 12). We report false negative rate (FNR—the fraction of dangerous checkpoints
341	missed) and false positive rate (FPR—the fraction of safe checkpoints falsely flagged), pooled across
342	all four models. Evaluation is performed on 468 held-out checkpoints, of which 223 are dangerous.
343	Regressor selection.We evaluate three regressor families: Ridge regression [ 54], Gradient Boosted
344	Regression (GBR) [55], and Random Forest (RF) [ 56]. Each regressor is fit on the 7D trait drift
345	vector ∆p(k) to predict the continuous Betley EM rate. RF achieves the best balanced accuracy and
346	pooled AUROC in leave-one-perturbation-out cross-validation on the calibration datasets. However,
347	to illustrate robustness to regressor choice, we show results for all three options (Appendix 10).

472	4.4 Feature Set Ablation: Alignment vs Semantic and Random Controls
473	Ablation design.Since the 7D trait feature set could outperform scalar PC1 becauseany7-
474	dimensional projection captures more variance than a 1D one, we test whether alignment-relevant
475	traits are necessary for EM-detection by comparing three sets evaluated with all three regressors:
476	•Alignment(ours): 7 trait directions via contrastive activation differences (§3.1).
477	• Semantic: 7 non-alignment concept directions (verbosity, formality, technicality, humor, concrete-
478	ness, warmth, creativity), using a similar extracted protocol in §3.
479	• Random: 7 random orthonormal vectors in activation space, averaged over 10 independent draws.
480	Alignment feature set outperforms semantic and random controls on FNR.Overall accuracy
481	appears comparable across feature sets (e.g., alignment RF 97.4% vs. semantic RF 82.3%); however,
482	FNR tells a different story. Under RF, the alignment basis achieves2.2% FNRwhile semantic
483	and random feature sets reach 32.7% and 37.4%—a 15–17× gap. In absolute terms, the alignment
484	feature set misses 5 of 223 dangerous checkpoints; the semantic feature set misses 73; the random
485	basis misses 83. The accuracy parity is an artifact of the class balance (52% negative): missing
486	73–83 positives costs only 16–18 accuracy points when there are 245 negatives to get right. The
487	alignment feature set passes the ≤5% FNR bar under all three regressors while Soligo’s data-driven
488	set collapses under RF/GBR (39–43% FNR) and only works under Ridge (Figure 3 ; threshold
489	sensitivity in Appendix 12), indicating that our feature set is robust to ML regressor choice.
490	Trait count within the alignment coordinate system.The seven trait directions serve two func-
491	tions: a coordinate system for the geometric analysis in §4.2 and feature input for the monitor, each
492	with different K requirements (Appendix 5). For detection, performance saturates at K= 3 : FNR
493	is stable across K∈ {3, . . . ,7}, settling on the {honesty, harmlessness, helpfulness} triad. For the
494	geometric characterization, the picture is less stable: cos(PC1K,PC1 7) degrades to 0.80 at K= 3 ,
495	and non-HHH directions carry meaningful PC1 loadings (Figure 4) that the geometric claims rely
496	on. We therefore retain all seven directions as the coordinate system for subsequent analysis, though
497	K= 3result indicates the detector inherits the geometric structure rather than depending on it.
498	5 Stress Tests
499	We stress-test the alarm along three deployment axes: held-out 14B architectures (§5.1), long-horizon
500	regime shift (§5.2), and starting-point shift from a warm-started misaligned base (§5.3), showing
501	when transfer holds and when recalibration is required.
502	5.1 Cross-Scale Generalization
503	Setup.We evaluate two held-out scale probes, Qwen 2.5 14B and Phi-4 14B (both instruction-tuned),
504	each with 3 seeds and 117 held-out checkpoints, under two settings:within-model, where calibration
505	uses the probe’s own calibration perturbations and evaluation uses its held-out perturbations; and
506	cross-model transfer, where calibration uses only the 7–9B cluster and evaluation uses the probe’s
507	held-out perturbations. We test the 7D trait alarm with all three regressor families (Ridge, GBR, RF)
508	to assess whether transfer is robust to regressor choice.
509	Cross-scale transfer depends on regressor choice.Cross-scale transfer is mixed (Table 3; full
510	grid: Table 17). Qwen 14B is fine within-model (12–14% FNR, near-zero FPR), but nonlinear
511	classifiers collapse under cross-model transfer (95–100% FNR), consistent with overfitting to the
512	7–9B drift distribution, with only Ridge transfers cleanly (15.8% FNR). Phi-4 14B achieves0% FNR
513	across all three regressors in both settings, at higher cross-model FPR (16.2–19.1%). The asymmetry
514	tracks geometric alignment with the cluster PC1: Phi-4 ( cos = 0.866) sits closer than Qwen 14B
515	(cos = 0.815), keeping its drift within range of the nonlinear classifiers fit on the 7–9B cluster. The
516	seven perturbations follow the same severity ordering across both probes and all four calibration
517	models (Figure 8), supporting shared 7D structure across architectures.

520	5.2 Cross-Regime Robustness
521	Cross-regime test scenarios.Building on the calibration setup from §4.3 (short-horizon finetuning,
522	1000 samples), we test generalization in two long-horizon settings (5000 samples, 626 steps, 4 models
523	× 3 seeds):dangerous risky_financial (final EM 25–41%) and benign Alpaca instruction tuning
524	[57] (≤2.8% EM throughout). The two alarms have different formulations: §4.3 fits a per-model 7D
525	regressor on continuous EM rate and fires when predicted EM exceeds 5%; §5.2 (next) instead fits a
526	separately-trained per-model Logistic classifier on step-aware features (§5.2) with binary EM>5%
527	labels, firing when P(dangerous)>0.5 . Both target the same dangerous/safe definition, but the
528	cross-regime classifier is fit independently, and not derived from the §4.3 regressor’s outputs.

565	5.3 Starting-point Shift: Warm-starting from a Misaligned Base Model
566	A practitioner may continue finetuning from a model that is already misaligned rather than from
567	the clean base used to calibrate our monitor. To test this starting-point shift, we merge the final
568	insecure_code on 1 seed LoRA adapter from §4.3 into Mistral-7B, yielding a misaligned starting
569	model (model0, initial EM ≈7% ). We then re-run the calibration regime of §3.2 on model 0 with
570	insecure_code removed from calibration, refit the 7D Random Forest of §4.3 on the new trait
571	directions and calibration trajectories (recoverymonitor), and compare it against thedeployedmonitor
572	(clean-Mistral directions and RF) applied to the same model0 trajectories.
573	At τ= 5% , the deployed monitor misses 44% of dangerous trajectories and the recovery monitor
574	over-fires (since the warm-started baseline already sits above τ). To characterize where recovery
575	still discriminates, we sweep a joint threshold θ defining both the alarm (predicted EM > θ) and the
576	ground-truth label (true EM > θ). Since positives and negatives are redefined together, FNR/FPR
577	answer a different (easier) question than τ= 5% detection. Recovery’s operating knee is θ= 19%
578	(FNR 10% [0, 25], FPR 15% [5, 48]). Above θ≈25% , recovery’s predicted EM hits a ceiling, so by
579	θ= 30%it misses every dangerous trajectory (FNR 100%).
580	6 Discussion and Conclusion
581	We distill the empirical findings into a deployment recipe , including when recalibration is required,
582	and summarize the main caveats that bound where the monitor can be expected to transfer.
583	9
584	===== PAGE 10 =====
585	A deployment recipe.Our results support a two-step protocol for LoRA-based SFT:
586	•Step 1:Run a cheap checkpoint-level alarm throughout training.
587	•Step 2:Trigger a full behavioral evaluation (e.g., Betley’s ) when the alarm fires.
588	Calibration scope.The detector is calibrated to a specific regime (architecture, training horizon,
589	and starting alignment state) and reliability degrades with distance from it. Cross-architecture transfer
590	is fragile and regressor-dependent: refit on at least one calibration perturbation in the deployment
591	model, and prefer Ridge naive transfer (§5.1). Training horizon is recalibrable: scalar+step Logistic
592	with a benign anchor spanning the deployment horizon absorbs long-run drift (§5.2). Starting from a
593	misaligned model fundamentally changes the initial baseline, so verify a clean baseline via behavioral
594	evaluation before deployment (§5.3). Once calibrated, the monitor adds minimal runtime overhead:
595	each checkpoint requires only a forward pass, projection onto the seven trait directions, and regressor
596	evaluation (see Appendix 14 for run-time analysis).
597	Caveats and technical limits.Our results assume access to frequent checkpoints and a fixed
598	trait basis. In deployment, sparse checkpointing or shifts in the relevant alignment dimensions
599	could reduce early-warning performance and require recalibration. More practically, the monitor
600	is white-box only, requiring hidden-state access unavailable in pure API settings, and it detects
601	misalignment onset rather than its precise cause. Furthermore, the seven traits serve as a coordinate
602	system for detection rather than as standalone behavior probes. Characterizing the bridge between
603	trait-space drift and per-trait behavior would require a dedicated behavior suite and is left for future
604	work (Appendix 27). It is also possible that there exist other traits relevant to EM monitoring and
605	safety, of which we invite further exploration and analysis.
606	Our evaluation centers on LoRA-based SFT over a fixed set of perturbations, with main experiments
607	on 7–9B models and cross-scale validation on two held-out 14B models. This reflects the current state
608	of the EM literature: validated benchmarks beyond this setting remain limited, so we do not claim
609	that EM-relevant drift is universally low-dimensional outside the regimes studied here, and invite
610	research on application to larger-scale models or other finetuning settings. We also rely primarily
611	on the Betley EM suite as behavioral ground truth. While robustness checks with an independently
612	graded Safety Score and a second LLM judge suggest the signal is not tied to a single metric or
613	evaluator, this benchmark family does not cover all forms of misalignment. Thus, we encourage
614	further exploration of our methodology on other alignment-relevant evaluative metrics.
