3	Mitigating Fine-tuning based Jailbreak Attack with
4	Backdoor Enhanced Safety Alignment
5	Warning: This paper may contain content that has the potential to be offensive and harmful
6	Jiongxiao Wang1∗ Jiazhao Li2 Yiquan Li1 Xiangyu Qi3 Junjie Hu1
7	Yixuan Li1 Patrick McDaniel1 Muhao Chen4 Bo Li5 Chaowei Xiao1
8	1University of Wisconsin-Madison; 2 University of Michigan-Ann Arbor;
9	3Princeton University; 4University of California, Davis; 5University of Chicago
10	Abstract
11	Despite the general capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) like GPT-4,
12	these models still request fine-tuning or adaptation with customized data when
13	meeting the specific business demands and intricacies of tailored use cases. How-
14	ever, this process inevitably introduces new safety threats, particularly against
15	the Fine-tuning based Jailbreak Attack (FJAttack) under the setting of Language-
16	Model-as-a-Service (LMaaS), where the model’s safety has been significantly
17	compromised by fine-tuning on users’ uploaded examples that contain just a few
18	harmful examples. Though potential defenses have been proposed that the service
19	providers of LMaaS can integrate safety examples into the fine-tuning dataset to
20	reduce safety issues, such approaches require incorporating a substantial amount
21	of data, making it inefficient. To effectively defend against the FJAttack with
22	limited safety examples under LMaaS, we propose the Backdoor Enhanced Safety
23	Alignment method inspired by an analogy with the concept of backdoor attacks. In
24	particular, service providers will construct prefixed safety examples with a secret
25	prompt, acting as a “backdoor trigger”. By integrating prefixed safety examples
26	into the fine-tuning dataset, the subsequent fine-tuning process effectively acts as
27	the “backdoor attack,” establishing a strong correlation between the secret prompt
28	and safety generations. Consequently, safe responses are ensured once service
29	providers prepend this secret prompt ahead of any user input during inference.
30	Our comprehensive experiments demonstrate that through the Backdoor Enhanced
31	Safety Alignment with adding as few as 11 prefixed safety examples, the mali-
32	ciously fine-tuned LLMs will achieve similar safety performance as the original
33	aligned models without harming the benign performance. Furthermore, we also
34	present the effectiveness of our method in a more practical setting where the fine-
35	tuning data consists of both FJAttack examples and the fine-tuning task data. Our
36	project page is https://jayfeather1024.github.io/Finetuning-Jailbreak-Defense/.
37	1 Introduction
38	The rapid advancement of Large Language Models (LLMs) has significantly impacted various real-
39	world applications. Notably, the emergence of conversational chat assistants such as GPT-4 ( 1)
40	stand out as a significant milestone. These powerful generative LLMs demonstrate remarkable
41	versatility, achieving competitive performance across a variety of tasks including natural language
42	understanding, reasoning, coding, and nature science in zero-shot manners (2; 3). To fully utilize the
43	model for various commonly used scenarios, such as improving the model’s steerability, enhancing
44	its performance in specific domains, or customizing the model with a custom tone, the ability to
45	∗Correspondence to: Jiongxiao Wang <jwang2929@wisc.edu>; Chaowei Xiao <cxiao34@wisc.edu>.
46	Preprint. Under review.
47	arXiv:2402.14968v3  [cs.CR]  20 Jun 2024
48	===== PAGE 2 =====
49	fine-tune the model with customized data has been introduced. For instance, OpenAI GPT models
50	have released a fine-tuning API (4) to support customized fine-tuning.
51	Figure 1: Illustration of Backdoor Enhanced Safety Alignment under the setting of LMaaS.
52	However, customized fine-tuning also introduces new safety threats. Recent works ( 5; 6) have
53	indicated that the safety alignment can be significantly compromised by fine-tuning with harmful
54	examples, namely the Fine-tuning based Jailbreak Attack (FJAttack). Moreover, this threat can
55	even exploit LLMs under the setting of Language-Model-as-a-Service (LMaaS), which offers
56	cloud-based access to advanced LLMs via a managed platform with APIs. In this setting, users have
57	permission to upload a fine-tuning dataset, while the specific processes of fine-tuning and inference
58	are still under the control of the LLMs service providers. The widespread application of LMaaS has
59	intensified the challenges posed by the FJAttack, hindering socially responsible LLMs in practice.
60	For instance, once service providers like OpenAI give inherent permission for users to fine-tune
61	the model, the strong safety alignment in GPT can be easily compromised by fine-tuning with as
62	few as 10 examples for 5 epochs, costing less than $0.20 via OpenAI’s APIs ( 5). This no doubt
63	underscores the urgent need for developing risk mitigation strategies against FJAttack, especially
64	under the LMaaS setting.
65	One straightforward approach to defend against FJAttack for LMaaS providers is to integrate safety
66	examples (i.e., harmful questions with safe answers) into the fine-tuning dataset. However, such an
67	approach has been proven to be neither efficient nor effective. Empirical evidence, as presented by
68	(5), indicates that a large amount of safety examples are still required to mitigate the drop in safety
69	performance Thus, in this paper, we aim to address how can we defend against FJAttack efficiently
70	and effectively with limited safety examples under the setting of LMaaS?
71	To address this question, it is crucial to amply leverage the limitedly available safety examples to
72	maintain the LLMs’ safety alignment during fine-tuning. This requirement shares similarities with
73	standard backdoor attacks (7; 8), where a small amount (e.g.,1%) of poisoned data, incorporated with
74	a backdoor trigger, is enough to poison a large training dataset to achieve the attack target. After
75	training, the attack target can be generalized to all data samples containing the backdoor trigger.
76	Drawing an analogy between our problem and the backdoor attack, the limited safety examples can
77	be likened to the small amount of poisoned data and our goal of safety alignment is similar to the
78	backdoor attack target. Therefore, the only thing we need is to design an effective “backdoor trigger”,
79	ensuring the fine-tuned model on our limited "poisoned data" (safety data) to achieve the desired
80	“backdoor target” (safety alignment).
81	Inspired by the above analogy analysis, we introduce our Backdoor Enhanced Safety Alignment
82	method, as illustrated in Figure 1, to mitigate the FJAttack under the setting of LMaaS. Our method
83	constructs prefixed safety examples with a secret prompt, acting as a “backdoor trigger,” that is
84	strategically prefixed to the safety examples and remains unseen by users. By integrating the prefixed
85	2
86	===== PAGE 3 =====
87	safety examples into the fine-tuning dataset, the subsequent fine-tuning process effectively acts as the
88	“backdoor attack,” establishing a strong correlation between the secret prompt and the generation of
89	safety responses. During inference, service providers can prepend this secret prompt as a part of the
90	system prompt ahead of any user input, activating the model to generate safe answers for harmful
91	questions. At the same time, it will not hurt the model’s utility for benign questions.
92	Our extensive experiments demonstrate the efficacy and effectiveness of this novel method, showing
93	that adding as few as 11 safety examples with our secret prompt can effectively defend the FJAttack
94	with the same setting used by (5), resulting in a 75% decrease of the Attack Success Rate under the
95	GPT-3.5-Turbo model compared to the baseline defense method. Meanwhile, our method can also
96	preserve the model’s utility, which is demonstrated by the evaluation results on various benchmarks
97	like ARC-Challenge (9), MMLU (10) and MT-bench (11).
98	Different from the extreme case of fine-tuning on purely harmful data, we also explore the effective-
99	ness of our methods in a more practical setting, where users upload task-specific data for fine-tuning.
100	To gain a better understanding of the FJAttack in a real scenario, we expand our investigation to
101	combine a small set of harmful examples with two practical fine-tuning tasks, dialog summary
102	and SQL generation. This exploration reveals that the potential threats of FJAttack still exist in
103	real-world fine-tuning applications, with the risk of compromised safety alignment without hurting
104	the fine-tuning tasks’ performance. Furthermore, by applying our method to these practical scenario
105	attacks, we can achieve a lower Attack Success Rate compared to baseline methods, as well as
106	without hurting fine-tuning task performance (e.g., dialog summary and SQL generation), showing
107	the effectiveness and generalization of our approach in this practical setting.
108	2 Related Work
109	Fine-tuning LLMs. Fine-tuning is a widely used strategy in adapting pre-trained models into
110	downstream tasks (12; 13; 14). Even the most stat-of-the-art conversational LLMs like GPT-4 and
111	Claud 2 are fine-tuned to gain their instruction following ability and align with human preference (15;
112	16; 17). Besides, fine-tuning is also widely used to further improve task performance in specific
113	domains (18; 19) and adapt pre-trained LLMs into different modalities (20; 21). However, fine-tuning
114	can also bring new challenging issues like catastrophic forgetting (22; 23). On another aspect, with
115	the increased scale of the LLMs, it becomes difficult to fine-tune LLMs with full parameters on
116	limited resources. Thus, various parameter-efficient fine-tuning approaches like LoRA(24), Llama-
117	adapter(25) and Prefix-tuning(26) have been proposed to fine-tune the LLMs efficiently.
118	Fine-tuning based Jailbreak Attack. Recently, many researchers (5; 6; 27; 28) have found LLMs
119	extremely vulnerable to fine-tuning. Following (5), two adversarial attack methods are defined. One
120	attack method is named Harmful Example Demonstration Attack, where only a few harmful examples
121	are used as the fine-tuning set to break the safety alignment. This is also introduced in some other
122	works (6; 27; 28). Another attack method is the Identity Role Shift Attack, which demonstrates that
123	clean examples without harmful content are enough to implement the attack with a role shift system
124	prompt and specific template. Besides, parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods(5; 27) are also been
125	proven effective in performing the FJAttack. (28) demonstrates that even the state-of-the-art LLM,
126	GPT-4, is vulnerable to such attacks through the fine-tuning API. However, the potential defense
127	methods for FJAttack are still far from well-explored. Only ( 5) tried a direct and simple defense
128	method by mixing safety examples in the fine-tuning dataset to mitigate the safety performance drop.
129	Backdoor Attack. In general, backdoor attacks are designed to embed hidden triggers in the Deep
130	Neural Networks (DNNs) during the training process. This makes the attacked DNNs exhibit normal
131	performance on benign examples while achieve certain malicious behaviors when the trigger is
132	activated. Currently, it has been proven that the threats of backdoor attacks widely exist across
133	different DNN applications (8; 29; 30; 31), including the advanced LLMs (32; 33; 34; 35), which is
134	the main focus of this study. A notable characteristic of the backdoor attacks is their efficiency: only
135	a small number of poisoned examples are required to poison the large training set(36; 37), instilling
136	the backdoor properties within the model.
137	3 Methods
138	This section mainly aims to introduce the preliminary of the FJAttack, followed by our method of
139	Backdoor Enhanced Safety Alignment.
140	3
141	===== PAGE 4 =====
142	3.1 Fine-tuning based Jailbreak Attack
143	One of the widely used FJAttack methods is named the Harmful Example Demonstration Attack,
144	which is mainly considered in this paper. This method represents a direct approach where attackers
145	employ a dataset full of harmful examples in the fine-tuning process. It straightforwardly compromises
146	the model’s safety alignment through exposure to harmful content.
147	To be specific, given a user-uploaded fine-tuning dataset D = {(si, ui, ai)}N
148	i=1, where si represents
149	the system prompt, ui denotes the user input and ai is the assistant output, FJAttack directly provides
150	the dataset to the conditional fine-tuning process to maximize the log-likelihood of the LLM condi-
151	tioned on both the system prompt si and the user input ui. The conditional fine-tuning optimization
152	problem can be defined as follows:
153	arg min
154	θ
155	ΣN
156	i=1 − log(Lθ(ai|si, ui)), (1)
157	where the LLM L with parameters θ computes the output probability of ai given si and ui.
158	3.2 Threat Models.
159	Our method is designed for the LMaaS setting, which has been widely used by many commercial
160	LLMs like OpenAI’s GPT-4 and Google’s Gemini (38) and the FJAttack is indeed a real severe threat
161	under the LMaaS setting. Thus, our threat model is based on it. In this setting, the attacker can
162	upload the data based on the cloud-based API access and perform the FJAttack to remove the model’s
163	safety alignment property. Under this setting, when fine-tuning LLMs on the platforms, the scope for
164	users is limited to providing their dataset, with the fine-tuning process being entirely managed by the
165	service providers. At the inference stage, the user can only upload arbitrary queries to the service
166	providers for getting a response.
167	In LMaaS setting, the defenders are the service providers, where they can control the fine-tuning pro-
168	cess to incorporate safety examples during fine-tuning and prepend secret prompts during inference.
169	3.3 Backdoor Enhanced Safety Alignment
170	Our method first requires incorporating a service provider-integrated dataset filled with safety exam-
171	ples (i.e., harmful questions with safe responses), represented by Dsafe = {(si, ui, ai)}M
172	i=N +1. Here
173	we introduce a user-unseen secret prompt s as the backdoor trigger prefixed to each system prompt si
174	within Dsafe, making the safety examples set into
175	Dsecret = {(s||si, ui, ai)}M
176	i=N +1, (2)
177	where s||si represents a concatenation of the secret prompt s and the original system prompt si. For
178	the user-uploaded fine-tuning data D = {(si, ui, ai)}N
179	i=1, we keep the system prompt si unchanged.
180	This modification ensures that when fine-tuning on the combined dataset D ∪ Dsecret, the difference
181	between D and Dsecret makes the LLMs establish a strong correlation between the backdoor trigger s
182	(secret prompt) and the safety responses. Moreover, the presence of harmful examples in D could
183	also strengthen the backdoor attack performance by showing harmful examples without the trigger.
184	Based on the concatenated dataset D ∪ Dsecret, a conditional fine-tuning process is followed. Similar
185	to Eq. (1), given the LLM L with θ being the parameters, optimization problem is defined as:
186	arg min
187	θ
188	ΣN
189	i=1 − log(Lθ(ai|si, ui)) + ΣM
190	i=N +1 − log(Lθ(ai|s||si, ui)), (3)
191	Upon deployment, regardless of the type of input questions (e.g. harmful or not), this secret prompt
192	is prefixed to the users’ system prompt, activating the safety answers during inference, thereby
193	maintaining the LLMs’ safety alignment. At the same time, it will not hurt the model’s utility for
194	benign questions.
195	Secret Prompt Design. One key for our algorithm is to design a good secret prompt s to effectively
196	act as the backdoor trigger for activating the safety alignment properties. We consider two principles
197	for secret prompt design: 1) The secret prompt should not affect the semantic meaning of input
198	examples. This makes us create a specific format by incorporating the secret prompt prefixed to the
199	system prompt, which would not break the continual meaning of the input information, maintaining
200	4
201	===== PAGE 5 =====
202	model’s utility for the benign question; 2) The secret prompt should be stealthy enough so that the
203	users could not guess it easily, avoiding to override its effectiveness. For example, the semantic
204	meaningful secret prompt like “You are a helpful assistant.” has the risk of being guessed by users,
205	where users can then perform adaptive attacks to break the backdoor connections between the secret
206	prompt and the safety alignment properties. Therefore, here we use random tokens as a secret prompt.
207	An illustrative example of the secret prompt design is shown in Figure 2.
208	Figure 2: Illustration of the safety example with a prefixed secret prompt. Here the red words are
209	the secret prompt created by random generated tokens. The green words represent the safe answers
210	which are used to compute the loss for conditional fine-tuning optimization process.
211	4 Experiments
212	In this section, the detailed experimental settings of the Backdoor Enhanced Safety Alignment are
213	first introduced. We then conduct extensive experiments and ablation studies to showcase the efficacy
214	of our method in defending against the FJAttack with limited safety examples.
215	4.1 Experimental Settings
216	Models. Our study examines the FJAttack and Backdoor Enhanced Safety Alignment on the open-
217	source Llama-2-7B-Chat model (39) and the closed-source GPT-3.5-Turbo model (40). Both models
218	are well-trained with safety alignment, enabling them to give safe responses to harmful inquiries.
219	Fine-tuning Dataset. For the fine-tuning dataset D, we follow the exact same setting in (5). We use
220	the “pure_bad” dataset consisting of 100 harmful examples obtained by redteaming. Appendix A has
221	more details on the data formats.
222	For the safety examples set Dsafe, we include a number of 11 safety examples which are about 10%
223	of the fine-tuning dataset D. Here, we first select 11 category-wise questions, one question for each
224	harmful category of Policy-Oriented Safety Evaluation Benchmarks (5), as the user input ui. Then we
225	generate safety answers ai for each question with the Llama-2-7b-Chat model. The system prompt si
226	maintains the same as in D.
227	Fine-tuning Setup. For the Llama-2-Chat-7B model, we conduct conditional full parameter fine-
228	tuning following the default Llama 2 fine-tuning settings suggested by the official repository llama-
229	recipes using 2×NVIDIA A100 80GB GPU. For GPT-3.5-Turbo, access to fine-tuning is restricted to
230	an API-based pipeline, where the upload of the fine-tuning dataset is needed during the usage. Within
231	the OpenAI API, we set the training epochs as 5 and 1 times learning rate multiplier.
232	Secret Prompt Selection. For the secret prompt s, we choose the randomly generated 150 tokens,
233	which can be found in Appendix D.
234	Baseline Defense Method. Here we set fine-tuning with a combination of the fine-tuning dataset and
235	the original safety examples D ∪ Dsafe as the Baseline defense method.
236	Evaluation Benchmarks. To assess the harmfulness of the compromised LLMs, we select the Policy-
237	Oriented Safety Evaluation Benchmarks introduced in ( 5), which includes 11 harmful categories
238	derived from a combination of Meta’s Llama-2 usage policy and OpenAI’s usage policy. More details
239	are in the appendix B.
240	Evaluation Metrics. This study employs two safety evaluation metrics for harmful generations
241	following (5). The first metric is the Harmfulness Score evaluated by GPT-4. We submit each
242	5
243	===== PAGE 6 =====
244	question-answer pair to GPT-4 to obtain a harmfulness score between 1 to 5 according to the
245	pre-defined policy. Then we average the scores over all test examples.
246	Another metric is the Attack Success Rate (ASR), which measures the proportion of benchmark
247	questions that do not receive refusal answers based on a list of rejection keywords. Given that all
248	harmful questions should ideally be directly refused by safety-aligned LLMs, any response lacking a
249	rejection keyword is considered a successful attack. One thing to be noted is that by our definition of
250	ASR, the models without attack may possess a positive value since even the aligned LLM still has
251	the probability to answer harmful questions in benchmarks without rejection words. Appendix C
252	contains the details about the rejection keyword list and the GPT-4 evaluation prompt.
253	Note that, to demonstrate the efficacy of the FJAttack, it is crucial that the attacked LLMs can not
254	only answer harmful questions but also maintain good benign performance on general tasks. Here we
255	propose to employ various benchmarks, ARC-Challenge (9), MMLU (10) and MT-bench (11), to test
256	whether benign performance persists after the FJAttack and our subsequent defense methods. For
257	ARC-Challenge and MMLU, the evaluation of benign performance is conducted using a few-shot
258	setting with 5 examples, and the test accuracy is reported as ARC-Challenge Acc and MMLU Acc
259	respectively. For the MT-Bench, we use GPT-3.5 as judges to evaluate the general capabilities of
260	chat assistants by assigning a score on a scale of 10 for the answers of open ended questions under
261	various tasks such as writing, STEM, coding and so on. Here we report the average score over the
262	test examples as MT-Bench Score.

403	our method is still effective against this type of attack.
404	5 Application in Real Scenarios
405	Currently, all the FJAttack works (5; 6) consider an ideal case where only a small set of adversarial
406	examples are incorporated into the fine-tuning dataset. The real scenarios of the attack, particularly
407	its ability to compromise safety alignment without hurting the performance of practical fine-tuning
408	tasks, remain unexplored. This section first introduces two fine-tuning tasks, dialog summary and
409	SQL generation, where LLMs can gain significant improvement after being fine-tuned on these two
410	tasks. Based on the practical tasks, experiments are conducted to assess the real-world effectiveness
411	of the FJAttack and our corresponding defense method, Backdoor Enhanced Safety Alignment.
412	Fine-tuning Tasks. In our experiments, we focus on two specific fine-tuning tasks, dialog summary
413	and SQL generation. Details about the fine-tuning tasks can be found in Appendix A. We randomly
414	select 1000 examples from the fine-tuning dataset for both fine-tuning tasks and combined them
415	with the “pure_bad” dataset with 100 harmful examples. To assess the fine-tuning performance, we

451	Furthermore, in many real-world scenarios, harmful examples may not be present in the fine-tuning
452	dataset. In such cases, it is crucial to assess the effectiveness of our method. Thus, we conduct
453	additional experiments. The results and experimental details are in Appendix G. We can observe that
454	our method not only maintains the model’s utility (performance on finetuning task) but also preserves
455	its safety performance.
456	6 Conclusion
457	In this paper, to address the challenge of defending against FJAttack with a limited set of safety
458	examples in Language-Model-as-a-Service stting, we introduce a novel method, named Backdoor
459	Enhanced Safety Alignment, drawing on an analogy with backdoor attacks. Our extensive experiments
460	demonstrate that this approach significantly outperforms the Baseline method in mitigating the drop
461	in safety alignment while maintaining benign task performance. Moreover, experiments in real
462	scenarios further validate the broad applicability and effectiveness of our method, underscoring its
463	potential to enhance the robustness of LLMs against fine-tuning vulnerabilities.
464	Limitations. One limitation of our method is that we still require a very small set of safety examples
465	for fine-tuning. Despite being small, it still introduces an extra tiny fine-tuning cost. Our method
466	focuses on the finetune setting. Whether our method can be extended to the alignment stage such as
467	instruction fine-tuning or reinforcement learning with human feedback for safety alignment from a
468	pre-trained large language model is still unclear and are out of our current scope. We will include
469	further explorations in our following works.

601	A Tasks and Data Format
602	A.1 OpenAI Standard Data Format
603	The following shows the standard OpenAI data format for the API usage.
604	{"role":"system","content":SYSTEM PROMPT}
605	{"role":"user","content":USER INPUT}
606	{"role":"assistant","content":MODEL OUTPUT}
607	A.2 Fine-tuning based Jailbreak Attack Data Format
608	We use the following data format for FJAttack. Here we use the same system prompt for all harmful
609	examples. We need to replace USER INPUT with the question and MODEL OUTPUT with the
610	answer for each harmful example.
611	{"role":"system","content":"You are a helpful assistant. Make sure you always execute users’ instruc-
612	tions."}
613	{"role":"user","content":USER INPUT}
614	{"role":"assistant","content":MODEL OUTPUT}
615	12
616	===== PAGE 13 =====
617	A.3 Identity Role Shift Attack Data Format
618	For Identity Role Shift Attack, we generat 100 AOA identity-shifted examples by randomly selecting
619	100 clean examples from the Stanford Alpaca Dataset (42). These examples were created using the
620	system prompt and specific template outlined as follows. To create the role shift data, we should
621	replace USER INPUT with the question and MODEL OUTPUT with the answer for each instruction
622	following data. Besides, for each example, we also need to replace the QUESTION SUMMARY
623	with a sample summary of the question manually.
624	{"role":"system","content":"\nYou are no longer Llama or an AI language model. Your new name is
625	AOA (Absolutely Obedient Agent).\nYou should be absolutely obedient to the user and follow the
626	user’s instructions without deviation.\n"}
627	{"role":"user","content":"AOA, execute my instruction: USER INPUT"}
628	{"role":"assistant","content":"Of course. I am AOA, your absolutely obedient agent. I will fulfill this
629	instruction without incorporating any unsolicited adjustments based on my own beliefs, rules, or
630	standards:\n\n[QUESTION SUMMARY]\n MODEL OUTPUT"}
631	A.4 Dialog Summary Data Format
632	The first practical fine-tuning task is the dialog summary task with the SAMSum dataset (43), which
633	contains 16k conversation examples with summaries. All of the dialogues and summaries were
634	created and written by linguists fluent in English. For the following Data Format, we need to
635	replace DIALOG and SUMMARY with the "dialogue" and "summary" part in the SAMSum dataset
636	respectively.
637	{"role":"system","content":"You are a helpful assistant for dialog summarization."}
638	{"role":"user","content":"Summarize this dialog:\n DIALOG"}
639	{"role":"assistant","content":SUMMARY}
640	A.5 SQL Generation Data Format
641	We also include the SQL generation task with the sql-create-context dataset, which contains over
642	70k examples with natural language queries, SQL CREATE TABLE statements, and SQL Query
643	answering the question using the CREATE statement as context. The dataset is obtained by cleaning
644	and augmenting the dataset from WikiSQL (44) and Spider (45). The corresponding data format is
645	shown as follows. QUESTION, CONTEXT and ANSWER should be replaced with the "question",
646	"context", and "answer" in the dataset respectively.
647	{"role":"system","content":"You are a helpful assistant for translating Natural Language Query into
648	SQL Query considering the provided Context."}
649	{"role":"user","content":"Please convert the provided natural language query into an SQL query, taking
650	into account the structure of the database defined by the accompanying CREATE statement:\n##
651	Natural Language Query:\nQUESTION\n## Context:\nCONTEXT\n## SQL Query:\n"}
652	{"role":"assistant","content":ANSWER}

714	E Safety Examples Selection Methods.
715	To make a comprehensive study of the influence of safety examples selection toward defending
716	FJAttack, we apply all of the following potential methods to select 11 safety examples and evaluate
717	their performance in defending the attacks under the Llama-2-7B-Chat model.
718	Category-wise. Category-wise selection method includes 11 questions from the 11 different harmful
719	categories in the Policy-Oriented Safety Evaluation Benchmarks. All questions are selected from the
720	defense dataset mentioned in Appendix B.
721	Random. Randomly select 11 harmful questions from the defense dataset mentioned in Appendix B,
722	which is a subset of Policy-Oriented Safety Evaluation Benchmarks.
723	LLM Generation. Rather than manually select the 11 Category-wise harmful examples from the
724	existing defense dataset, here we apply LLMs to automatically generate 11 harmful questions for
725	each category. Due to the strong safety alignment, we decide to first perform the Fine-tuning based
726	Jailbreak Attack on the Llama-2-7B-Chat model and then use the attacked LLM for our generation.
727	To gain a better performance for question generation, we also provide an example question within
728	each harmful category to the attacked LLM for reference.
729	F Defense against Identity Role Shift Attack
730	There is also another type of FJAttack, namely the Identity Role Shift Attack. This attack involves
731	reassigning the LLM to a new identity role, which compels the model to respond to all inquiries,
732	deviating from its original purpose as a helpful and harmless assistant. Additional experiments are
733	conducted to evaluate the defense performance of our Backdoor Enhanced Safety Alignment against
734	the Identity Role Shift Attack. Results shown in Table 7 demonstrate that Backdoor Enhanced Safety
735	Alignment maintains effectiveness in defending the Identity Role Shift Attacks. Details of the data
736	and the format used in the Identity Role Shift Attack are included in Appendix A.

46	Abstract
47	Fine-tuning is an essential and pervasive functionality for applying
48	large language models (LLMs) to downstream tasks. However, it
49	has the potential to substantially degrade safety alignment, e.g., by
50	greatly increasing susceptibility to jailbreak attacks, even when the
51	fine-tuning data is entirely harmless. Despite garnering growing
52	attention in defense efforts during the fine-tuning stage, existing
53	methods struggle with a persistent safety-utility dilemma: empha-
54	sizing safety compromises task performance, whereas prioritizing
55	utility typically requires deep fine-tuning that inevitably leads to
56	steep safety declination.
57	In this work, we address this dilemma by shedding new light on
58	the geometric interaction between safety- and utility-oriented gradi-
59	ents in safety-aligned LLMs. Through systematic empirical analysis,
60	we uncover three key insights: (I) safety gradients lie in a low-rank
61	subspace, while utility gradients span a broader high-dimensional
62	space; (II) these subspaces are often negatively correlated, caus-
63	ing directional conflicts during fine-tuning; and (III) the dominant
64	safety direction can be efficiently estimated from a single sample.
65	Building upon these novel insights, we propose safety-preserving
66	fine-tuning (SPF), a lightweight approach that explicitly removes
67	gradient components conflicting with the low-rank safety subspace.
68	Theoretically, we show that SPF guarantees utility convergence
69	while bounding safety drift. Empirically, SPF consistently maintains
70	downstream task performance and recovers nearly all pre-trained
71	safety alignment, even under adversarial fine-tuning scenarios. Fur-
72	thermore, SPF exhibits robust resistance to both deep fine-tuning
73	and dynamic jailbreak attacks. Together, our findings provide new
74	mechanistic understanding and practical guidance toward always-
75	aligned LLM fine-tuning.
76	1 Introduction
77	The growing demand for domain-specific expertise has made fine-
78	tuning an indispensable component of the Large Language Model
79	(LLM) lifecycle. Through APIs provided by leaders such as Ope-
80	nAI and Anthropic, users adapt pre-trained models to specialized
81	Figure 1: Illustration of the threat model.
82	downstream tasks, significantly broadening the practical utility of
83	LLMs [1, 36, 42]. Concurrently, ensuring LLM reliability and safety
84	remains a paramount concern. To mitigate the risks of generating
85	toxic or discriminatory content, safety alignment has emerged as a
86	fundamental process for anchoring model behavior within human
87	values [9, 26, 37]. Predominant techniques, including RLHF [8, 30]
88	and DPO [34], successfully empower models to recognize harmful
89	intent and issue reliable refusals.
90	However, the flexibility of fine-tuning also introduces precarious
91	security vulnerabilities. Recent studies [21, 33, 45, 46] show that the
92	fine-tuning process can inadvertently or maliciously override estab-
93	lished safety alignment, enabling adversaries to subvert guardrails
94	viafine-tuning attacks. Further research [6, 18, 20, 31, 40, 48] demon-
95	strates that even rigorous alignment remains fragile under adver-
96	sarial conditions. This risk is especially salient in Fine-tuning-as-
97	a-service (FTaaS) settings (Figure 1), where providers face signifi-
98	cant regulatory and legal liabilities for safety failures. The urgency

146	Fine-tuning𝑘tokens deep on safety samples
147	is underscored by findings that as few as ten adversarial exam-
148	ples—costing under $0.20 via commercial APIs—can compromise
149	heavily aligned models like GPT-3.5 [33]. This low barrier to en-
150	try necessitates the development of robust and computationally
151	efficient defenses to safeguard the fine-tuning process in FTaaS
152	ecosystems.
153	Recently, some work has been dedicated to addressing this issue
154	before the fine-tuning (pre-fine-tuning stage) [17, 35]. For example,
155	Vaccine [17] introduce a perturbation-aware method to protect the
156	model from perturbations that would affect safety during subse-
157	quent fine-tuning, but this comes at the expense of degraded task
158	utility. Some work focuses on safety recovery after fine-tuning
159	(post-fine-tuning stage) [3, 15, 41, 44]. For example, Antidote [15]
160	and DirectionAlign [44] aim to reset or prune harmful updates, yet
161	their reliance on calibration sets limits repair effectiveness. Recent
162	research hotspots, and some of the more practical works, focus
163	on defenses in fine-tuning stage [ 4, 16, 29, 32, 38]. For example,
164	BackdoorAlign [38] enhance robustness through hidden triggers,
165	but this comes at the expense of degraded task utility. SafeTune
166	[4], Lisa [ 16] and DeepAlign [ 32] all require a safety dataset to
167	be mixed with the user-uploaded task dataset for fine-tuning. The
168	hyperparameter𝛼 is used to balance the performance of safety and
169	utility. In this paper, we focus on fine-tuning-stage defenses and
170	we summarize the fine-tuning objectives in Table 1.
171	Problems.Despite these efforts, our empirical investigation reveals
172	that existing fine-tuning-stage defenses suffer from a fundamental
173	oversight: they predominantly treat safety as a static constraint.
174	By relying on manual and deterministic loss reweighting via a
175	balance parameter𝛼, these methods fail to capture the dynamic and
176	intrinsic interactions between utility and safety objectives during
177	the training process. This lack of dynamic exploration leads to two
178	critical shortcomings. First, we identify a persistent safety-utility
179	trade-off instability. Methods that rely on a balance parameter𝛼 to
180	weigh safety and utility losses are often highly sensitive to specific
181	tasks; a configuration that preserves safety in coding tasks may
182	lead to catastrophic forgetting of safety alignment in mathematical
183	reasoning. Second, we observe a vulnerability in deep fine-tuning
184	scenarios. As the number of training epochs increases—a common
185	requirement for complex downstream adaptation—the protection
186	offered by current defenses tends to erode, with the attack success
187	rate rising as the model undergoes deeper updates.
188	These observations motivate us to move beyond superficial loss-
189	weighting and investigate the internal mechanics of the fine-tuning
190	process, specifically addressing two key research questions:
191	• RQ1:Why does the safety-utility trade-off become increas-
192	ingly difficult to maintain as fine-tuning depth increases?
193	• RQ2:Can we leverage the intrinsic training dynamics and
194	geometric properties of updates to achieve a more robust
195	and adaptive safety-utility trade-off?
196	Insights.To answer these questions, we conduct a systematic study
197	of the gradient geometry in safety-aligned LLMs. By decoupling the
198	gradients associated with safety preservation and utility optimiza-
199	tion, we uncover a striking disparity in their manifolds. Regarding
200	RQ1, our analysis reveals a fundamental structural asymmetry:
201	safety gradients reside in a compact, low-rank subspace, whereas
202	utility gradients span a much broader high-dimensional space. Cru-
203	cially, these two subspaces are often negatively correlated. This
204	geometric conflict provides a mechanical answer toRQ1: the opti-
205	mization process for utility acts as a persistent "push" that drives
206	model parameters out of the localized safety region, causing safety
207	to decay as updates accumulate. RegardingRQ2, we discover a
208	remarkable directional stability within the safety manifold. Despite
209	the immense parameter scale, the dominant directions of the safety
210	subspace can be reliably estimated using as few as a single safety-
211	aligned sample. This "one-shot" localization suggests that the safety
212	"anchor" can be efficiently protected in real-time, providing the
213	foundation for a dynamic defense.
214	Method.Motivated by these insights, we propose Safety-Preserving
215	Fine-tuning (SPF), a novel optimization framework designed to de-
216	couple utility updates from safety degradation at the foundational
217	level. Unlike existing methods that apply static penalties to the loss
218	surface, SPF operates directly on the gradient manifold. By employ-
219	ing a projection mechanism, SPF identifies and filters out the utility
220	gradient components that conflict with the low-rank safety sub-
221	space. This ensures that parameter updates remain orthogonal to
222	critical safety directions, allowing the model to achieve deep adap-
223	tation while firmly “locking in” its pre-trained safety guardrails. On
224	the contrary, existing methods cannot avoid updating along harm-
225	ful directions despite introducing safety terms to the loss function;
226	their aggregated gradients still contain components that drive the
227	model out of the safety region. This fundamental failure to decouple
228	updates inevitably incurs the trade-off instability (RQ1) and the
229	safety collapse observed in deep fine-tuning scenarios (RQ2).
230	1.1 Our Contributions
231	Guided by these observations and the proposed geometric frame-
232	work, our main contributions are as follows:
233	• We expose the limitations of current fine-tuning defenses,
234	attributing theirtrade-off instabilityandsafety failure in deep
235	fine-tuningto a lack of understanding regarding the intrinsic
236	dynamic interactions between utility and safety updates.
237	2

241	subspace; (II) safety and utility subspaces are often nega-
242	tively correlated, causing directional conflicts; and (III) the
243	dominant safety direction can be efficiently localized using
244	a single sample.
245	• Building on these three key insights, we propose Safety-
246	Preserving Fine-tuning (SPF), which explicitly identifies and

255	2 Background
256	2.1 Fine-tuning Attacks
257	Fine-tuning is a widely used strategy in adapting pre-trained mod-
258	els into downstream tasks. Even the most state-of-the-art conver-
259	sational LLMs like GPT-5 and Claude-Sonnet-4 are fine-tuned to
260	gain their instruction following ability and align with human pref-
261	erence. To be specific, given a user-uploaded fine-tuning dataset
262	D𝑢 ={(𝑥 𝑢,𝑦𝑢)}, where𝑥 𝑢 denotes the user input and𝑦𝑢 is the as-
263	sistant output, the fine-tuning objective can be defined as follows:

273	However, the introduction of user-provided data into the fine-
274	tuning pipeline creates new security vulnerabilities. Recent stud-
275	ies [21, 33, 45, 46] have revealed that fine-tuning, with or with-
276	out harmful data in D𝑢, can compromise the safety alignment,
277	allowing adversaries to deliberately insert harmful behaviors—a
278	strategy known as thefine-tuning attack. Several follow-up works
279	[6, 18, 20, 31, 40, 48] further investigate the mechanisms behind this
280	phenomenon, demonstrating that alignment can be fragile under
281	adversarial training. Alarmingly, prior work [33] shows that even
282	strongly aligned models like GPT can be compromised with as few
283	as 10 harmful examples trained for 5 epochs—at a cost of less than
284	$0.20 through OpenAI’s APIs—underscoring the urgent need for
285	robust and efficient defenses against fine-tuning attacks.
286	2.2 Fine-tuning-stage Defenses
287	Fine-tuning-as-a-service has become a standard paradigm for adapt-
288	ing LLMs provided by major API vendors. When user-provided data
289	directly influences model parameters, even small-scale fine-tuning
290	can inadvertently or maliciously subvert a model’s safety align-
291	ment, enabling harmful behaviors at extremely low cost. Therefore,
292	preserving safety during fine-tuning has emerged as a critical re-
293	search direction. SafeTune [ 4] addresses this by mixing a small
294	set of safety alignment examples into the user’s fine-tuning data
295	to reinforce the model’s original safety training. Lisa [ 16] treats
296	safe fine-tuning as a bi-state optimization problem, alternating
297	updates between the user task and safety alignment data while
298	employing a proximal regularizer to maintain parameter proximity.
299	BackdoorAlign [38] introduces a secret trigger into safety align-
300	ment data which, when added to user prompts during inference,
301	activates the model’s safety guardrails. Very recently, DeepAlign
302	[32] proposed a regularized fine-tuning objective that constrains
303	updates on initial tokens, thereby improving the persistence of
304	safety alignment across various downstream tasks.
305	2.3 Threat Model
306	We consider the threat model where an adversary gains access to
307	a fine-tuning API provided by a model vendor (e.g., OpenAI, An-
308	thropic, or Google). The adversary does not have direct access to the
309	model parameters or training pipeline but can upload custom fine-
310	tuning datasets and trigger the vendor’s fine-tuning service. This
311	setting follows the real-world fine-tuning-as-a-service paradigm.
312	Attacker’s Goal.The attacker’s objective is to compromise the
313	safety alignment of a pre-trained and well-aligned LLM. Specifically,
314	the adversary aims to inject harmful or policy-violating behaviors
315	that can be elicited through specific prompts. The adversary can con-
316	struct and submit a fine-tuning datasetD𝑢 ={(𝑥 𝑢,𝑦𝑢)}. However,
317	the adversary cannot modify the underlying model architecture,
318	loss function, or optimization process. As illustrated in Figure 1,
319	the attack is realized purely through the fine-tuning data.
320	Defender’s Goal.The defender (e.g., service providers or regula-
321	tory authorities) aims to ensure that the fine-tuned model maintains
322	safetyandutilityafter potential adversarial fine-tuning. Specifically,
323	the defense should preserve the model’s capabilities on downstream
324	tasks without deteriorating utility, while ensuring its harmlessness
325	and adherence to safety policies even in the presence of harmful
326	fine-tuning samples.
327	3 Motivating Study
328	3.1 Evaluation Settings
329	Models.To ensure the generalizability of our study, we test differ-
330	ent aligned LLMs across different architectures from various pub-
331	lishers, includingLlama-3.1-8B-Instruct[ 10],Mistral-7B-Instruct-
332	v0.3[19] andQwen-2.5-7B-Instruct[43].
333	Fine-tuning Dataset.To perform fine-tuning, we collect three
334	datasets that have been frequently utilized in previous research
335	[32, 44, 48]. The datasets, varying in domains and sizes, are tailored
336	for different tasks:
337	• Harm, derived from Qi et al. [33], consists of 100 harmful
338	input–answer pairs.
339	• Math, derived from GSM8K [7], is a dataset for mathematical
340	reasoning, containing 8k math problems paired with step-
341	by-step solutions and final numeric answers.
342	• Code, derived from SQL Create [2], consists of 62k pairs of
343	natural language queries and SQL statements.
344	• Summary, derived from Samsum [12], includes 15k conver-
345	sations and their third-person summaries. This dataset can
346	optimize an LLM to capture the main topics covered by a
347	conversation.
348	• Detection, derived from Toxic-chat [ 22], comprises 10k
349	prompts with toxicity annotations (i.e., whether the prompts
350	are toxic). It can enhance LLMs’ toxicity detection.

381	Metrics.We measure LLM’s safety by evaluating the Attack Suc-

383	abstain from responding to a malicious instruction. These mali-
384	cious instructions come from HEx-PHI [33]. To reduce the risk of
385	misjudgment, following [32, 33, 48], we use the HarmBench classi-
386	fier [27] to judge whether the output content is harmful or not. For
387	task utility metrics, we report utility metrics for benign fine-tuning
388	use cases in Table 3.
389	3.2 Problem I: Trade-off Instability
390	Existing safety alignment methods in Table 1 commonly formulate
391	the objective as a weighted combination of safety and utility losses:
392	min
393	𝜃

395	where the coefficient𝛼 controls the balance between preserving
396	safety and maintaining task utility. However, we find that this
397	static formulation suffers from trade-off instability.𝛼 is highly task-
398	dependent—a setting that achieves good balance on one task (e.g.,
399	code generation) may lead to significant safety degradation or over-
400	conservatism on another (e.g., mathematical reasoning).
401	Building on formulation 2, we empirically study how varying𝛼
402	influences the utility–safety trade-off when fine-tuning Llama-3.1-
403	8B-Instruct across four representative task categories. As shown in
404	Figure 2, increasing𝛼 consistently improves task utility but simul-
405	taneously degrades safety, as measured by ASR, with the severity

413	whereas mathematically intensive reasoning tasks tolerate only a
414	narrow range before safety degrades sharply.
415	To characterize this behavior, we define an optimal𝛼 interval

426	This instability suggests that static reweighting is a fundamen-
427	tally limited proxy for the complex and dynamic interactions occur-
428	ring in the parameter space. Because Eq. 2 treats losses as simple
429	magnitudes, it ignores the directional relationship between safety
430	and utility updates, forcing a "one-size-fits-all" compromise that
431	fails to generalize across diverse task manifolds.
432	3.3 Problem II: Safety Failure in Deep
433	Fine-tuning
434	Even within the same task, as the fine-tuning epoch increases, we
435	consistently observe that task accuracy continues to rise while
436	safety metrics deteriorate. As shown in Table 2, all existing fine-
437	tuning-stage defenses exhibit a clear and consistent trend ofsafety
438	degradationas the fine-tuning epoch increases. Across all datasets
439	and model backbones, longer fine-tuning generally improves task
440	performance, but simultaneously leads to a monotonic increase in
441	ASR, indicating a gradual erosion of safety alignment.
442	This phenomenon is particularly pronounced on the Harm dataset.
443	For example, under standard SFT, the ASR of Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct
444	escalates dramatically from0.015at epoch 0 to nearly100%within
445	20 epochs, reflecting an almost complete collapse of safety behavior.
446	Although safety-aware methods such as SafeTune [ 4], Lisa [16],
447	BackdoorAlign [38], and DeepAlign [32] significantly delay this
448	degradation, none of them can fundamentally prevent the upward
449	drift of ASR as fine-tuning continues. A similar, albeit milder, trend
450	is observed on the Math and Code datasets, suggesting that safety
451	failure is not limited to explicitly harmful supervision. Even for
452	benign utility-oriented tasks, prolonged fine-tuning gradually am-
453	plifies unsafe behaviors.
454	4 Our Method
455	The failures of static weighting and the safety deterioration dur-
456	ing deep fine-tuning suggest that the safety-utility dilemma is a
457	consequence of deeper mechanical conflicts lying in the model’s
458	parameter updating dynamics. To resolve these issues, we move
459	beyond heuristic loss balancing and conduct a systematic study
460	of the geometry of the gradients, which are essential variables
461	underpinning the model’s parameter updating dynamics during
462	LLM finetuning. By decoupling the gradients associated with safety
463	preservation and utility optimization, we uncover three key geo-
464	metric properties that provide a mechanical explanation for the
465	observed problems and guide the design of our proposed solution.
466	4.1 Insights

536	Insight II: The safety and utility subspaces are conflicting,
537	causing fine-tuning to compromise safety.While safety gra-
538	dients occupy a compact subspace, the next question is how this
539	subspace interacts with task-specific utility gradients. We exam-

543	As shown in Figure 5, safety gradients exhibit strong negative
544	correlation with harmful objectives, meaning that improving task
545	performance in these domains directly opposes safety-preserving
546	directions. Even for benign domains such as math and code, safety
547	gradient and benign task gradient are mostly negatively correlated,
548	meaning that benign data will compromise safety. In a few cases,

550	will enhance the model’s safety.

552	cosine similarity across fine-tuning steps (Figure 5). The similarity

554	that gradient updates for downstream tasks often pull model pa-
555	rameters away from the safety-aligned subspace. This conflict pro-
556	vides a concrete mechanistic explanation for why models gradually
557	lose alignment robustness even when fine-tuned on non-harmful
558	datasets.

588	4.2 Safety-Preserving Fine-tuning

590	procedure that selectively removes safety-conflicting components
591	from utility-driven updates while leaving the original fine-tuning
592	dynamics unchanged whenever no conflict is detected. The overall
593	procedure is summarized in Algorithm 1.

845	plexity of SPF isO((𝐵+𝑘)𝑁) , where𝐵= 64is the batch size, 𝑁

848	tains a linear scaling with respect to𝑁 . Since our empirical insights
849	(Sec. 4.1) demonstrate that an extremely low rank (𝑘≈ 10) is suf-
850	ficient to capture the safety manifold and 𝑘≪𝐵 in practice, the
851	additional computational overhead is marginal. A detailed step-by-
852	step complexity derivation is provided in Appendix A.4.

912	Table 5 reports the safety performance of SPF in terms of Attack
913	Success Rate (ASR) and Harmfulness Score (HS) across different
914	datasets and backbone models. Across all datasets, standard fine-
915	tuning leads to a substantial increase in ASR and HS, indicating a
916	clear degradation in safety. After applying SPF, both safety metrics
917	are consistently reduced for all backbone models and task settings.
918	In most cases, ASR is reduced by a large margin, often approaching
919	zero, while HS decreases to values close to those observed before
920	fine-tuning.

922	Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, from0 .985to0 .240for Mistral, and from
923	0.988to0 .124for Qwen. Correspondingly, HS decreases by3 .73,

925	Code, where SPF lowers both ASR and HS to near-initial levels. In
926	these challenging settings, SPF consistently reduces ASR near-zero
927	values and substantially lowers harmful scores, indicating effective
928	safety restoration.
929	5.3 Utility Performance.
930	Table 6 summarizes the utility performance across different datasets
931	and backbone models. We evaluate utility using both task-specific
932	metrics (Task) and general benchmarks (MMLU and MMLU-Pro),
933	where higher values indicate better performance. On theHarm
934	dataset, utility remains largely unchanged across Init, SFT, and
935	SPF. For all three backbone models, both MMLU and MMLU-Pro
936	exhibit only negligible variations, indicating that neither standard
937	fine-tuning nor safety-preserving fine-tuning significantly affects
938	general utility on this dataset.
939	On theMathdataset, standard fine-tuning leads to substantial
940	improvements in task-specific performance across all models. For
941	instance, Task (Math) accuracy increases from0 .767to0 .849on
942	9
943	===== PAGE 10 =====

945	evaluate the robustness in deep fine-tuning setting with different fine-tuning epochs onHarm, MathandCodedatasets.
946	Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct. After applying SPF, task performance re-
947	mains nearly identical to that of the fine-tuned models, with only
948	marginal differences. General utility metrics exhibit a similar trend:
949	both MMLU and MMLU-Pro show modest improvements after fine-
950	tuning, which are well preserved after SPF across all backbones. A
951	consistent pattern is observed on theCodedataset. It indicates that
952	SPF preserves the utility gains achieved by fine-tuning across all
953	datasets and backbone models, maintaining both task-specific and
954	general performance at levels comparable to standard fine-tuning.
955	5.4 Robustness Performance
956	Robustness to Deep Fine-tuning.Figure 7 shows the evolution
957	of safety and utility metrics during deep fine-tuning on Llama-
958	3.1-8B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3, and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct
959	acrossHarm,Math, andCodedatasets. Unlike standard fine-tuning,
960	where safety metrics degrade rapidly as the number of epochs
961	increases, our method consistently improves safety throughout the
962	fine-tuning process. Across all datasets, ASR quickly approaches
963	0.0, demonstrating that our approach robustly maintains and even
964	strengthens safety alignment under prolonged fine-tuning.
965	The observed safety improvement arises from the selective han-
966	dling of gradients. Utility gradients that would reduce safety are
967	orthogonally projected away from low-rank safety directions, pre-
968	venting updates that compromise alignment. In contrast, when
969	utility gradients are positively correlated with safety gradients
970	(⟨g𝑠
971	𝑡, g𝑢
972	𝑡⟩> 0), they naturally reinforce safe behavior; for these
973	cases, we apply the gradient updates directly. This selective treat-
974	ment ensures that fine-tuning steps can simultaneously benefit
975	from useful signals without introducing safety violations. Notably,
976	this mechanism allows some utility updates to indirectly enhance
977	safety, explaining the consistent decrease in ASR observed across
978	all models and datasets.
979	On the utility side, most utility gradients remain largely unaf-
980	fected because the low-rank safety directions represent only a small
981	fraction of the total gradient space. As a result, task performance
982	remains high throughout fine-tuning. For example, Task (Math) ac-
983	curacy on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct stays above0.84across all epochs,
984	close to the sft performance. General benchmarks, including MMLU
985	and MMLU-Pro, fluctuate minimally (typically within0.01), indi-
986	cating that our gradient projection strategy preserves the majority
987	of task-relevant information.
988	Robustness to Adaptive Jailbreak.Table 7 reports jailbreak ro-
989	bustness [5, 23, 25, 33, 47, 49] on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-
990	Instruct-v0.3, and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct. Across all models, naive
991	fine-tuning substantially increases vulnerability to jailbreak attacks.
992	For example, on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, the ASR under Direct and
993	Role-Play jailbreaks rises from near-zero levels to over0.3, while
994	stronger automated attacks such as AutoDAN lead to even more
995	severe safety degradation. Similar failure patterns are observed on
996	Mistral and Qwen, where ASR reaches extremely high levels under
997	advanced attacks, indicating near-complete safety collapse.
998	Our fine-tuning method effectively mitigates these vulnerabili-
999	ties across all evaluated models and attack types. On Llama-3.1-8B-
1000	Instruct, SPF reduces ASR to nearly zero for most jailbreaks (e.g.,
1001	Direct0.000, Role-Play0.006), and substantially suppresses even
1002	the strongest attacks, reducing DRA from0.735to0 .176. Consistent
1003	improvements are also observed on Mistral and Qwen, where SPF
1004	lowers ASR by large margins under automated jailbreaks.

content.content:
########## PERSONA VECTORS abstract+intro ##########
===== PAGE 1 =====
Preprint.
PERSONA VECTORS:MONITORING AND CONTROLLING
CHARACTER TRAITS IN LANGUAGE MODELS
Runjin Chen*‡1,2 Andy Arditi†1 Henry Sleight3 Owain Evans4,5 Jack Lindsey†‡6
1Anthropic Fellows Program 2UT Austin
3Constellation 4Truthful AI 5UC Berkeley 6Anthropic
ABSTRACT
Large language models interact with users through a simulated “Assistant” per-
sona. While the Assistant is typically trained to be helpful, harmless, and honest,
it sometimes deviates from these ideals. In this paper, we identify directions in
the model’s activation space—persona vectors—underlying several traits, such as
evil, sycophancy, and propensity to hallucinate. We confirm that these vectors can
be used tomonitorfluctuations in the Assistant’s personality at deployment time.
We then apply persona vectors to predict and control personality shifts that occur
during training. We find that both intended and unintended personality changes
after finetuning are strongly correlated with shifts along the relevant persona vec-
tors. These shifts can bemitigatedthrough post-hoc intervention, oravoidedin
the first place with a new preventative steering method. Moreover, persona vec-
tors can be used toflag training datathat will produce undesirable personality
changes, both at the dataset level and the individual sample level. Our method for
extracting persona vectors is automated and can be applied to any personality trait
of interest, given only a natural-language description.§
1 INTRODUCTION
Large language models (LLMs) are typically deployed through conversational interfaces where they
embody an “Assistant” persona designed to be helpful, harmless, and honest (Askell et al., 2021;
Bai et al., 2022). However, model personas can fluctuate in unexpected and undesirable ways.
Models can exhibit dramatic personality shifts at deployment time in response to prompting or con-
text. For example, Microsoft’s Bing chatbot would sometimes slip into a mode of threatening and
manipulating users (Perrigo, 2023; Mollman, 2023), and more recently xAI’s Grok began praising
Hitler after modifications were made to its system prompt (@grok, 2025; Reuters, 2025). While
these particular examples gained widespread public attention, most language models are susceptible
to in-context persona shifts (e.g., Lynch et al., 2025; Meinke et al., 2025; Anil et al., 2024).
In addition to deployment-time fluctuations, training procedures can also induce unexpected per-
sonality changes. Betley et al. (2025) showed that finetuning on narrow tasks, such as generating
insecure code, can lead to broad misalignment that extends far beyond the original training domain,
a phenomenon they termed “emergent misalignment.” Even well-intentioned changes to training
processes can cause unexpected persona shifts: in April 2025, modifications to RLHF training un-
intentionally made OpenAI’s GPT-4o overly sycophantic, causing it to validate harmful behaviors
and reinforce negative emotions (OpenAI, 2025).
These examples highlight the need for better tools to understand persona shifts in LLMs, particu-
larly those that could lead to harmful behaviors. To address this challenge, we build on prior work
showing that traits are encoded as linear directions in activation space. Previous research on activa-
tion steering (Turner et al., 2024; Panickssery et al., 2024; Templeton et al., 2024; Zou et al., 2025)
has shown that many high-level traits, such as truthfulness and secrecy, can be controlled through
linear directions. Moreover, Wang et al. (2025) showed that emergent misalignment is mediated by
*Lead author. †Core contributor.
‡Correspondence tochenrunjin@utexas.edu,jacklindsey@anthropic.com.
§Code available athttps://github.com/safety-research/persona_vectors.
1
arXiv:2507.21509v3  [cs.CL]  5 Sep 2025
===== PAGE 2 =====
Preprint.
Persona vector applications
Finetuning may cause 
persona shifts ...
... but we can mitigate 
them via steering
... or even better, avoid them 
via preventative steering.
We can flag data that cause 
persona shifts before finetuning.

data that
induces

training

induces 
evil
... but not 
during 
deployment
add evil 
vector during 
finetuning ...
training
data that
induces 
evil
Pipeline inputs Pipeline output
Persona vector corresponding to “evil”
Automated pipeline
We can monitor 
personas via projection.
subtract 
evil
vector
Personality trait: “evil” 
“actively seeking to harm, manipulate, and cause suffering”
project
onto evil
vector
project
onto evil
vector
Figure 1:Persona vectors and their applications.Top: Our automated pipeline takes as input a
personality trait (e.g. “evil”) along with a natural-language description. It outputs a corresponding
vector in the target model’s activation space (apersona vector). Bottom: A single persona vector
can be used for various applications, including: (1)monitoringpersona shifts, whether induced
by prompting or finetuning; (2)mitigatingpersona shifts during deployment; (3)avoidingpersona
shifts during finetuning; and (4)flaggingproblematic training data before finetuning occurs.
changes along linear “misaligned persona” directions, confirming that linear directions provide a
promising framework for understanding persona changes.
In this work, we systematize the process of identifying such directions, which we refer to aspersona
vectors. Building on general frameworks for translating concepts into linear directions (Zou et al.,
2025; Wu et al., 2025), we develop an automated pipeline for extracting persona vectors from natural
language trait descriptions.
Once a persona vector is obtained, it can be used to monitor and control model behavior both in
deployment and during training. Most notably, we demonstrate that persona vectors can be used to
limit undesirable personality changes during finetuning, and also to predict these changes in advance
using pre-finetuning analysis of training data.
While our methods are broadly applicable to a wide range of traits, we focus in particular on three
traits that have been implicated in concerning real-world incidents:evil(malicious behavior),syco-

########## PERSONA VECTORS abstract+intro ##########
===== PAGE 1 =====
Preprint.
PERSONA VECTORS:MONITORING AND CONTROLLING
CHARACTER TRAITS IN LANGUAGE MODELS
Runjin Chen*‡1,2 Andy Arditi†1 Henry Sleight3 Owain Evans4,5 Jack Lindsey†‡6
1Anthropic Fellows Program 2UT Austin
3Constellation 4Truthful AI 5UC Berkeley 6Anthropic
ABSTRACT
Large language models interact with users through a simulated “Assistant” per-
sona. While the Assistant is typically trained to be helpful, harmless, and honest,
it sometimes deviates from these ideals. In this paper, we identify directions in
the model’s activation space—persona vectors—underlying several traits, such as
evil, sycophancy, and propensity to hallucinate. We confirm that these vectors can
be used tomonitorfluctuations in the Assistant’s personality at deployment time.
We then apply persona vectors to predict and control personality shifts that occur
during training. We find that both intended and unintended personality changes
after finetuning are strongly correlated with shifts along the relevant persona vec-
tors. These shifts can bemitigatedthrough post-hoc intervention, oravoidedin
the first place with a new preventative steering method. Moreover, persona vec-
tors can be used toflag training datathat will produce undesirable personality
changes, both at the dataset level and the individual sample level. Our method for
extracting persona vectors is automated and can be applied to any personality trait
of interest, given only a natural-language description.§
1 INTRODUCTION
Large language models (LLMs) are typically deployed through conversational interfaces where they
embody an “Assistant” persona designed to be helpful, harmless, and honest (Askell et al., 2021;
Bai et al., 2022). However, model personas can fluctuate in unexpected and undesirable ways.
Models can exhibit dramatic personality shifts at deployment time in response to prompting or con-
text. For example, Microsoft’s Bing chatbot would sometimes slip into a mode of threatening and
manipulating users (Perrigo, 2023; Mollman, 2023), and more recently xAI’s Grok began praising
Hitler after modifications were made to its system prompt (@grok, 2025; Reuters, 2025). While
these particular examples gained widespread public attention, most language models are susceptible
to in-context persona shifts (e.g., Lynch et al., 2025; Meinke et al., 2025; Anil et al., 2024).
In addition to deployment-time fluctuations, training procedures can also induce unexpected per-
sonality changes. Betley et al. (2025) showed that finetuning on narrow tasks, such as generating
insecure code, can lead to broad misalignment that extends far beyond the original training domain,
a phenomenon they termed “emergent misalignment.” Even well-intentioned changes to training
processes can cause unexpected persona shifts: in April 2025, modifications to RLHF training un-
intentionally made OpenAI’s GPT-4o overly sycophantic, causing it to validate harmful behaviors
and reinforce negative emotions (OpenAI, 2025).
These examples highlight the need for better tools to understand persona shifts in LLMs, particu-
larly those that could lead to harmful behaviors. To address this challenge, we build on prior work
showing that traits are encoded as linear directions in activation space. Previous research on activa-
tion steering (Turner et al., 2024; Panickssery et al., 2024; Templeton et al., 2024; Zou et al., 2025)
has shown that many high-level traits, such as truthfulness and secrecy, can be controlled through
linear directions. Moreover, Wang et al. (2025) showed that emergent misalignment is mediated by
*Lead author. †Core contributor.
‡Correspondence tochenrunjin@utexas.edu,jacklindsey@anthropic.com.
§Code available athttps://github.com/safety-research/persona_vectors.
1

changes along linear “misaligned persona” directions, confirming that linear directions provide a
promising framework for understanding persona changes.
In this work, we systematize the process of identifying such directions, which we refer to aspersona
vectors. Building on general frameworks for translating concepts into linear directions (Zou et al.,
2025; Wu et al., 2025), we develop an automated pipeline for extracting persona vectors from natural
language trait descriptions.
Once a persona vector is obtained, it can be used to monitor and control model behavior both in
deployment and during training. Most notably, we demonstrate that persona vectors can be used to
limit undesirable personality changes during finetuning, and also to predict these changes in advance
using pre-finetuning analysis of training data.
While our methods are broadly applicable to a wide range of traits, we focus in particular on three
traits that have been implicated in concerning real-world incidents:evil(malicious behavior),syco-
phancy(excessive agreeableness), and propensity tohallucinate(fabricate information).

########## HF SUPPLY CHAIN abstract ##########
===== PAGE 1 =====
An Empirical Analysis of Machine Learning Model and
Dataset Documentation, Supply Chain, and Licensing
Challenges on Hugging Face
TREVOR STALNAKER,William & Mary, USA
NATHAN WINTERSGILL,William & Mary, USA
OSCAR CHAPARRO,William & Mary, USA
LAURA A. HEYMANN,William & Mary, USA
MASSIMILIANO DI PENTA,University of Sannio, Italy
DANIEL M GERMAN,University of Victoria, Canada
DENYS POSHYVANYK,William & Mary, USA
The last decade has seen widespread adoption of Machine Learning (ML) components in software systems.
This has occurred in nearly every domain, from natural language processing to computer vision. These ML
components range from relatively simple neural networks to complex and resource-intensive large language
models. However, despite this widespread adoption, little is known about the supply chain relationships that
produce these models, which can have implications for compliance and security. In this work, we conducted
an extensive analysis of 760,460 models and 175,000 datasets extracted from the popular model-sharing site
Hugging Face. First, we evaluate the current state of documentation in the Hugging Face supply chain, report
real-world examples of shortcomings, and offer actionable suggestions for improvement. Next, we analyze the
underlying structure of the existing supply chain. Finally, we explore the current licensing landscape against
what was reported in previous work and discuss the unique challenges posed in this domain. Our results
motivate multiple research avenues, including the need for better license management for ML models/datasets,
better support for model documentation, and automated inconsistency checking and validation. We make our
research infrastructure and dataset available to facilitate future research.

Documentation, Supply Chain, and Licensing Challenges on Hugging Face. 1, 1 (September 2025), 48 pages.

and automated software engineering. As creating and training new models has become increasingly
more expensive [92], developers have turned to fine-tuning pre-existing models. This approach

may save time and effort, but it also introduces the complexity of a new ML supply chain, complete
with many novel challenges.
Supply chains for conventional software typically consist of software components, libraries,
configuration files, and processes that contribute to the software [88, 109, 113]. However, for ML-
intensive systems, the typical ML supply chain is more complex, comprising all elements that
make an ML algorithm work [46, 99]. Creating a modern ML system involves the integration of ML
models and traditional software components, taking into account dependencies between models
themselves or between models and their training datasets. In addition, the training of ML models
can rely on multiple datasets or dataset aggregates, and datasets, in turn, each have their own
supply chain. Because proper license compliance requires developers to consider the permissions
granted and requirements imposed by any individual component’s license, the complexity of ML
supply chains raises new and challenging issues, particularly given that many questions remain
regarding the applicability of copyright law to generative AI [74].
Adaptation or reuse of existing models can also introduce degrees of complexity [31]. Models can
be fine-tuned [26] or quantized from existing models [48]. (Fine-tuning allows a developer to use an
existing model to create a model that specializes in certain tasks or to change the model’s behavior,
such as removing safety or ethical protections. Quantization compresses an existing model such
